The success of any market, and the financial success\nof participating farmers, depends to a great extent on the skills\n Gain an overview of\nand abilities of the manager of the market. In the first volume\nthe contents of this\nof this series, we introduced you to the process of starting a\nvolume.\nfarmers market. In this volume, we outline and describe ways\n Understand the\nto build necessary management skills. The third volume is\norganization of\ndevoted to strategic growth and marketing.\nchapters and contents.\n\n See how the layout is\n\ndesigned to support\nyour skill development.\n\nWhat is a Manager?\n\nManagers can be described as catalysts. As the manager of a\nfarmers market, you are responsible for making things happen.\nYou organize, you plan, you promote. You are also responsible\nfor managing people. You communicate, you delegate, you resolve conflicts.\nBeing a manager means that you must know both the “what” and the “how.” The\n“what” refers to the vision and direction of your organization. What you do, what\nyou represent, what you plan to do in the future. The “how” is the collective myriad\nof details that helps you, and the people you work with, accomplish your goals and\nobjectives. How you organize the vendors, how you promote the market, how you\ncommunicate with the people around you. As a manager you have to be able to\nsee the forest and the trees.\nYour task is not an easy one. Count the number of really great managers you have\nhad in the past. Now think about how many bad managers you have had. Chances\nare the bad managers outnumber the great ones. Being a good manager requires\nnot only strong organizational and planning skills but also the ability to work well\nwith others and develop productive relationships with a wide variety of people.\nIf you are new at managing, take some time to look around you and understand\nhow things work. Who makes decisions? Who do people respect and why? What\nare the underlying tensions? How do things actually get done? You may be full of\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nenergy and ideas that make you want to jump in head first to begin doing your\njob, but it will pay off in the long run if you take the time to more fully assess the\nsituation. Talk to vendors, customers, and former managers, if possible, about the\nhistory of the market. This will signal to others who have been around longer than\nyou that you are aware of and respect what has come before you. Once you have a\nsolid grasp of what you have gotten yourself into, you can be more active in branding the market with your unique personality and leadership.\n\nHow to Use This Book\nIn addition to observation, there are other tools to help you be succeed in managing your market, and this book is designed to be one of those tools. This volume is\ndivided into three sections. The first introduces you to the roles and responsibilities\nof the manager. Chapter Two describes the manager’s roles as visionary and leader.\nChapter Three describes the sometimes overlapping roles of analyst, goal-setter,\ndecision-maker, communicator, and evaluator. No manager plays all roles at every\nmoment, but at different times you may be called upon to play each of the roles\ndescribed in Chapters Two and Three.\nThe second section of this book, covering Chapters Four through Eight, is dedicated to relationship-building. Chapters Four and Five discuss the relationship\nbetween the manager and the board of directors and how to strengthen the\nboard. Chapters Six and Seven describe ways to maintain positive relationships\nand, when necessary, manage conflict with vendors. And Chapter Eight outlines\nways to maintain and improve\ncustomer relations.\nThe third section of the book\ndiscusses specific skills involved\nin carrying out the roles and\nresponsibilities of the manager\nand in building and maintaining positive relationships with\nall those involved in the farmers\nmarket.\nIt is unlikely that any manager\nwill need every chapter of this\nbook. As you read through this\nintroductory chapter, consider\nwhich ones seem familiar and which may contain new information. Think about\nyour interactions with others—what areas cause you concern or problems? What\n\n\u0018\n\n\fIntroduction\n\n1\n\nManager Responsibility Matrix\nThe manager coordinates and\noversees ongoing functioning\nof the market.\n\nThe manager\nenvisions and plans for the future\nof the market.\nObjectives\n\nTo ensure that, as a venue for the sale of\nfarmers’ products, the market functions\nsmoothly and effectively.\n\nTo ensure the sustainability and viability\nof the market.\n\nResponsibilities\n\nAnalyzing: Analyze situations to identify\nproblem areas and opportunities for\nimprovement.\nDecision-making: Make timely, wellinformed decisions with fairness and the\nbest interests of the market in mind.\nGoal-setting: Set realistic, achievable,\nmeasurable goals for the market.\nOrganizing: Organize own time, work\nspace, market activities, and personnel.\nPlanning: Plan each season’s market\nactivities, including special events,\nadvertising, and meetings.\nBudgeting: Work with the board to\ndevelop and manage a reasonable budget\nbased on short-term goals and activities.\nImplementing: Implement actions\ncollectively chosen or individually as\nappropriate.\n\nProblem-solving: Identify, analyze, and\npropose solutions for problems.\nCommunicating: Communicate\neffectively with various constituencies\nof the market—vendors, customers,\nregulators, public officials, the media, the\ncommunity at large, and the board of\ndirectors.\nManaging conflict: Effectively manage\nconflict that arises among vendors,\nconsumers, the staff, volunteers, and\nboard members.\nDelegating: Delegate tasks that can be\naccomplished by others.\nLong-range planning: Periodically\nengage the board to develop and revise\nlong-range strategic plans to position or\nreposition the market to access emerging\nopportunities or confront threats to its\nsustainability.\n\nMonitoring: Monitor progress on\nprojects and activities and provide\nappropriate feedback.\nEvaluating: Evaluate the level of\nachievement and performance of changes\nin the system.\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nissues keep you awake at night? The answers to these questions can tell you where\nto begin. Alternately, you may want to start with something that seems familiar,\nhone your skills, and continue to improve on your strengths.\nEach chapter begins with a brief introduction and outlines the skills and abilities the chapter is designed to develop. By looking at the learning objectives for\nthe chapter, you can assess your need for the contents. You may return again and\nagain to certain chapters and skip others completely. While the skills, abilities, and\nrelationships discussed in this book are complex and interwoven, you may already\nhave many of the necessary qualities and skills.\nTwo of the most important sources of learning for managers are on-the-job experience and discussions with colleagues. We encourage you to read this book\nwith your actual experiences in mind and to find colleagues with whom and from\nwhom you can learn.\n\n\u0018\n\n\f2\n\nThe Manager as Visionary and Leader\n\nY\n\nThis chapter will\nenable you to:\n\n Understand what is\n\ninvolved in developing\nthe vision and direction\nfor your farmers\nmarket.\n\n Know the traits\n\ncommon to effective\nleaders.\n\n Know how vision and\nleadership contribute\nto your success as a\nmanager.\n\nou may know more about your farmers market than\nanybody else. You know who your farmers are, who your\ncustomers are, who supports your market, how and when the\nbills are paid, and where the stationery is kept. You know the\nsmall details and the big issues. But you are not working alone.\nTo be an effective leader, you need to have a vision. It is the\nresponsibility of the board of directors to work with you to\ncreate the vision for your market, but you play a huge role in\ndeveloping, communicating, and implementing the vision.\n\nDeveloping the Vision\nand Direction for Your Market\n\nYou need to be clear about the goals and objectives of your\nmarket so that you can articulate them to the people with\nwhom you work. Even though you may have a hundred things\non your to-do list each week, it is critical always to keep the\n“big picture” in mind.\nTake some time to reflect on these questions:\n\n\nHow does this market fit into the community?\n\n\n\nWhat products and services does it provide?\n\n\n\nWhat role do I see it playing in the neighborhood now and into the future?\n\n\n\nHow do the decisions I make affect the bigger picture?\n\nKnow how to talk about your market. Be prepared for questions from a variety of\ndifferent sources and have answers to them that will generate interest and excitement in what you are doing. Whether you are recruiting new vendors for the\nmarket or planning a promotional event with local talent, sharing your market’s\nstory will get people involved.\nFor instance, if a local reporter is interested in writing an article on your market,\nwhat would you say at the interview? “Well, we are a bunch of farmers out here\nselling our produce so come support us” does not convey the same message as\n“We are providing a valuable service to the community by bringing high quality,\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nfresh produce straight from the farm to a friendly social environment in the center\nof your community.” The way you represent the market to the public will have a big\nimpact on how customers perceive it.\nThe vision that you carry will be the source of energy and enthusiasm from which\neveryone involved in your market will draw. People will be looking to you for\ndirection; if you are not motivated to make the\nmarket the best that it can be, they likely will not\nbe either.\n\nThe Manager as Leader\nWhen you think back on the best managers you\nhave worked with in the past, what personality\ncharacteristics stand out for you? Is it their strong\nbooming voice or the infectious energy they brought to the job? Is it the way they\nalways seemed to make the right decision at the right time? Great managers are\nleaders. They are confident, optimistic, decisive, creative, and ethical. They also\ntreat people with respect and often maintain a sense of humor when the going\ngets tough. There are different leadership styles, and some great managers get\nthings done in the background, enabling others to shine and developing their\ntalents, while others naturally gravitate toward the spotlight. Use the following list\nof leadership traits to build on your natural strengths.\n\nConfident\nGreat managers know they can get the job done. No problem is insurmountable.\nIt may take some creative thinking and hard work to figure out, but it can be fixed,\naccomplished, or improved. To inspire the confidence of the people with whom\nyou work, you must display this trait yourself. People do not like to work for someone who is unsure or can be easily pushed around. The confidence you project as\nmanager will help others feel more confident about the work they are doing for\nthe market as well. Being confident does not mean making unrealistic promises\nabout your abilities. Rather, it means knowing exactly what you are capable of and\nworking to your highest capacity.\n\nOptimistic\nPeople are attracted to optimism. If you see the gold at the end of the rainbow, you\nmake it easier for others to see it too. No one wants to work for a negative or pessimistic manager. Focusing on the difficulties of running the market or the lousy\n\n\u0018\n\n\fThe Manager as Visionary and Leader\n\n2\n\nweather on market day will demotivate people around you. On the other hand,\nyou have great potential to infect others with positive energy that will make them\nfeel good about the market and their contributions to it.\n\nDecisive\nA good manager knows how to make decisions. Effective decision-making, which\nis discussed in more detail later, requires not just blurting out orders and directives\nfrom the top but gathering input from diverse perspectives and critically assessing\nsituations.\n\nCreative\nManagement techniques are not set in stone. A good manager often relies on\na little creative thinking to develop new strategies, solve problems, or find new\nsources of funding. Do not be afraid to look beyond the “normal” way of doing\nthings for some creative options that have not been tried before.\n\nRespectful\nPeople fundamentally want to be treated well. If you treat others with respect, they\nwill return the favor. A great deal of your job centers on your ability to get other\npeople to work with you. Respecting your coworkers and business associates will\nmake your task much easier.\n\nBalanced\nDo not forget your sense of humor. If you are not enjoying your work, you may be\nmaking others miserable too. Humor can take the edge off the most difficult situations, so do not be afraid to look at the humorous side of things.\n\nEthical\nOver time, you will develop a detailed understanding of the issues and problems\nthat your market faces. But have you taken time to think about your personal ethics in regard to these issues? Say, for instance, that you have begun to suspect that\none of your more popular vendors is selling produce that she did not grow herself.\nShe draws many customers to the market, and the other vendors do not appear\nto be aware of what she is doing. Where do you draw the line? Do you run the risk\nof losing customers and kick her out of the market? Do you say nothing and hope\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nthat no one else figures out what is going on? In this case, it is your personal and\nprofessional ethics that will decide the outcome. Perhaps no one will figure out\nthat you let this woman sell outside produce, but they may know instinctively that\nyou are applying different standards to different situations.\n\nTrustworthy\nBuilding trust takes time. If you have been active in your community for a long\ntime, you likely have already established a reputation with many of the people\naround you. You know the value of the long-term relationships you have developed with your neighbors, business associates, and friends. If you are new to the\narea, you have a lot of work to do to encourage trust from the people with whom\nyou work. If you have taken over a market from a previous manager, you may be\nseen as an outsider for awhile and you will have to put some extra effort into building trust with established vendors and customers.\nThe importance of being trustworthy cannot be overstated. The degree to which\nothers trust you will determine how likely they will be to work with you now and to\ncontinue to work with you in the future. Once you have secured the trust of those\naround you, it is important to maintain that trust. If a person trusts you and you\nbetray that trust, it will be quite difficult, perhaps impossible, to regain it. Furthermore, word of mouth from scorned business associates or former customers can\nbe devastating to a business.\n\nConclusion\nThis chapter has introduced you to some of the responsibilities of leaders and to\nsome of the characteristics shared by effective leaders. The next chapter discusses\nroles and responsibilities of the manager in carrying out the day-to-day work of\nthe market.\n\n\u0018\n\n\f3\n\nDay-to-Day Management\n\nC\n\nhapter One introduced you to the manager’s roles as visionary and leader\nand as day-to-day manager and administrator. This chapter provides further\nexplanation and detail about the manager’s functions in running the market. The\nfunctions of a manager, with some overlap, includes analyst, goal-setter, organizer,\ndecision-maker, communicator, and evaluator. Each of those roles occurs to some\nextent as you carry out others; for example, in your role as organizer you certainly need to communicate and when you set\nThis chapter will\ngoals you must analyze and evaluate. In this chapter we bring\nenable you to:\neach of those different roles to the foreground so that you can\n Understand the many\nsee how each contributes to your success as a manager.\nfunctions of the farmers\nmarket manager.\n\n Add to the strategies\n\nThe Manager as Analyst\n\nAs a farmers market manager, you must be able to think critically and, at times, ask difficult questions. Your vision of the\nideal farmers market must be grounded in practical and realistic\nterms. The simplest solution is not always the best so do not be\nafraid to ask questions and rethink your original plans. Some\nquestions to keep in mind whenever you are trying to accomplish something new\nor reflecting on recent events include:\n\nand techniques you use\nin each function.\n\n\n\nWhat can we do to make this situation better?\n\n\n\nWhat have we overlooked?\n\n\n\nDoes everyone have the resources they\nneed to accomplish the task?\n\n\n\nDo we have a backup plan in case something goes wrong?\n\nTaking time to critically analyze and revise your plans will support actions that better fit into your big picture rather than detract from it.\n\nThe Manager as Goal-Setter\nEvery organization has goals that it is trying to achieve and every manager is\nresponsible in some part for setting them. Well-defined goals are critical for provid-\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\ning direction and purpose to an organization. You cannot achieve something if you\ncannot see it, and goals are the lanterns lighting the way. They not only help you\nsee where you are going; they help you measure how far you have come. Goals\nhelp clarify individuals’ roles in an organization, identify tasks that need to be accomplished, and articulate the vision of the organization to others.\n\nSetting Goals\nWhether you are setting the goals for the entire organization or working with a diverse group of board and\ncommunity members, setting goals is an important process in determining the future direction of your market.\nFollowing are some things to consider when setting\ngoals.\n\nKeep Your Goals Simple\nSimple goals can be understood by everyone involved in the organization. They\nare clear and unambiguous. If the goal you have in mind takes more than one\nsentence to state, you probably are trying to describe multiple goals. If this is the\ncase, break your idea into single, one-sentence goals. Goals should be specific\nstatements about what you want to achieve. Following are examples of clear, welldefined goals:\n\n\nIncrease the number of customers at the market by 10 percent by next\nyear.\n\n\n\nFind two new sponsors for our market by Christmas.\n\nNotice that these goals have specific timelines and numbers that can be measured.\nIf your goals are too vague, you will have difficulty working toward them or knowing when they have been accomplished.\n\nLimit the Number of Goals\nHaving too many goals is as bad as not having any. An extensive list of goals will\nonly detract energy from the focus of making real progress in your organization.\nPick two or three goals that have the greatest relevance to your organization and\nwork on accomplishing those.\n\n10\n\n\fDay-to-Day Management\n\n3\n\nTake Small Steps to Reach Big Goals\nIt is important to break big goals into small steps. For example, you may want to\nreach the goal of increasing your number of customers by 10 percent in the next\nyear. That may seem like an overwhelming task without a plan for how to get there.\nBy breaking that goal down into smaller, more easily accomplished steps, however,\nyou can begin to understand your progress toward the goal and know when you\neventually will reach it.\n\nLink Goals to the Roles and Responsibilities of Individuals\nIt does little good to define goals that no one has the time or resources to achieve.\nMake sure that the goals you set for your organization fit with the responsibilities\nof the people working with you. Otherwise, they soon are forgotten or pushed\naside.\n\nRevisit and Revise\nPeriodically look back to your goals and assess your progress toward achieving\nthem. Goals should be flexible enough that you can modify them as situations and\nenvironments change. If the goals you first set do not address the reality of your\nmarket, do not be afraid to change them. They should help you, not hold you back.\n\nCommunicate Your Goals\nOnce you have identified your\ngoals, everyone involved in\nyour organization needs to be\nmade aware of them. Communicating your goals will help\nget others to commit to them.\nHave one-on-one meetings\nwith people to inform them\nof the significance of the goals. Get people excited about what you are trying to\naccomplish. If a goal requires a team effort, have a meeting with the entire team\nto plan and discuss the project. Well-defined, relevant goals help create a shared\nvision of the organization, so use them to your advantage.\nIf your goals require work to be done by others, assign specific projects with clearly\nunderstood timelines. Explaining the overall vision of the project will help get\npeople committed to it. By outlining specific tasks, you help others understand\n\n11\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\ntheir roles in making the vision happen in a way that is manageable. Make sure\nthat people are clear about what their responsibilities are before sending them off\nto do the job. Finally, in subsequent days or weeks, monitor their progress. Provide\npositive feedback as well as constructive criticism when you can to encourage continued performance on the project.\n\nThe Manager as Organizer\nMuch of your time is spent organizing—organizing yourself and organizing the\nwork of others.\n\nGetting Yourself Organized\nBefore you can effectively organize other people and coordinate something as\ncomplex as a farmers market, you must first get yourself organized. Knowing exactly what your roles and responsibilities are—and, more importantly, how you are\ngoing to accomplish them—will make your job much easier and make your goals\nmore attainable.\nManage your time. One of the most difficult and essential elements of being\norganized is managing your time. Chances are you spend more time than\nyou realize doing things that are not top priorities for your job: getting\ninterrupted by phone calls, shuffling through endless emails, coordinating\nparts of an event that other people could do just as easily. If, as nineteenth-century economist Vilfredo Pareto theorized, most people spend 20\npercent of their effort accomplishing 80 percent of their job, where does\nthe rest of their effort go? Chapter Thirteen offers some helpful tips for\nlearning to effectively manage your time.\nPrioritize your responsibilities. Another tool to help you get organized is to\nprioritize the tasks associated with your job. Which activities are the most\nimportant? Which are most urgent? Which ones can wait for a few days\nuntil things slow down? Try to identify what must be done and which tasks\nare just not that important. Remember, someone else’s crisis is not necessarily your crisis. Poor planning or lack of foresight on their part may not\nbe your fault. Do not let someone else’s problems distract you from what is\nmost important for you to accomplish to effectively do your job.\nKeep your desk organized. As simple as it may seem, having an organized\nwork space will make it easier for you to get your work done. When the\nphone rings and someone wants to know who is performing at your\n\n12\n\n\fDay-to-Day Management\n\n3\n\nmarket next week, do you have the schedule nearby? Can you find things\neasily when you need them? Take time to create a system for filing your\npaperwork, stacking your bills, and keeping track of appointments. Get\na day planner or desk calendar on which you can keep track of meetings\nand make notes so you know what you have coming up in the near future.\nMake sure this system works for you—it does no good if you cannot remember how it works or spend more time organizing your desk than doing\nyour work.\nSet clear boundaries. Because you are responsible for making the market\nfunction, you most likely have people coming to you all the time asking\nyou to solve problems and make decisions. It is easy to get distracted from\nwhat you are doing to answer a question or do something else that is “more\nurgent.” To effectively manage time, however, try to keep interruptions to\na minimum when you work on a complex project. Make sure that other\npeople know your availability, both while you are in the office and once\nyou leave. It is okay to turn your pager off when you are not at work and\nnot take phone calls at home past a certain hour in the evening. Set clear\nboundaries on your time and make sure people respect them. This will save\nyour energy for the times you are actually on the clock.\n\nOrganizing Others\nOne of your primary roles as manager is to organize the activities and responsibilities of the people with whom you work. Doing this effectively requires strong\norganizational and planning skills and the ability to see how individual people\nand available resources fit together to accomplish the goals of the organization. It\nrequires both that “big picture” thinking and critical analysis of little details to make\nthe market a success.\nDetermine Tasks. Before you can lead other people in the\nright\ndirection, you must know what steps need to be\ntaken to accomplish the market’s goals and objectives. You need to have a clear idea of what tasks need\nto be done and the deadlines for each job. Some will\nbe\nshort-term, one-time projects. Others will be long term and complex. Make\nlists if this helps you identify everything that must be done.\n\n13\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nThe following questions are useful to consider when trying to organize tasks:\n\n\nWhat are we going to do?\n\n\n\nWho will do what? Who does it involve?\n\n\n\nWhy must we do it? What will happen if we do\nnot do it?\n\n\n\nWhen does it start? When does it end?\n\n\n\nWhere will it take place?\n\n\n\nHow will it take place?\n\nAssess your resources. Now that you know what needs to be done, you have\nto figure out what resources are available to accomplish it. Assessing your\nresources includes identifying the skills and strengths offered by people\nwith whom you work and determining whether the financial and material\nresources are available. To do this, think about all the details involved in the\nproject, including:\n\n\nHow much support does the project require?\n\n\n\nWhat is the timeline?\n\n\n\nWhat types of equipment will be needed?\n\n\n\nWho is the best person or group of people to do this job?\n\n\n\nHow will the project be coordinated?\n\n\n\nHow will the project be monitored?\n\nYou may find that you need to modify or scale back a project based on the\nresources that are currently available. Taking time to assess all the details of\na project make it more likely that your goals actually will get accomplished\nrather than be forgotten or buried under a huge pile of other things to do.\nSet up a structure to accomplish all tasks. When you are sure that you have\nthe resources to get the job done, set priorities and assign someone to do\neach of the tasks you have identified. Whether you are the one who will do\nmost of these things or you have a large number of people working with\nyou, you need to be organized about how things get done and keep track\nof progress along the way. Chapter Fourteen discusses the process of delegating tasks in more detail.\n\n14\n\n\fDay-to-Day Management\n\n3\n\nThe Manager as Decision-Maker\nAs a manager, you make decisions every day. When people have questions regarding the market, they most likely will look to you for answers. Some decisions are\neasy to make and require little effort on your part. At times, however, you will be\nfaced with the task of making really difficult decisions. Some managers postpone\nmaking such decisions for fear of making the wrong decision. Or they wait for\nsituations to work themselves out instead of taking decisive action. But remember,\npart of the reason you were hired as manager was to make those tough decisions and to facilitate discussion about difficult situations. How you handle this\nresponsibility will affect how you are perceived by those with whom you work. This\ndoes not mean you should make instant decisions whenever someone asks you a\nquestion so that you seem decisive. Nor does it mean ordering people around and\nacting like a dictator. Strong managers include other people in the decision-making process and encourage new ideas and different solutions from others.\nTake some time to think about the following questions. They will help you reflect\non the way decisions are made in your organization.\n\n\nAre decisions being made by the people most affected by the decision?\n\n\n\nAre all the major points of view (consumers, vendors,\nthe community, the board) represented?\n\n\n\nHow is the decision-making process spelled\nout?\n\n\n\nAre decision-makers democratically chosen?\n\n\n\nIs the organization hierarchical or more lateral?\n\nAt times, the decision-making process can be difficult.\nIt is perhaps one of the biggest challenges managers\nface. Following are some considerations to keep in\nmind when faced with making difficult decisions.\n\nSeek Input\nWhile you may have a great deal of information about a particular problem or\ncircumstance, your perspective is not the only one. Talk to other people who may\nhave different views about the situation. Often there are aspects of a situation that\nyou will overlook or are not aware. Do not be afraid to tap this resource and ask for\nopinions from coworkers and others. The fact that their opinions may differ from\nyours does not mean they are wrong. Ultimately, your decision will more accurately\nreflect reality when it is based on more input and information rather than less.\n\n15\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nOn the other hand, do not procrastinate when it comes to the hard work\nof making the decision by continuing endlessly to gather information and talk to people about the situation. A good manager\nknows when enough information has been gathered.\nIn the end, people will be more likely to support your decision if\nthey were included in the decision-making process and understand the reasoning behind the decision.\n\nDefine the Problem\nOnce you have all the information you need, define the problem in clear terms. It\nmay be that what seems to be the problem is actually a symptom of a larger issue.\nFor example, if some of your vendors are consistently going home without having sold as much as they expected, you at first think that attendance is low and\nyou need to do more to promote the market. After doing some research, however,\nyou realize that the market has reached the maximum potential of its customer\nbase and is unlikely to gain more customers no matter how much you advertise. In\nthis case, the real problem may be that you have too many growers selling similar\nproducts. The problem underlying the symptom in this case, then, may be a lack of\ncommunication and coordination at planting time and when recruiting vendors.\n\nThink about Alternative Solutions\nOften there is more than one way to solve a problem or make a decision. Take time\nto think about various outcomes that will likely result from your decision. Is there\na better way to solve this problem? Will this “solution” create a different problem\ndown the road?\n\nMake a Decision\nOnce you have clarified the issue, make the decision in a timely manner. Do you\nremember being frustrated in the past when you asked someone in a position of\nauthority to decide on something and you were kept waiting or the decision was\ndelayed for a long time? People are looking to you for action. If you waffle on an\nissue, you may be seen as lacking confidence or as not caring about their concerns.\n\nExplain the Process\nIf you make a decision that conflicts with another group’s interests, which is bound\nto happen, make sure that you explain the process you went through to reach\n\n16\n\n\fDay-to-Day Management\n\n3\n\nthat decision. Just as you may not initially have had all the information required to\nmake a good decision, others may not initially understand the reasoning behind a\ndecision unless you make it clear.\n\nEvaluate the Results\nFinally, do not forget to monitor the outcomes of your decisions. Have you actually\nsolved the problem or just temporarily appeased people? Has the decision-making\nprocess itself made other people feel included or excluded? Does something need\nto be done differently at the next market, next month, or next year to keep the\nsituation from recurring?\n\nThe Manager as Communicator\nAs the maxim says, “Communication is a two\nway street.” To traverse that street, managers\nmust learn the fine art of listening. Listening\nmeans more than simply hearing what other\npeople say. It also entails understanding more\nsubtle signals such as the listener’s body language and eye contact. Whether you realize it\nor not, you listen with more than your ears. A\ngood listener also uses his or her eyes and gut instincts to understand what someone is trying to say. Keep your eyes open, watch peoples’ body language, and trust\nyour instincts so you can really hear and, more importantly, understand another.\n\nListen\nWhen someone comes to talk to you, stop what you are doing and give them your\nfull attention. Try to put the million other things you have to think about out of\nyour mind. If you continue to work while someone is talking, you send a signal that\nyou are not interested in what they have to say or that they are not worth your\ntime. Do not sit back and yawn as they sit down near you or look around the room\nwhile they are talking. Do not answer the phone in the middle of a conversation\nunless you must. If people think that you are not interested, they will be less likely\nto talk to you about something in the future. How many times have you heard a\nmanager say, “Why didn’t you tell me about this?”\n\n17\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nIf you really are too busy at the moment someone wants to talk, say so and establish a time within the next few days to have the conversation. Making a firm\nappointment shows that you are committed to listening. Do not miss that appointment!\nWhen you listen to someone, look the person in the eye and ask questions if you\nare not sure about something said. Once you think you understand the person’s\npoint, convey it back to make sure that you did not misinterpret what the person\nsaid.\n\nGive Feedback\nAs the manager of a farmers market, you may feel that the responsibility falls on\nyou to do everything required to make the market work. In reality, however, many\npeople are involved in making the market work. No matter how overwhelmed you\nmay feel at times, particularly on market day, it is important to take time to recognize the contributions of others and to give them feedback on the work they have\ndone.\nOne of the biggest mistakes managers make is failing to recognize the contributions of others. A well-timed “Thanks, that looks great!” or “Hey, Bob, did you see\nwhat a good job Linda here did on that sign?” will go a long way toward making\npeople feel valued and appreciated. If you forget to appreciate the people with\nwhom you work, they may just forget to help you next time.\nIf you have to give negative feedback, which you inevitably will, make it constructive. Do not say “Wow, Joe, that flyer is really ugly. Make another one.” Suggest how\nit could be improved. Encourage the positive aspects of the work and make sure\nyou recognize the effort. Also, resist the urge to take the task away from someone\nbecause you think you can do it better. You probably do not have time. A bit of\ncoaching can save you time and get the results you are looking for.\n\nThe Manager as Evaluator\nA key step in managing any kind of project or organization is evaluating progress\ntoward the accomplishment of your goals. Big projects are rarely accomplished\novernight and they do not happen in one easy step. It is your responsibility to\nmonitor the many activities and individuals that, in total, influence the direction of\nyour market.\nEvaluation of programs and people should occur frequently and consistently. This\ndoes not mean watching every move that every person makes or calling your volunteers every day to make sure they are doing what you would like. It does mean\n\n18\n\n\fDay-to-Day Management\n\n3\n\nchecking in with people periodically, asking questions, watching the environment\naround you, and generally being aware of what is happening.\nFollowing are some questions that you can ask yourself when evaluating your market as a whole or a specific project that is underway to help you monitor progress\ntoward your objectives.\nHow much progress has been made toward your goals? If your organization has clearly defined goals with specific timelines and tasks associated\nwith them, it should be relatively easy to assess the progress that has been\nmade toward meeting them. If you are unable to answer this question,\nperhaps you need to go back and do some work to better define your goals\nand initiate a plan to reach them.\nWhat major problems need to be addressed? Look around and critically\nassess the environment. What is not occurring the way you would like? Is\nmorale declining among the vendors? Have you noticed that fewer customers are showing up for the Tuesday market? Keep an eye on things and\ntake note of both subtle and obvious signs of distress or discontent. When you notice something, do not just ignore\nit and hope it will go away. Address problems early or\nthey will grow.\nHow are the actual costs comparing to the planned costs?\nResponsible financial management is a key component of running a business. Keep track of how much is being spent on specific projects before you\ngo over budget. Sighting potential financial problems early will help avoid\nbigger ones down the road.\nWhat would you do differently if you could? In addition to looking at the\nproblems or difficulties that your organization faces, you need to be able to\noffer solutions. If you see something that is not working right, think about\nhow you would improve it and work toward making that happen. If the\nchanges are out of range right now, perhaps because of limited human\nresources or finances, plan how you can eventually accomplish them.\nWhat have you learned so far? Finally, do not forget to take time to learn\nfrom your past accomplishments and your past mistakes. You do not need\nto reinvent the wheel every time you start a project. Being aware of the\nstrengths and weaknesses of your organization will help you to fine-tune\nthe market into a stronger operation.\n\n19\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\n\nConclusion\nThis chapter has introduced you to some of the roles and responsibilities of\nmanagers as they carry out the day-to-day work of the market. The next section\naddresses ways to use these and other skills to build relationships with key constituencies of the market—vendors, the board, and customers. Later we provide more\ndetailed descriptions and support for developing specific skills such as communication and time management.\n\n20\n\n\f4\n\nWorking with Your Board of Directors\n\nM\n\n Be familiar with\n\nost farmers markets have a board of directors. All nonprofit organizations are required to have a board and\neven farmers markets that are sponsored by other agencies or\norganizations often have boards. The relationship between the\nmarket manager and the board can take many forms but, to be\neffective, you as the manager should be aware of that relationship and seek to make it as positive and productive as possible.\n\n Be aware of the\n\nTypes of Farmers Market Boards\n\nThis chapter will\nenable you to:\ndifferent types of\nboards and their\nadvantages and\ndisadvantages.\nresponsibilities of the\nboard of directors.\n\n\n\n\nFarmers markets across the U.S. utilize several different models\nof board leadership. While there is no one right way to organize\nDefine your\nand recruit your board, it is important to construct a governrelationship to the\ning body that has the time and the energy to assist you in your\nboard.\nefforts at the market. Furthermore, many managers agree\nthat the most effective market board is one that reflects the\nChoose strategies to\ndiverse interests of the surrounding community as well as the\nstrengthen your board\ngeographic reach of the market. If you are involved in starting\nand your relationship\na market, you may have the opportunity to contribute to the\nwith your board.\nformation of the board; otherwise, this information will simply\nhelp you to recognize the characteristics, strengths, and weaknesses inherent in the model of board leadership your market has adopted. In\nsome cases, you may use this information to initiate a discussion of changes in the\nmarket structure and bylaws to strengthen the board’s ability to provide effective\nleadership for the market.\nFollowing are three different types of boards of directors and the strengths and\nconstraints they face.\n\nVendor-Only Boards\nMany market boards are comprised exclusively of vendors at the market. This type\nof board ensures a direct interest in the success of the market since each member\nis, to some degree, trying to make a living from their sales. In addition, the policies\nand procedures set by the board will accurately reflect the realities of the market if\n\n21\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nthe members are familiar with its day-to-day operations and the issues it faces. At\nleast a few board members will always be present at the market to assist, particularly in situations of conflict.\nVendor involvement on the board can, however, create a conflict of interest if one\nor more board members are personally involved in a dispute with other vendors. It may be hard at times to separate a board role from a vendor role. If board\nmembers see themselves as being in direct competition with other vendors or,\nconversely, if they are good friends with other vendors, it may be difficult to make\ndecisions fairly and without bias.\nVendor-only boards can also face difficulties if the vendor members are too busy\nduring market season to attend to larger issues. Furthermore, as one manager\nstated, “Farmers are not bureaucrats. I have a hard enough time getting them to\nfill out paperwork, let alone attend meetings and participate in strategic planning.”\nBoards composed entirely of vendors may rely more strongly on the manager to\nbuild community relations since most vendors will not live in town or possess nonprofit management skills. In some cases, the manager may end up directing the\nboard rather than the other way around. With no community representation, the\nboard may have a more difficult time understanding the issues and needs of the\nlocal community.\nFinally, if vendor board members are selling at more than one market, they may be\ntoo busy to focus on their board duties. In some cases, they may rarely attend the\nmarket, sending an employee instead, which gives them little connection to the\nmarket’s day-to-day issues.\n\nA Combination of Vendors and Community Members\nAnother common type of board is one that is made up of a mix of market vendors\nand representatives of the local community. Board members bring with them\nvarious strengths, such as legal or financial expertise, well-developed ties to the\nbusiness or political community, and strong marketing and publicity skills. A board\ncan maximize its effectiveness by paying careful attention to the assets of each\nmember and developing a complementary and well-rounded mix of individuals.\nFurthermore, this type of board not only offers representation and awareness\nof vendor issues but also reflects the diverse interests in the community. Board\nmembers who are local residents will be more attuned to issues that may affect the\nmarket and can potentially be more connected to the resources that will assure the\nmarket a strong position in the community.\n\n22\n\n\fWorking with Your Board of Directors\n\n4\n\nOn the other hand, board members who are less familiar with the realities of\nfarming and direct marketing will have to pay extra attention to the concerns of\nvendors and make sure that the plans and policies they implement reflect the\nneeds of the vendors. A common complaint is that board members who have prestige and power in the community but who are not well-versed in the organization\nthey guide are “out of touch.” As one board chairperson said, “The diversity of the\nboard is our greatest strength and our greatest weakness. The challenge is to live\nwith the conflict that diversity brings.”\n\nOrganizationally Driven Boards\nA third type of governing board comes from the nonprofit or local business organizations that often oversee farmers markets, such as downtown improvement\ndistricts and community development organizations. Markets sponsored by these\ntypes of organizations may have boards that have little connection to the people\ndirectly involved in the market. For example, a market may have a board composed\nentirely of business owners from the downtown area where the market is located,\nproviding no vendor representation whatsoever. As one manager said, “Our board\nmembers connect us to the downtown district really well, but they do not shop at\nour market.”\nThese boards may have the benefit of substantial expertise that members bring\nand well-developed ties to the local business community. In this case, it often falls\nto the market manager to ensure that vendor interests are well represented and\nthat communication between the board and vendors remains open. The potential\nexists for vendors to feel that the decision-making process is beyond their control\neven though they depend to some degree on the board for the success of the market. Vendor investment in the market may also be more difficult to achieve if the\ninfrastructure does not include them in the larger issues of the organization.\n\nResponsibilities of the Board of Directors\nThe purpose of a board of directors is to provide vision and direction to an organization. The board can help shape the market and its performance in many ways,\nincluding strategic planning, monitoring and evaluation, public relations, and\npromotions. Boards of directors generally consist of several officers (chairpersons,\ncochairs, secretaries, treasurers) whose positions are defined in the bylaws. The\nmanager may or may not be a voting member of the board.\n\n23\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nIn theory, the board works closely with the market manager to assist in the development and implementation of long-range plans as well as\nday-to-day activities. In reality, the level of board involvement\nin farmers markets depends on the way the board is structured, its relationship with the manager, and the time, assets,\nand strengths the individual members bring to the board.\nThe responsibilities of the board vary from market to market but may include:\n\n\nLong-range planning and goal-setting.\n\n\n\nSetting policies and fees for the year.\n\n\n\nDeveloping the market bylaws and mission statement.\n\n\n\nEstablishing an annual budget.\n\n\n\nProviding direction to the manager.\n\n\n\nSupporting the manager in situations of conflict and intervening when\nnecessary.\n\n\n\nMaking major staffing decisions.\n\n\n\nPromotions and marketing.\n\n\n\nFundraising.\n\n\n\nRecruiting new vendors.\n\nResponsibilities assumed by the board fall into two general categories: governance\nand support. Governance refers to the broad-based, policy-making responsibilities\nthat address the benefits and services the organization provides. Support refers\nto day-to-day responsibilities conducted through committees or on an individual\nbasis that further the goals of the organization. The Board Responsibility Matrix on\nthe next page outlines the different roles that board members traditionally fulfill.\n\nClarifying the Purpose,\nRole, and Responsibilities of the Board\nAll leadership models have strengths and weaknesses. The main criterion for judging the model is the extent to which your board meets the needs of your market\nand your community. The function and organization of your board will emerge in\nsome form from the initiating purpose of the market. For example, a market primarily initiated by several farmers as a way to market their produce is more likely\nto have a vendor-driven board than one initiated by a neighborhood organization\nthat is focused on revitalizing its community. {"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:3","agent_id":"abfdbe450","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:1","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"A STUDY ON PRICE VOLATILITY IN\nAGRICULTURE AND LIVESTOCK PRODUCTS\n\nSubmitted by\nTechScientia Consultancy LTD\n\nJune 2023\n\n1\n\n\fExecutive summary\nThe objective of this study was to carry out a deep analysis of the causes and\nconsequences of price volatility of agricultural and livestock products on both consumers\nand producers in Rwanda. The commodities considered in this study include priority\ncrops under the crop intensification program namely maize, rice, wheat, beans, Irish\npotatoes, cassava, and a few other key commodities like soybeans, meat, milk, and eggs.\nThe study adopted both qualitative and quantitative methods. The qualitative methods\nincluded desk review of relevant documents such as policies, strategies, laws,\npublications and reports related to production and prices. The key informants’ interviews\nwith Government officials, private sector and NGOs were conducted to obtain additional\ninformation and examine factors leading to price volatility of agricultural and livestock\nproducts. Individual interviews used structured and semi-structured questionnaires to\ncollect information related to price volatility. These interviews targeted producers,\ntraders, processors and individual consumers. Focus group discussions were also\ncarried out to confirm individual survey results.\nThe calculation of the price volatility was undertaken using the standard deviation of\npercentage change in prices. This methodology was used to measure the price volatility\nover time in other studies like Cariappa et al.,2022; Hu et al., 2022; Ribeiro et al., 2020 and\nde Nicola et al., 2016. The higher the standard deviation, the more volatile the prices\nchange and vice-versa.\nFindings indicated that the price volatility of agriculture and livestock commodities is a\ncommon phenomenon in Rwanda. Results from secondary data show that the prices\nfluctuate overtime for all commodities studied. However, some commodities such as rice,\nIrish potatoes, beans, and eggs are characterized by higher price volatility whereas the\nrest of the commodities studied showed relatively low-price volatility. The field survey\nresults from consumers, traders and producers affirmed the cases of low and high price\nvolatility nature of various agricultural and livestock commodities. Majority of\nrespondents reported that there is a general disequilibrium between demand and supply\nand the seasonality nature of agricultural and livestock commodities. This imbalance\nbetween demand and supply is likely the main cause of price volatility and affect\nproducers and consumers.\nThe study revealed that there is inadequate harmonization system for setting-up farmgate prices for agricultural and livestock commodities. The agricultural commodities\nconsidered under this study are produced nationwide in different agro-ecological zones.\nEach agro-ecological zone has its own specificities in terms of commodity production\nclimate, soil characteristics, labor and access to the market. All these factors affect the cost\nof production of agricultural and livestock commodities. The study found out that the\npricing mechanism in place does not take into consideration the specific factors of\n\n2\n\n\fproduction cost. Majority of producers interviewed revealed that the inadequate farm\ngate pricing mechanism prevails and make them vulnerable to the price volatility.\nIt was found out that the low productivity has a negative impact on price volatility. The\navailability and use of agricultural technologies play an important role to increase the\nproduction of agricultural and livestock commodities and improve its quality. The study\nnoted that the production of agricultural and livestock commodities is affected by\ninappropriate use of agricultural technologies leading to low productivity. For example,\nthe price of locally produced rice is volatile and often influenced by the imported Asian\nand Tanzanian rice due to inadequate technology.\nThe commodity value chain organization was found to have impacts on price volatility.\nThe organized platforms of stakeholders play a big role in linking different actors across\nthe value chain and helps to share equitably benefits among the actors. The study realized\nthat for some commodities such as Irish potatoes and maize experience high volatility as\na results of inefficient value chain organization. The interviewed producers and traders\naffirmed that the presence of middlemen in the value chain causes high price fluctuations\nbecause of speculations of prices and other associated anti-competitive practices like\nstock hoarding and market sharing. The volatility problem was found to be also\nprevailing in all the countries of the region the region as well.\nBased on the above findings, the study recommends the following: (i) to establishment\nthe community based food/grain reserves for strengthening direct market linkages, and\nreduction of exploitative middlemen (ii) to strengthen efforts to increase the local\nproductions and reduced the gap between demand and supply across all commodities;\n(iii) to create a more effective national governance on prices of agriculture and livestock\ncommodity; (iv) to promote regional integration and improve the business environment\nand (iv) to develop effective alert system.\n\n3\n\n\fList of abbreviations and acronyms\nAfCFTA: African Continental Free Trade Area\nBNR: National Bank of Rwanda\nCBG/FR: Community based food / grain reserve\nCOMESA: Common market of Eastern and Southern Africa\nCPI: Consumer Price Index\nEAC: East African Community\n\nEDPRS: Economic Development and Poverty Reduction Strategy\nFAO: Food and Agriculture Organization\nHIC: High-Income Country\nMINAGRI: Ministry of Agriculture and Animal Resources\nMINICOM: Ministry of Trade and Industry\nNAEB: National Agriculture Export Board\nNISR: National Institute of Statistics of Rwanda\nNST: National Strategy for Transformation\nPSF: Private Sector Federation\nPSTA: Strategic Plan for the Transformation of Agricultural\nRAB: Rwanda Agriculture and Animal Resources Development Board\nRICA: Rwanda Inspectorate, Competition and Consumer Protection Authority\nRRA: Rwanda Revenue Authorities\nRSB: Rwanda standards Board\nSADC: Southern African Development community\nSMEs: Small and Medium Enterprises\nUMIC: Upper-Middle Income Country\nWB: World Bank\nWTO: World Trade Organization\n\n4\n\n\fTable of Contents\nExecutive summary ................................................................................................................................... 2\nList Consumer price index in Rwanda from 2014 to 2022 ....................................................... 60\n\n9\n\n\fChapter 1. Introduction\n1.1.Background of the study\nRwanda aims to transform its economy and improve the lives of all Rwandans by\nimplementing bold ambitions towards self-reliance and competitiveness as embedded in\nits long term-strategic direction (Vision 2050). The country plans to become an uppermiddle income country (UMIC) by 2035 and a high-income country (HIC) by 2050.1\nIn Rwanda, agriculture is one of the major economic activities with 80.1% of the total\ncountry households participating in agricultural sector2, and around 72% of the working\npopulation employed in agriculture3. The country’s economy is dominated by agriculture\nwhich has been steadily growing at 7.9% over the past few years. Rwanda’s GDP at\nmarket prices stood at Frw 2,588 billion in the first quarter of 2022 where agriculture\ncontributed 23%, industry 22% while service accounts for a big share at 47%4. This was\npartly due to Government policies and programs that have consistently supported\nagriculture transformation and modernization of the sector. These policies include the\nNational Strategy for Transformation (NST1) and Strategic Plans for Agriculture\nTransformation (PSTAs) which increased productivity of agriculture and livestock. This\nis also aligned to the 3rd pillar of the vision 2050 “Agriculture for Wealth Creation” which\nalso seek to ensure inclusiveness and total transformation of the sector from substance\nfarming to commercialized value chains5.\nLike the rest of Africa, Rwanda’s economy is based on agriculture and the country is\ncommitted to implementing SDG Indicators 2.c.1, which covers concepts related to price\ndetermination and price volatility. Price fluctuations are a common feature of wellfunctioning agricultural product markets. However, when these fluctuations become\nfrequent and persistent, they negatively impact the food security systems and commodity\nvalue chains.\nIn the past few years, instability in food prices and partial or complete unavailability of\nessential food commodities has impacted millions of consumers, affecting their\npurchasing power, jeopardizing their nutritional status and food security. Most of the\npriority commodities (maize, rice, Irish potatoes, meat, eggs, etc.) that play a big role in\nRwandans’ diet have been characterized by price volatility. However, little is known\nabout the underlying causes, patterns, and impacts of the price volatility of agricultural\nand livestock commodities in Rwanda.\n\n1\n\nRwanda long-term development strategy (Vision 2050)\n\n2\n\n(NISR, 2020)\n\n3\n\nFood and Agriculture Organization of United Nations (FAO) report\nMinistry of Finance and Economic Planning (MINECOFIN), also available at https://www.minecofin.gov.rw/newsdetail/economy-grew-by-79-in-1st-quarter-with-key-contributions-from-services-and-industry lastly accessed on\n1st March 2023\n5\nPlanning and Policy Blueprint (Vision 2050), 3rd Pillar, p 10, published.\n4\n\n10\n\n\fIt is against the above background that a study on price volatility in the sector has been\nthought to deeply assess what could problematic and identify possible solution.\nThat would inform the Government and stakeholders in designing appropriate strategies\nand policy to address the matter and ensure that prices of agricultural and livestock\nproducts are stabilized while achieving the national development targets.\nAgricultural and livestock commodity price changes context in Rwanda\nIn 2022, Rwanda experienced a dramatic increase in prices of food and non-alcoholic\nbeverages. The prices increased to 45% higher in 2022 compared to last five year and 41%\nhigher compared to 2021.6There has been price volatility in agricultural and livestock\nproducts in the market and it has often been hard to predict the sustainability of prices of\nagricultural commodities. Prices of staple food dramatically increased, for instance, dry\nbeans and maize grains increased by 121% and maize grain by (102%) more than double.\nPrices of Irish potatoes and maize flour also sharply increased by 67% and 70%\nrespectively in October 2022 7.\nThese price increases are mostly associated with increased fuel and transportation prices,\nhigh prices of agricultural inputs, and poor weather conditions that translated to poor\nseason B performance.\nThe literature shows that different factors, both internal and external, contribute to the\nprice volatility in Rwanda, the common sited ones are climate shock factors, dominance\nof subsistence agriculture farming, low investment in the sector, inadequate commodity\nvalue-chains organization (e.g. existence of middlemen) and seasonality, steady pace\nincreased in fuel and transportation prices, high prices of agricultural inputs, and\ninadequate technologies 8.\nThe market of some agricultural commodities in Rwanda is affected by external market\nprices and regional and global market dynamics. It is common in Rwanda that some\nimported agricultural commodities are relatively cheaper compared to locally produced\ncommodities such as rice, wheat, soybean, etc. leading to price volatility. In addition,\ncross-border trade also often affects the price volatility due to differences in costs of\nproduction and clear mechanisms of checking the commodity volumes entering and/or\nexiting the countries.\nThe price changes of agricultural and livestock products often impact the consumption\nhabits and negatively affect agricultural production and productivity particularly for\nsmall-scale farmers who lack technical and financial capacities to cope with\nunpredictability of market price trends.\n\n6\n\nUnited Nations, Rwanda|update on food security drivers August-October 2022— issue no 63, published.\nIbid\n8\nIdem\n7\n\n11\n\n\f1.2.Rationale of the current study\nThe current study was prompted by the current situation of fluctuating agricultural and\nlivestock market prices which affects the livelihood of the cross section of the population.\nThere arose the need to conduct this study to assess the causes, the impacts and trends of\nthese prices volatility among others and give recommendations on how to deal with that.\nThe key output expected from the current study is the “final analytical work with\nrecommendations on price volatility of agricultural and livestock commodities.\n1.3.Objectives of the study\nThe objective of the study was to carry out a deep analysis of the causes and consequences\nof price volatility of agricultural and livestock commodities on both consumers and\nproducers in Rwanda. The study helped to investigate the current issue of price\ninstability in agricultural and livestock commodities.\nThe study provided sections in this study report detailing the following:\n•\n•\n•\n•\n•\n•\n\nTrends and status of agricultural and livestock price volatility in Rwanda and the\nimplication of the current policy framework\nDeterminants of price volatility in agricultural and livestock commodities\nAnalysis of gap in supply and demand in their effects on price fluctuation\nagriculture and livestock commodities\nThe analysis and results of a systematic assessment of the concept, approaches for\nformulating and implementing the study options;\nThe presentation and analysis of relevant regional and international best practices\nto be promoted;\nSound policy recommendations for coping with price volatility in agricultural and\nlivestock commodities.\n\n12\n\n\fChapter 2. Methodology of the study\n2.1.Approach of the study\nAccording to the nature of the study and intended objectives, the study employed a hybrid\napproach where both qualitative and quantitative methods were used. The choice of the study\ndesign (Mixed/Hybrid Method) is in line with the two main study parts where qualitative\napproach were used to get insights and in interpretation of different legal frameworks and\ninstitutional setup regarding the topic while quantitative approach was also used in quantifying\nnumbers, especially in measuring prices trends as well as the contribution of the agricultural and\nlivestock to the country GDP.\nThe integration of the two methods (Qualitative and Quantitative) and its preference over others\nis based on key five considerations, namely:\n\na) The study was multidisciplinary and covered opinion/legal part where\nqualitative was used and other domain (i.e) economic/statistical part where\nquantitative is more effective,\nb) The study involved collecting and analyzing of both quantitative (closed-ended)\nand qualitative (open-ended) data,\nc) A mixed method is key in understanding contradictions between quantitative\nresults and qualitative findings,\nd) It provides methodological flexibility, and\ne) The study uses complex procedures in collecting and analyzing data appropriate\nto each method; and procedures that may implement qualitative and quantitative\ncomponents either concurrently or sequentially.\nThe study was undertaken in various steps which include desk review for secondary\ndata collection, field data collection for primary data gathering, data cleaning and\nanalysis, and the results reporting as illustrated in figure 1.\n2.1.1. Study area\nThe study covered nineteen districts of the country as a research area. Districts\nconsidered during semi-structured interviews and key consultations were purposely\nselected, guided mainly by the dominance of proposed value chains where SMEs of\ninterest were involved. Our Team collected data among sampled SMEs operating in\nvarious agricultural and livestock products viz. staple crops (maize, rice, Irish potatoes\nand cassava, wheat), and livestock (dairy, poultry, and piggery for milk, meat, and eggs)\nacross the country.\nThe sample was purposively selected to include respondents from each of the following\nmajor categories along the value chain i.e., producers, traveling, traders, transporters,\nwholesalers, and retailers.\n\n13\n\n\fFigure 1. Study design\nTo obtain respondents SMEs, the purposive sampling technique was used since it was difficult to\nhave or reach the entire population of SMEs (e.g., Etikan et al., 2016). This technique has been\nused by other similar studies across the different parts of the world such as Pakistan and Sri\nLanka (e.g., Shafu et al., 2020; Robinson and Kengatharan, 2020). The selection of SMEs to be\ninterviewed depended on those existing and already known which informed the research team\non other SMEs operating in the respective value chains using both their personal and professional\nnetworks. Others considered were from the list of active SMEs already available in the database\nprovided by PSF-Rwanda Chamber of Agriculture and Livestock, RDB and the Department of\nStatistics in the Rwandan Ministry of Agriculture which collects commodity prices at the\nmarketplaces around the country on different traded commodities.\n\n2.1.2. Sampling procedures for primary data collection\nThe main objective of this sample survey was to gather data with respect to the changes\nthat have recently been occurring in selected agri-chain values, the salient causes and the\nconsequences of these unpredicted surges and fall in the market price. Given that for each\ncommodity the market players and structure is different. The target groups may differ\nslightly from commodity to commodity. However, they will include the farmers, and\nfarmer's cooperatives, the merchants at various levels (wholesaler, retailer), consumers,\netc.\n\n14\n\n\fan accurate, complete, and up-to-date sampling frame was developed in three stages: (1)\nRecruited the enumerators (2) the Data enumerators then helped to access administrative\ndata on SMEs registered, their sector of operation, and other information needed in this\nstudy , (3) the Data enumerators then purposively selected SMEs operating in staple\ncrops (Maize and Rice Irish potatoes and cassava ) and livestock (dairy, poultry, and\npiggery across the country. Sampling was done from all the major functions actors across\nthe value chain (e.g., primary production, trading (wholesaling and retailing), processing,\nmarketing and support services (e.g., distribution - transport, storage etc.) for each value\nchain.\nThe total sample size per value chain was calculated based on the population from the\nsampling frame and a random process was applied to select SMEs at value chain level.\nThe sample size is equally distributed across the value chains.\nFor the interviews with stakeholders. To get the number of respondents; the following\nformula will be used to determine the sample size (Krejcie and Morgan, 1970):\n\n𝑛=\n\n𝑥 2 ∗ 𝑁∗ 𝑃(1−𝑝)\n(𝑀𝐸 2 ∗ (𝑁−1) + (𝑥 2 ∗ 𝑃(1−𝑝)\n\nWhere:\nn: sample size,\n𝑥 2 : Chi-square for the confidence interval (C.I) of 95% at 1 degree of freedom\n(3.841),\nN: Population size (assuming that N>100,000 population),\np: Population proportion (assumed to be 0.50 as no additional information\nabout the population characteristics was provided and since this would\nprovide the maximum sample size),\nME: Desired Margin of Error (6.7%).\nUsing the formula above, the sample size was found to be 214 to be interviewed which\ninclude farmers, wholesalers, retailers and consumers. The distribution of respondents\nper district is indicated in table 2 while the respondent by commodity and value chain\nactors are shown in table 3. The selection of the exact SMEs retailers in the markets to be\ninterviewed about the retail prices depended on the list of active SMEs already available\nin the database provided by MINAGRI’s e-soko data and MINICOM (Appendix 1-3)\n2.2. Data Collection and Analysis\nThis study adopted both primary and secondary data collection approaches. The fieldbased data collection and analysis phase will be done by using a cascading method,\nwhereby the analysis is informed by collecting information initially from the “centre”\n15\n\n\fgovernment and donor level, then at regional district and community levels. This will\nmake it possible first to understand and frame the overall national vision and policy\nobjectives and then to capture the range of views and experiences from a diverse group\nof stakeholders, as well as to observe the way policy and programmes play out at\ndifferent levels.\nMajor steps that will be undertaken include desk review, field surveys of traders of\nvarious commodities, farmers and value chain players like processors, key informant\ninterviews, focus group discussions, data cleaning, processing, analysis and reporting.\nThe activities of each step are detailed in Figure 1 while the Evaluation Questions,\nInformation to be collected, and Approach are detailed in Table 1.\n2.2.1. Methods of data collection\nThe analytical work on Price Volatility in agriculture and animal commodities required\nfirst the collection of both primary and secondary data. Different data collection methods\nwere utilized to triangulate information and verify its reliability. The data collection\nmethods included collection of secondary data through the literature review, face to face\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}

Committee\nWorld Trade Organization\nGroupe de Recherche et d’Echange sur la régulation des\nmarchés agricoles\nLDC\nLeast Developed Countries\nPAA\nAgricultural Food Acquisition Program\nADMARC
