There is still room to regulate food markets\nmore actively, such as through buffer stock and import controls. Periods of heavy\nmarket intervention seem to correspond to greater stability (although improved\nweather conditions could be the influencing factor, as well). The governement should be\nencouraged to define and implement credible policies based on a transparent\nconsultative process that involves the key stakeholders.\n\n149\n\n\f2. Asian experiences\n2.1\n\nBiting off more than it can chew? Agricultural price stabilization policies in\nIndia\nFrédéric Landy, Professor of Geography, GECKO, Université de Paris Ouest Nanterre‐La\nDéfense, Associate researcher at the Centre d’Etudes de l’Inde et de l’Asie du Sud (CNRS\nEHESS)\n\nSummary\nWhat has been done?\nStabilization policies can be broken down into three periods. After Independence in\n1947, the government heavily intervened in agricultural prices. Before the Green\nRevolution, this involved forced levies on farmers and traders, an environment that\nshied away from private speculation, and subsidized food prices in large cities. Once the\nGreen Revolution got underway (1965), policies focused on incentives to guarantee\nprices and redistribute surpluses to the entire population. Since 1991, there has been\ngradual liberalization.\nHow has it been implemented?\nSince 1965, the Food Corporation of India, backed by state governments, has purchased\nwheat and rice at reserve prices (the same system exists for sugar), thereby amassing\nstocks that are distributed and sold at subsidized prices through a countrywide\ndistribution network. Purchases by firms are still controlled, as are import‐export\ntransactions to a certain extent. Input subsidies (fertilizer, water, electricity) play a\nmajor role but ultimately, Indian agriculture is more taxed than than it is subsidized.\nWhat were the effects?\nThe agricultural growth in areas influenced by the Green Revoluation has started to\nslow, the public distribution system is riddled with corruption, and food security stocks\nare poorly managed. The latter results in costly imports, less costly subsidized exports,\nand resale on the domestic market. But, India has acquired grain self‐sufficiency and\nfood sovereignty. Agricultural and food prices are smoothed compared to world prices,\nwhich (for the most part) allows the country to avoid “hunger riots”. “The coefficient of\nvariation for rice and wheat prices in India held steady around 4% to 7% between 1980\nand 2000, compared to 15% to 20% for world prices” (High Level Committee, 2000, §\n1.8).\n\n150\n\n\fTable 25: Trends in wheat prices (1979‐99): India and world\n\nWhat recommendations could be derived?\nProtectionism and government price policy have brought agricultural expansion and\nrelative consumer satisfaction. However, India lacks flexibility in its policy (long‐term\nmanagement of stocks devoid of political pressure, food vouchers in cities, measures to\navoid competition with distribution channels used by local producers).\n\n151\n\n\f2.2\n\nThe Indonesian experience with rice price stabilization\nF. Gérard, CIRAD\n\nSummary\nWhat has been done? Indonesia pursued rice price stabilization through two\ninstruments used simultaneously: (i) intervention in marketing through public\nstorehouses managed at a local level (ii) monopoly control over international trade.\nPrice stabilization protects both consumers and producers by maintaining rice prices\nwithin a predetermined band and by widely disseminating floor and ceiling prices. Stable\nand low rice prices were a major objective of the government following the period of\neconomic and political instability that came to an end in the mid‐sixties. The rice price\npolicy can be split into several periods, corresponding to changes in the country’s\ncontext. These changes in turn have influenced the policy’s implementing agency’s\nobjectives and constraints.\nHow has it been implemented? Rice price stabilization was implemented by a special\nagency created in 1968 (BULOG). BULOG managed a nationwide network of local\nagencies and district‐level warehouses (DOLOG), which enabled it to store substantial\nquantities of rice. DOLOG warehouses bought rice to bring prices in rural markets up to\nthe floor price. BULOG procured rice paddy from farmers’ cooperatives as well as from\nprivate traders. Farmers were encouraged to establish village cooperatives. Major\nefforts were made to build capacity of BULDOG’s management and staff from the\nbeginning. Extensive analytical studies were carried out on key factors such as the size of\nmargins between floor and ceiling prices, the size of buffer stocks, and the price of\nfertilizer relative to floor and world prices. A monopoly control over international trade\nwas established, which allowed the government to import when domestic production\nwas insufficient and to export when there was a surplus and stocks levels were already\nhigh.\nWhat were the effects? The supply response was dramatic: rice production increased by\n10.5 millions tons over the 1978‐1985 period. Fertilizer use increased by 500% between\n1970 and 1985, while yields increased from 2.5 T/ha in 1965 to 4.4 T/ha in 1990. A huge\nimprovement in food security followed.\nFigure 2 : World rice prices and domestic rice\nprices 1985 to 2002\n\nFigure 1 : Evolution of selected indicators for rice in Indonesia\n(1980‐2001)\n45\n\nmillion metric tons\n\n40\n35\n30\n25\n20\n15\n10\n5\n\nSource: reprint from Timmer 2004 p. 71\n\n20\n00\n\n19\n98\n\n19\n96\n\n19\n94\n\n19\n92\n\n19\n90\n\n19\n88\n\n19\n86\n\n19\n84\n\n19\n82\n\n19\n80\n\n0\n\nproduction\n\nimports\n\navailable\n\nconsumption\n\n152\nSource : Central Bureau of Statistics\n\n\fWhat recommendations could be derived? The Indonesian experience shows that\ngovernment intervention can successfully adapt to a changing context and contribute to\nquick economic growth, but that it is difficult to implement. It is important to note that\nthe rice price stabilization policy was only one component of the Indonesian approach to\nmodernizing its rice economy. Public investment, not only in infrastructure such as roads\nand irrigation facilities, but also in human capital, through extension services and\neducation, played an important role in the country’s success. One important\ncharacteristic of government interventions is that they focused on avoiding markets\nfailures and supporting private economic initiatives rather than substituting public\ninitiatives for private initiatives. The whole commodity chain was not in the hands of\nparastatal companies.\nThe agricultural policy was part of a broader policy ensuring (i) macro‐economic\nstability, (ii) making markets working more efficiently, (iii) ensuring political stability, and\n(iv) creating enabling environment for private investment.\nSome technical lessons on price stabilization can also be drawn from the Indonesian\nexperience:\n- The target price should be aligned with international prices;\n- The policy is far less costly when the country relies on imports than when it has\nto manage a surplus.\nKey factors including the size of the margins between floor and ceiling prices, the size of\nbuffer stocks, and the price of inputs relative to floor and world prices need to be\nconstantly updated.\n\n153\n\n\f2.3\n\nRice price stabilization policies in Thailand\nYi Chen (SupAgro Montpellier, UMR MOISA)\n\nSummary\nWhat has been done? 1) From 1950 to 1985, the government established export taxes\n(pro‐consumer policies); 2) From the mid‐1980s to 1997, it removed export taxes and\nimplemented modest measures to support producer prices; 3) From 2001 to 2009, it\ndeveloped buffer stock and support price schemes (pro‐producer policies).\nHow has it been implemented? From late 1950s to the mid‐1980s, Thailand used a\ncombination of four instruments: a rice premium, ad valorem export duty, rice reserve\nrequirement and quantitative restrictions on exports. From 2001 to 2009, the\ngovernment purchased rice at above market prices under the Paddy Pledging Program;\nthe pledged paddy was then sold through bids or “Government to Government\ncontracts”.\nWhat were the effects? From late 1950s to the mid‐1980s, export taxes on rice\nconstituted an important source of government revenue. Domestic prices were kept\nbelow the export prices and stabilized during the spikes (1967 and 1973). From 2001 to\n2009, production levels grew thanks to high intervention prices. Domestic prices started\nmoving upwards, levels of buffer stock rose, and the government spent a considerable\npart of the budget on the policy. There were also some undesirable effects on exports\n(lower competitiveness and availability of rice).\n\nFigure 3 : Trends in production, exportation and end‐of‐year\nstocks\n25000\n\n25000\n\n20000\n\n20000\n\n15000\n\n15000\n\nProducti on\n\n10000\n\n10000\n\nExporta ti on\nStock fi n d'annee\n\n5000\n5000\n\nSource: Bank Of Thailand, Website: http://www.bot.or.th/\n\n2000/2001\n2001/2002\n2002/2003\n2003/2004\n2004/2005\n2005/2006\n2006/2007\n2007/2008\n2008/2009\n2009/2010\n2010/2011\n\n0\n0\n1/2000\n7/2000\n1/2001\n7/2001\n1/2002\n7/2002\n1/2003\n7/2003\n1/2004\n7/2004\n1/2005\n7/2005\n1/2006\n7/2006\n1/2007\n7/2007\n1/2008\n7/2008\n1/2009\n7/2009\n1/2010\n\n(thousands of tons of milled rice, 2000‐2010)\n(baht/ton, Jan. 2000 – June 2010)\n\nFigure 4 : Wholesale real price of rice 5% broken\n\nSource: USDA‐FAS\n\n154\n\n\fWhat recommendations could be derived? Up until the early 1980s, the comparative\nadvantage of rice production in Thailand could be explained by the country’s wealth of\nresources, optimized by government policies aimed at ensuring relatively equitable land\ntenure; investments in road, railway and irrigation infrastructure; and an active credit\npolicy (Phélinas, 2010). Since 2001, the Thai government’s policy to stabilize the income\nof rice producers has started to conflict with the sector’s export orientation. Above‐\nmarket intervention prices have led to significant production increases. The widespread\noutreach of intervention policies has appeared to cause difficulty for small actors on the\nmarket. Some undesirable effects may be related to inefficient management of buffer\nstocks and speculation on the part of market actors. Finally, there are questions as to\nwhether intervention policies are equitable, as they do not benefit the poorest small‐\nscale farmers.\n\n155\n\n\f3. South American experiences\n3.1\n\nLinking town and country: public policy as a tool to promote family farming,\nfood security and market regulation\nCarlos G.A. Mielitz Netto, Agronomist, PhD in Economics, Professor of the Programa de\nPos‐Graduação em Desenvolvimento Rural da Universidade Federal do Rio Grande do Sul\n‐ PGDR‐UFRGS\n\nSummary\nWhat has been done? Created in 2003, the Family Farming Food Acquisition Program\n(PAA) aims to support family farms and encourage them to market to vulnerable\npopulations facing food insecurity. The PAA uses four instruments: (i) Direct purchase of\nfoodstuffs from family farmers (CDAF), whereby the government purchases produce\nfrom farmers at subsidized or market prices to supply populations facing food insecurity.\n(ii) A guaranteed purchasing contract for family farmers (CGCAF). Farmers’ groups stock\npart of their production in exchange for a promissory note (CPR) that provides individual\nfarmers access to financial resources. (iii) Direct local purchase of family farm production\n(CDLAF), whereby farmers’ groups signed an agreement with one or more entities that\nserve vulnerable populations (e.g., charities, hospitals, public schools) to provide\nfoodstuffs. The agreement defines the quality, quantity, value and delivery of the\nfoodstuffs. The agreements in turn facilitates farmers’ access to credit, for an amount\nthat corresponds to the negotiated value placed on deposit with the national supply\ncompany (CONAB). (iv) Incentive program for milk production and consumption (IPCL):\nBased on the principle of sectoral preference, this instrument allows vulnerable\npopulations to purchase milk directly from producers.\nHow has it been implemented? Brazilian agricultural policy is two‐pronged. The Ministry\nof Agriculture mainly addresses export‐oriented agribusinesses, while the relatively\nrecent Ministry of Agrarian Development supports family farming and addresses issues\nof food security. The four mechanisms of the PAA are implemented by CONAB, based on\nan agreement with the Ministry of Social Development and Fight Against Hunger (MDS),\nthe Ministry of Agrarian Development, state and local governments. The mechanisms\nare managed locally by family farmers’ associations and civil society organizations. To\nparticipate, farmers must meet the criteria of the National Program to Strengthen\nFamily Agriculture (PRONAF). Finally, there are caps on the amount of production each\nfarmer can market through the PAA, to avoid supply surplus and inequality among\nbeneficiaries.\nWhat were the effects? The program has increased incomes of family farmers and\nimproved food security of vulnerable populations. Thanks to increased consumption of\nfresh local produce and decreased consumption of processed products, dietary habits\nhave improved. In addition, the program has resulted in a number of positive\n\n156\n\n\fexternalities: quality and hygiene standards that sometimes extend to all local\nproduction and improve the nutritional value of foodstuffs as well as the health of non‐\ndirect beneficiaries; stronger ties between the town and country; and women’s\nempowerment through processing activities. The PAA has faced some challenges.\nOutreach is limited due to financial constraints (less than 5% of family farmers are\ninvolved) and bureaucratic delays are common, due to a management structure that\ninvovles three levels of government. Finally, the program is simply an initiative of the\ncurrent government, and has not yet been institutionalized as a formal policy.\nFigure 5 : Trends in the growth of the PAA per instrument\n\nReserve purchase\n\nSimultaneous purchase and donation\n\nDirect Purchase MDS and MDA\nMilk Program\n\nFood stocks – MDS and MDA\nBudget allocation 2010 – MDS ans MDA\n\nWhat recommendations could be derived? The Brazilian experience is an example for\ncountries with similar characteristics, i.e., a family farming sector with great production\npotential and a large segment of vulnerable consumers. In this context, food policies\nthat aim to increase incomes/market access for family farmers and improve food\nsecurity of vulnerable populations through social policies, can serve to rationalize\nresources and lay the groundwork for efficiency gains in the long term. Furthermore, the\nexistence of two Ministries with different objectives allows for interventionist measures\nin favor of vulnerable populations, despite the hegemonic liberal discourse of Brazilian\nagribusiness.\n\n157\n\n\f4. Developed countries experiences\n4.1\n\nComparative study on regulation of agriculture in the United States, Canada\nand the European Union\nLucien Bourgeois, Economist; Sophie Vivienne, AgroParisTech, Department of\nComparative Agriculture and Agricultural Development; Daniel‐Mercier Gouin, Chair of\nAgricultural Policy Analysis, University of Laval, Quebec\n\nSummary\nWhat has been done? In the 1990s, the governments of these three countries set out to\nlimit budget spending without affecting the incomes of farmers: a task easier said than\ndone.\nWith the reform of the CAP in 1992, the European Union (EU) adopted a system of direct\nincome aids, like those used by the United States (US). The two major powers came to\nan agreement on this basis, and imposed it on the world by including it in the “blue box”\ncategory, set up under the Marrakech Agreement. Initially, the EU measure involved\ncompensatory aid to farmers, as a way to convince them to accept the price difference\nthat might exist between the European domestic market and the global market. The aids\nwere designed to be temporary. It was expected that European producers would\ngradually manage to offset the price difference by exporting larger volumes. But hopes\nfor global market expansion proved overly optimistic. Markets for agricultural raw\nmaterials, and especially cereals, have barely increased since the early 1980s.\nHow has it been implemented? After a period of heavy market intervention,\ngovernments were seeking solutions that made producers more accountable. Public\nstorage systems and the export subsidies used in some sectors in the United States and\nthe European Union were phased out. So‐called shared responsibility mechanisms and\ncrop insurance schemes were set up. The problem was that these schemes could only\noperate at a high cost to their governments. Recently, however, governments have\nencouraged the processing of bio‐fuels through heavy subsidies. The US has also sharply\nincreased consumer subsidies on food for the poor.\nWhat were the effects? The three countries studied have, over time, changed the\ninstruments used to regulate their agricultural sectors, either as a response to internal\npressure (often budgetary), or the international context (increased market competition\nand multilateral agreements on agriculture). In both the EU and the US, this first led to a\ndecrease in the level of price support, replaced by an increase in the level of direct\npayments, with the objective of maintaining a guaranteed income to farmers. Gradually,\ndirect payments have evolved and have been decoupled from production, to meet the\nconstraints set by the Agreement on Agriculture of the Uruguay Round. This change is\njust about complete in the European Union, with the introduction of single payment\n\n158\n\n\fentitlements. It is still only partial in the United States, which has maintained\ncountercyclical payments. In Canada, the picture is somewhat different. Price support\npolicies, which target a few products (milk, poultry and eggs), continue intact to this day.\nIncome support policies for other sectors of agricultural production, however, have also\nevolved towards decoupled payments.\nWhat recommendations could be derived? At first glance, these regulatory instruments\nappear to have reduced economic distortions. But this statement must be qualified. If\nwe look at the changes to levels of total support to agriculture in the three countries, it\nbecomes clear that while all three have respected the commitments they made at the\nWTO on domestic support, they have not decreased overall support throughout the\nperiod.\nIn fact, for all three countries, total support granted to agriculture exceeds the level of\ncommitments. Canada, the EU and the US have managed to respect their commitments,\nthanks to the \"box game\": to agree on priorities for reducing domestic support, types of\naid were categorized in three boxes. The orange box was for aid that cause the most\ndistortion and should be reduced first; the blue box included support measures\nconsidered less harmful and therefore still tolerable for a short period of time; the green\nbox was for support deemed to cause little market distortion. The result has been a rise\nin green box aids everywhere.\nThe total support to agriculture remains as high as it was in 1995 in the European Union\nand even higher in Canada and the United States, before the sharp increase in\nagricultural commodity prices in 2007 and 2008. These results reflect a reality: the\ngovernments concerned consider the agricultural sector still needs support.\n\n159\n\n\f4.2\n\nCorn markets in the United States\nLucien Bourgeois, Economist\n\nSummary\nWhat has been done? World corn production has exceeded that of wheat and rice.\nProduction could reach 811 million tons in 2010‐2011, which would be 2 million tonnes\nbelow the record high established in 2009‐2010. This is exactly double 1980 production\nlevels. Despite these perspectives, prices are soaring on the world market and exceeded\n$7.40 a bushel in early March 2011, compared to $3.70 a bushel in early March 2010,\nand $3.25 in early July 2010. Prices have doubled in a year.\nUnlike other cereals, corn production is highly concentrated in a small number of\ncountries. The United States and China account for more than 60% of global harvests.\nWith more than 40% of the total, the US is by far the largest player in the market. It is an\ninteresting case study. Corn, which has become the most abundant food on the planet,\ndepends essentially on the world’s largest economy. But curiously, American supremacy\nin corn production has not yielded impressive results in terms of entering new foreign\nmarkets or improving the performance of the agro‐food industries that rely on them. It\nis therefore relevant to see how agricultural policy has evolved in this country and its\nconsequences on the world market.\nHow has it been implemented? Since the mid‐1980s, the government has tried to\nreduce subsidies, hoping to develop demand in foreign markets. This new policy was\nfully formalized in the 1996 Farm Bill, entitled the “FAIR Act” to indicate that the US was\nready to embrace competitive practices. Results were disappointing. Just two years\nlater, prices collapsed and emergency measures were needed to once again support\nfarmers’ income. Unlike the EU, which has decoupled direct aid, the bulk of aid to US\nfarmers is countercyclical, which takes into account market prices.\nWhat were the effects? This strategy, aimed at developing export markets, has not been\nsuccessful. US corn exports have not increased for 30 years and the overall balance of\nfood products from the United States is in decline due to a relative inability to expand\nexports of processed products, which now make up most of the market.\n\nFigure 7 : Corn prices in the US and France\n\nFigure 6 : Main corn exporters 2009\n\n160\n\n\fWhat recommendations could be derived? This new policy has another major\ndrawback. It resulted in a long period of excessively low prices on the global market,\nwhich serves as a worldwide reference, despite representing just 10% of total\nproduction.\nIt took a sudden demand for corn from the domestic non‐food market to turn things\naround. Starting in 2003, ethanol production began to absorb more than than 10% of\nannual corn production. In 2007, this table grew to 20%, and exceeded 40% in early\n2011. This new demand now accounts for almost three times the tonnage of US corn\nexports. It is causing a decline in stocks that worries importing countries. This is what\nexplains the prices spikes, even though harvest levels are record.\nDue to a substitution effect, the increase has affected all types of grains. Furthermore,\nthe absence of buffer stocks in both the US and the EU has encouraged speculation.\nAltogether, these factors have led to rising food prices in importing countries. What is\nironic in this new scenario based on ethanol production is that market regulation is\nclearly easier when it relies on domestic demand rather than exports. Even when it\ncomes to the world’s largest economy.\n\n161\n\n\f","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:2","agent_id":"ad5683d3d","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:3","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"\n\nMake sure you are clear about the objectives of the project and the results\nyou want.\n\n\n\nClearly communicate the job, giving expectations and due dates. Ask your\nassistant for ideas. He or she may have good input and asking the question\nand hearing ideas also ensures that the task is understood.\n\n\n\nOffer advice but do not tell your assistant exactly how to do the work—give\nsome room for creativity. Train your assistant, if necessary, and fill him or\nher in on the needed facts and anticipated complications.\n\n\n\nRemember to build confidence and enthusiasm, so pick jobs that are suited\nto the individual’s interests and abilities. Explain why he or she was chosen\nand why the project is being done. Discuss how it fits into the bigger picture as putting assignments in a context makes them more meaningful.\n\n\n\nFor large, important projects, establish a budget, timeline, and process for\nreporting progress.\n\n\n\nGet a commitment and make sure your assistant has the time and interest\nto follow through.\n\n\n\nBe available to offer guidance, feedback, and other necessary support.\n\n\n\nTake time to evaluate the finished product for the benefit of you and your\nassistant. Recognize the lessons that both of you learned.\n\n\n\nFind someone who is a good delegator and use that person as a role model. Practice, observation, and feedback are keys to mastering delegation.\nYou may approach the person you have chosen as a role model and ask\nher or him to mentor you in gaining that skill. Mentoring can be a simple,\ntime-limited arrangement involving a phone call when you are having\ndifficulty—for example, when you are unsure how to supervise someone\nwhose work needs improvement. Experienced managers are often willing\nto share their knowledge and appreciate being asked as long as mentoring\nis not so demanding that it interferes with their own work.\n\n93\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\n\nEncouraging Participation\nLike many managers, you may feel that there is no one to whom you can delegate\ntasks. This is a problem for small and large markets alike. During the height of the\nmarket season, vendors are often too busy with their own farms to help with many\nof the market’s needs. When the whole community seems strapped for time, it is\ndifficult for a manager to ask for help. You need the support of your community\nand vendors, but it takes time to build. Following are a few steps you can take to\nencourage volunteer participation.\n\nList the Markets “Assets”\nAt the beginning of each market season, ask your\nvolunteers, board members, and vendors to give you\na list of their “assets.” What are their strengths? What\nprevious jobs have they had that give them skills in\nhelpful areas? What do they like to do? Then when\ncrunch time comes around, you will have some idea of\npeople’s strengths, interests, and experience and who\nmay be willing to help.\n\nMake Tasks Quick and Clear\nWhen you ask busy people to help you, make sure the tasks you develop are clearcut and short-term. Even if the overall project from your perspective is long and\nin-depth, breaking it up into smaller segments allows each person to handle it bit\nby bit. This makes it much less intimidating and easier for people to agree to the\ntask.\n\nMake Tasks Consistently Short\nAnother way to have help through the season is to ask a number of assistants to\nbe in charge of minor repetitive chores every week. In this way, their assignment\nis long term but still easy to handle. For instance, none of the vendors would have\ntime to help you hang signs every market morning. But if each was in charge of the\none sign nearest to their truck, they could do it every time.\n\nBuild Support\nThe best way to have a large volunteer force to whom you can delegate jobs is by\nencouraging support throughout the season. Kas Gurtler, market manager in Port\n\n94\n\n\fDelegation Techniques\n\n14\n\nTownsend, Washington, says that delegation fortunately is not a problem for her\nbecause not only does she have a large and active board to back her up but she\nalso has many “friends of the market.” Their Friends program invites the community\nto become members by paying an annual fee that gives that individual the right\nto vote on market issues. Members of Friends of the Market are interested in the\nmarket being a success, shop there regularly, and are a ready source of volunteers\nwhen needed.\n\nShow Appreciation\nBe sure to thank your volunteers when they have helped. Send a card or include\ntheir name in the market’s newsletter. Mention their help in front of others—it\nalways feels good to be recognized. Even something as small and simple as mentioning them to shoppers on market day can help a person feel valued: “Do you\nlike the new banners? Joe here and his family volunteered their time to put those\ntogether.”\n\nCelebrate Volunteerism\nEnd-of-the season parties and celebrations are opportunities to appreciate the\ntime others have put into the market. Whether it is a Boy Scout troop, the city, or\nsenior citizens that helped out, throwing a party in honor of your volunteers makes\nthem feel better about assisting you again next year.\n\nJobs That Are Good to Delegate\nSome tasks are ideal for delegation. Whenever you can, take the opportunity to\nshare them with someone else. You may find that certain individuals are especially\nadept at detail work, phone calls, or artwork. Which is great—because those are\nthe types of assignments that you want to pass around. Jobs to delegate include:\nRepetitive tasks. Those “I have to do it again . . . ” jobs seem boring, in part\nbecause you have done them a million times. Other people may have fun\ndoing them for a while. And before they get sick of it, have them help train\nsomeone else.\nGathering information. Whether it is calling around, handing out a survey,\nor searching the internet, another person can help. Often that person’s\napproach is different than yours, which may lead them to find new information. It also helps your assistant learn more about the topic and get a better\noverall perspective on a market issue.\n\n95\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nJob-building skills. Delegation is important not only to help you manage\nyour time but to also help others build skills and expand their knowledge\nand interests. Assignments that help someone move ahead, train for future\nemployment, or just learn the ins and outs of the community are helpful to\nyou both. There may be a high school or college student in the area who\nwould like to work in community development, agricultural extension, or\ncity government who would like to be an intern.\nTime-consuming details. The time to form the idea of a project is often\nmuch shorter than the time it takes to perfect the details, especially if it\nis intricate, colorful, or in need of many edits. Intermediate steps can be\ncompleted by someone else, though you should maintain control over the\nfinal result.\n\nJobs NOT to Delegate\nSetting long-term goals and a vision are things that can be done only by the market manager or by the board, depending on how active the board is. It is important\nto get input from others and to involve vendors, the community, and city officials\nin the process, but management has the ultimate responsibility for directing the\nmarket. You, as the manager, are the one with the broad-based knowledge and\nwell-rounded perspective that is needed to plan for the future of the market.\nThree other key management responsibilities should never be delegated:\n\n\nFeedback, whether positive or negative, should always be given by the\nmanager.\n\n\n\nDo not delegate the responsibility to discipline or fire someone, as appealing as it may be to let someone else deal with it.\n\n\n\nNever delegate jobs that are expected to be politically sensitive. For example, a task like dropping off fliers at the town hall and other government\nbuildings may be a great one to delegate. But if relations with one of the officials are tense and he is likely to put the volunteer on the spot, you should\ntake care of that building yourself.\n\nFairness and Morale\nIt is important to remember when delegating jobs that your volunteers and employees will be interpreting your choices. For instance, if you make a decision\nabout which task to give somebody, others may feel it was done unfairly. It is quite\nnatural as a manager to continuously rely on a few dependable assistants, but con-\n\n96\n\n\fDelegation Techniques\n\n14\n\nsider that other members may see you as having favorites or feel there is a group\nof insiders who control the market. Do some community members feel that their\nvoices are not being heard in the organizational process? Do farmers resent feeling that they are not involved enough in decision-making? Like many other tasks\na market manager has to fulfill, delegation is a matter of balance between your\nmanagerial needs, the interests and perspectives of others, and the conditions in\nwhich you are operating.\nIf you continually delegate a majority of tasks to the same individuals, it may be\ninterpreted in another way—as a sign of disrespect instead of trust. You may be\nsending the message that you believe these individuals have nothing better to\ndo or that their own work is secondary to the assigned tasks. If the jobs that are\ndelegated are not considered meaningful, then assistants may feel undervalued\nand disliked.\nManagers cannot anticipate with certainty how assistants will perceive assignments. Something you consider fun and creative may seem to your assistant as\nexcessive and unnecessary. Likewise, a serious and important job may be something you delegate to help a person gain skills, but they may see it as an impossible\nburden that you did not want to take on yourself. Failing to take note of such perceptions can leave well-intentioned managers with a load of work and no one to\nhelp. Keep in mind, though, that how you delegate work is one factor among many\nin the overall environment of the market; if you are straightforward, respectful, and\nskilled in communication and other management skills, creating a generally positive atmosphere, then your staff, vendors, and others will give you leeway for trial\nand error as you learn to delegate.\n\nConclusion\nAs a market manager, you may be working with a board of directors, volunteers,\nfriends, community members, local government offices, and farmers. It is important to be sensitive to the messages that are conveyed by delegation. It is an\nimportant tool for your job, and you and the market can benefit greatly from your\nskillful use of delegation. Only in large, urban market offices is there likely to be\na manager with a number of actual employees so most managers rely on people\nvolunteering their time. Even for the smallest of markets, the workload needs to\nbe shared. By becoming a good delegator, you can share the work appropriately,\njudiciously, and effectively.\n\n97\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\n\n98\n\n\f15\n\nConclusion\n\nI\n\nn this volume we have discussed the skills needed to be effective as a market\nmanager, as well as the stakeholder relationships that occupy the manager’s time\nand energy. As you gain experience, grow your market, and face new challenges, it\nis likely you will revisit these chapters and find new points to be useful and relevant. One farmer described his learning as a spiral:\nYou kind of experiment a little bit and you say, ‘Okay, yeah, I have gotten here\nto the next level’ . . . experiment a little bit . . . ‘Okay, I am up to the next level.’ So\nyou are always going up. It just feels like you are going in circles sometimes.”\nAs you proceed through the seasons of the market and cycles of learning and\nexperiment, this book is designed to accompany you and provide information and\ntips to consider when you need them.\nIn the introduction to this book, we encouraged you to find peers and possibly a\nmentor to share or guide your skill development. Experience and the support of\ncolleagues are two of the most powerful sources of learning and growth. While\nsome task-oriented managers may perceive time spent with colleagues to be\nunproductive, in fact the time it takes to reflect on your experiences and share\nlessons with colleagues is well spent. By sharing experiences and perspectives, you\ncan develop new ways of looking at familiar situations and learn more than you\nmight on your own. This pays off in skills and in your ability to lead your market to\nsuccess, which leads to positive results for all your stakeholders. The information\nin this volume, including tips from experienced market managers, can be a part of\nyour road map to success as a manager.\n\n99\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\n\n100\n\n\fThe University of California prohibits discrimination against or harassment of any person employed by or seeking employment with the University\non the basis of race, color, national origin, religion, sex, physical or mental disability, medical condition (cancer-related or genetic characteristics),\nancestry, marital status, age, sexual orientation, citizenship, or status as a covered veteran (special disabled veteran, Vietnam-era veteran or any\nother veteran who served on active duty during a war or in a campaign or expedition for which a campaign badge has been authorized). University\npolicy is intended to be consistent with provisions of applicable state and federal laws. Inquiries regarding the University’s nondiscrimination policies may be directed to the Affirmative Action Director, University of California, Agriculture and Natural Resources, 300 Lakeside Drive, 6th Floor,\nOakland, CA. 94612-3550. (510) 987-0096.\n\n\fThe Farmers Market Management Series\n\nS\n\neveral years ago, having observed trends in the\nevolution of food marketing, I decided it would\nbe a good idea to professionalize management of\nfarmers markets by, among other things, developing\nand making available a Farmers Market Management\nprofessional development curriculum. I approached\nthe U.S. Department of Agriculture’s Agricultural\nMarketing Service (USDA AMS) for seed money to\nlaunch the project, and the agency was supportive in\nhelping the UC Small Farm Center get the project off\nthe ground.\nThe project has proceeded deliberately since its\ninception with support from the Small Farm Center.\nHappily, the center has now produced three primary volumes as a foundation for\nthe curriculum: Starting a New Farmers Market, Farmers Market Management Skills,\nand Growing Your Farmers Market.\nI wish to acknowledge the early support of this project by Errol Bragg of USDA\nAMS and, more recently, by USDA’s Risk Management Agency. Also, farmers\nmarket managers throughout the country were extremely cooperative in sharing\ntheir experiences and insights regarding the many aspects of their work.\nThe UC Small Farm Program and Small Farm Center were early advocates\nof farmers markets and facilitated education in this regard. Although much wisdom\ncan be extracted from Tomlin’s The Pesticide Manual, even more is\nfound in The Norwegian Folk Tales and Astrid Lindgren’s Pippi\nLangstrømpe, which you for long periods gave me an excuse to\nconcentrate upon.\n\n\f\fAbout the Author\nJørgen H.V. Stenersen, Dr. Philos., is a professor in ecotoxicology at the\nBiological Institute, University of Oslo. He graduated as Cand. Real. in\nbiochemistry in 1964 (University of Oslo) and subsequently worked at the\nNorwegian Plant Protection Institute on research related to possible side\neffects of pesticides. His first interests were the mechanisms behind insect\nresistance to insecticides, with emphasis on DDT resistance in stable flies.\nHe was also engaged in studies of the extent of DDT contamination of soil,\nfauna, and humans as a result of DDT usage in orchards.\nDuring a one-year stay at the Agricultural Research Laboratory in London, Ontario, Canada, he became interested in the effects of pesticides on\nearthworms at the biochemical level. This became his research focus for some\nyears. A stay at the Biochemical Institute of the University of Stockholm led\nto research in the comparative biochemistry of biotransformation enzymes\n(glutathione transferases).\nIn 1985, Dr. Stenersen was a senior lecturer in ecotoxicology at the\nUniversity of Oslo, becoming a professor in 1994. Since then he has been\ndevoted to the education of environmental toxicologists. It is his opinion\nthat the basic knowledge of health-oriented toxicologists and ecotoxicologists should be the same, or at least overlap so they can compete in the same\njob market and “speak the same language.” He is a member of the Norwegian Committee for Approval of EUROTOX Registered Toxicologists and is\nresponsible for the master’s and Ph.D. studies of toxicology at the Biological\nInstitute, University of Oslo.\nThe mode of action of pesticides, development of resistance, and their\nside effects are central topics in this pursuit. The present book is an enlarged\nrevision of an earlier book written in Norwegian (Kjemiske plantevernmidler,\n1988, Yrkeslitteratur as, Oslo, pp. 218), and is suited to introductory courses\nin general toxicology. Dr. Stenersen’s current research interest is the application of biochemical methods in terrestrial ecotoxicology.\n\n\f\fAcknowledgments\nI thank in particular Professor John Ormerod at the Department of Biology,\nUniversity of Oslo; Senior Scientist Avi Ring at the Norwegian Defense\nResearch Institute; and Senior Scientist Christian Thorstensen at the Norwegian Plant Protection Institute for reviewing the many chapters, correcting,\nand proposing rewriting. Thanks also go to Baard Johannessen for providing\nmaterials to the chapter about interaction. I also thank my students that had\nto hear and learn more about pesticides than they actually needed for their\nexams and future jobs.\nI also thank Catherine Russel at Taylor & Francis for her difficult job of\ncorrecting my English.\n\n\f\fContents\nChapter 1 Introduction ......................................................................................1\n1.1 Motivation Conclusions.................................................................................................246\nLiterature..............................................................................................................249\nIndex .....................................................................................................................265\n\n\fchapter one\n\nIntroduction\n1.1 Motivation\nThe mode of action of pesticides is extremely fascinating because the subject\ncovers so many fields of biology and chemistry and has many practical\nimplications.\nAll disciplines of biology have developed greatly since 1,1,1-trichloro-di-(4-chlorophenyl)ethane — better known as DDT — and the other\nsynthetic pesticides were introduced just after the Second World War. At that\ntime, the knowledge of the normal biochemical and physiological processes\nin organisms was not sufficiently clarified to make it possible for us to\nunderstand properly either the mode of action of the pesticides at the target\nsite or their uptake, distribution, and degradation in the ambient environment. The development of resistance of various pests to pesticides should\nhave been possible to predict at that time, even before the use of these\npesticides had expanded so much, but how rapidly or to what degree resistance would develop and what biochemical mechanisms where behind the\ndevelopment had to be a matter of experience and research.\nWe now know how nerve impulses are transmitted, how plants synthesize amino acids, and how fungi invade plant tissue. The textbooks in the\nvarious biological disciplines have become enormous, but in spite of this,\nthey do not tell us where and why pesticides interfere with the normal\nprocesses. Other toxicants are mentioned occasionally, but only when they\nhave been tools for the exploration of the normal processes. The intention\nof this book is therefore to try to collate some of the knowledge in the\nrespective biological sciences and to explain the points at which the pesticides have an effect. While reading this book students are encouraged to\nconsult textbooks in biochemistry, nerve physiology, plant biochemistry, and\nso forth, in order to get a more comprehensive explanation of the normal\nprocesses disturbed by pesticides. To understand the toxicology of pesticides,\nit is first necessary to learn organic chemistry, biochemistry, almost all disciplines of plant and animal physiology at the cellular or organismic level,\nand ecology, as well as the applied sciences within agriculture. This is, of\ncourse, impossible, but these disciplines will for many students be much\n1\n\n\f2\n\nChemical pesticides: Mode of action and toxicology\n\nmore interesting when put into the context of an applied science, e.g., pesticide science. At the least, myself and many of my students have become\nmotivated to go back to learn more of organic chemistry and the biological\nsciences when confronted with the pesticides or other groups of toxicants,\ncurious about why they are toxic or not for different organisms.\n\n1.2 Pesticides and opinion\nFrom 1962 to about 1975, there was hot debate about pesticides. Everyone\nhad an opinion about them. Knowledge of chemistry, agriculture, toxicology,\nand so on, was not necessary. The debate was a precursor to the conflicts in\nthe 1970s about environmental issues. In those days words like pollutants,\nenvironmental contamination, biocides, pesticides, DDT, mercury, etc., were synonymous. People were putting together all negative properties of synthetic\ncompounds: they were all called biocides, were persistent with a tendency\nto bioaccumulate, and were all regarded as carcinogens. The scientific and\ntechnological establishment had, of course, difficulties in meeting this avalanche of opinion. Toxicology was, and still is, a much less developed science\nthan, for instance, the science of making bridges, or other fields where hazard\nand risk assessments must play an essential role.\nPesticides are toxic substances applied on plants that are going to be\nfood. It is therefore not difficult to understand the great focus from the public\non these substances. The legislation and control of their use were not very\nmuch developed, and at the same time, the need for pest control agents was\nvery high, but the growing urban population was a little removed from this\nreality. We must, of course, not forget the very high and unchallenged optimism of the first decade after the Second World War. DDT and the newer\npersistent pesticides should solve all problems of controlling insect-borne\ndiseases as well as preventing food loss due to insect pests. The use of\npesticides was indiscriminate and the remonstrance was few. Rachel Carson’s book Silent Spring (1962) was an important warning and should be\nread (with caution) today.\nToday the legal systems for approving pesticides are much more\ndemanding than they were during the first optimistic years. This is illustrated by the situation in Norway. In 1965 there was only one person in a\npart-time job that collected data about the toxicology of pesticides in order\nto advise official authorities about approval and safe use. Today there are at\nleast a dozen toxicologists as well as a few agriculturists to do the same job.\nThe safety and agricultural advantages of all pesticides have to be scrutinized\nbefore approval. A committee of independent experts now advises the agricultural minister.\n\n1.2.1\n\nThe fly in the soup\n\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}

However, it\n\nwould be the responsibility of the respective testing laboratory to confirm that the above methods are\nvalidated in its laboratory and gives proper result in their laboratory.\n\n4\n\n\fPESTICIDE RESIDUES 2016\n\nMANUAL FOR ANALYSIS OF PESTICIDE RESIDUES\n1. CODEX GUIDELINES ON GOOD PRACTICE IN PESTICIDE RESIDUE ANALYSIS\n\n1.1 Introduction:\nThe Codex document ALINORM 76/24 Appendix IV (Report of the ad hoc Working Group\non Methods of Analysis) contained the following statement:\n\"It was considered that the ultimate goal in fair practice in international trade depended,\namong other things, on the reliability of analytical results. This in turn, particularly in\npesticide residue analysis, depended not only on the availability of reliable analytical\nmethods, but also on the experience of the analyst and on the maintenance of good\npractice in the analysis of pesticides.\nThese guidelines define such good analytical practice and may be considered in three\ninter-related parts:\nThe Analyst\nBasic Resources\nThe Analysis\n1.2 The Analyst:\nResidue analysis consists of a chain of procedures, most of which are known, or readily\nunderstood, by a trained chemist, but because the analyte concentrations are in the range\ng/kg to mg/kg and because the analysis can be challenging, attention to detail is essential.\nThe analyst/ in-charge should have an appropriate professional qualification and be\nexperienced and competent in residue analysis. Staff must be fully trained and\nexperienced in the correct use of apparatus and in appropriate laboratory skills. The\nanalyst can use the method within the expected performance parameters established\nduring method validation prior to analysis of samples. They must have an understanding\n5\n\n\fPESTICIDE RESIDUES 2016\nof the principles of pesticide residue analysis and the requirements of Analytical Quality\nAssurance (AQA) systems. They must understand the purpose of each stage in the method,\nthe importance of following the methods exactly as described and of nothing any\nunavoidable deviations. They must also be trained in the evaluation and interpretation of\nthe data they produce. A record of training and experience must be kept for all laboratory\nstaff.\nWhen a laboratory for residue analysis is set up, the staff should spend some of their\ntraining period in a well established laboratory where experienced advice and training is\navailable. If the laboratory is to be involved in the analysis for a wide range of pesticide\nresidues, it may be necessary for the staff to gain experience in more than one expert\nlaboratory.\n1.3 Basic Resources\n1.3.1 The laboratory:\nThe laboratory and its facilities must be designed to allow tasks to be allocated to welldefined areas where maximum safety and minimum chance of contamination of samples\nprevail. Laboratories should be constructed of, and utilise materials resistant to chemicals\nlikely to be used within them. Under ideal conditions, separate rooms would be designated\nfor sample receipt and storage, for sample preparation, for extraction and clean-up and for\ninstrumentation used in the determinative step. The area used for extraction and clean-up\nmust meet solvent laboratory specifications and all fume extraction facilities must be of\nhigh quality. Simple receipt, storage and preparation should be handled in areas devoted\nto work at residue levels. Maintenance of sample integrity and adequate provisions for\npersonal safety for priority requirements.\nLaboratory safety must also be considered in terms of what is essential and what is\npreferable, as it must be recognised that the stringent working conditions enforced in\nresidue laboratories in some parts of the world could be totally unrealistic in others. No\nsmoking, eating, drinking or application of cosmetics should be permitted in the working\narea. Only small volumes of solvents should be held in the working area and the bulk of\n\n6\n\n\fPESTICIDE RESIDUES 2016\nthe solvents stored separately, away from the main working area. The use of highly toxic\nsolvents and reagents should be minimised whenever possible. All waste solvent should\nbe stored safely and disposed of both safely and in an environmentally friendly manner\ntaking into account specific national regulations where available.\nThe main working area should be designed and equipped for utilisation of an appropriate\nrange of analytical solvents. All equipment such as macerators and refrigerators should be\nspark free or explosion proof. Extraction, clean-up and concentration steps should be\ncarried out in a well ventilated area, preferably in fume cupboards.\nSafety screens should be used when glassware is used under vacuum or pressure. There\nshould be an ample supply of safety glasses, gloves and other protective clothing,\nemergency washing facilities and a spillage treatment kit. Adequate fire fighting\nequipment must be available. Staff must be aware that many pesticides have acutely or\nchronically toxic properties and therefore, great care is necessary in the handling of\nstandard reference compounds.\n1.4 Equipment and Supplies:\nThe laboratory will require adequate, reliable, supplies of electricity and water. Adequate\nsupplies of reagents, solvents, gas, glassware, chromatographic materials etc. of suitable\nquality are essential.\nChromatographic equipment, balances, spectrophotometers etc. must be serviced and\ncalibrated regularly and a record of all servicing/repairs must be maintained for every\nsuch item of equipment. Calibration is essential for equipment including glassware\nperforming measurements. Calibration curves and comparison with standards may\nsuffice. Regular calbration and re-calibration of measuring equipment must be done where\nthe possible change in nominal value may significantly contribute to the uncertainty of the\nmeasurement. Balances and automated pipettes/dispensers and similar equipment must\nbe calibrated regularly. The operating temperatures of refrigerators and freezers should\nbe continually monitored or be checked at specified intervals. All records should be kept\nup-to-date and retained.\n\n7\n\n\fPESTICIDE RESIDUES 2016\n\nAll laboratories require pesticide reference standards of known and acceptably high\npurity. Analytical standards should be available for all parent compounds for which the\nlaboratory is monitoring samples, as well as those metabolites that are included in MRLs.\nAll analytical standards, stock solutions and reagents whose integrity could be influenced\nby degradative processes must be clearly labelled with an expiry date and stored under\nproper conditions. Pure reference standards must be kept under conditions specified by\nmanufacturer or lower that will minimise the rate of degradation, e.g. low temperature,\nexclusion of moisture, darkness. Equal care must be taken that standard solutions of\npesticides are not decomposed by the effect of light or heat during storage or become\nconcentrated owing to solvent evaporation.\n1.5 The Analysis\n1.5.1 Avoidance of contamination:\nOne of the significant areas in which pesticide residue analysis differs significantly from\nmacro-analysis is that of contamination and interference. Trace amounts of contamination\nin the final samples used for the determination stage of the method can give rise to errors\nsuch as false positive or false negative results or to a loss of sensitivity that may prevent\nthe residue from being detected. Contamination may arise from almost anything that is\nused, for or is associated with, sampling, sample transport and storage, and the analysis.\nAll glassware, reagents, organic solvents and water should be checked for possible\ninterfering contaminations before use, by analysis of a reagent blank.\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}

When Charles Wiley ﬁrst\nopened his small printing shop in lower Manhattan in 1807, it was a generation\nof boundless potential searching for an identity. And we were there, helping to\ndeﬁne a new American literary tradition. Over half a century later, in the midst\nof the Second Industrial Revolution, it was a generation focused on building the\nfuture. Once again, we were there, supplying the critical scientiﬁc, technical,\nand engineering knowledge that helped frame the world. Zabkiewicz (New Zealand)\n\n1341vch00.indd VI\n\n23.03.2007 13:08\n\n\fPreface\nThe 11th IUPAC International Congress of Pesticide Chemistry was held from\nAugust 6–11, 2006, in Kobe, Japan. Since the 5th Congress held in Kyoto in 1982,\nthis was the second time that the Congress took place in Japan. During this\n24-year time period, we witnessed dramatic changes in science and technology\naround pesticides. The Congress’ subtitle, “Evolution for Crop Protection, Public\nHealth and Environmental Safety”, focused on the current situation surrounding\npesticides, which are now more commonly referred to as agrochemicals.\nPesticides or agrochemicals have played not only a critical role in the production\nof food and feed to support the growing world population’s demands, but also in\ncontrol of infectious diseases transmitted by insect vectors and microorganisms.\nAdvances in technologies such as computational chemistry, automated highthroughput biological screens, crop genetics, biotechnology, formulations, and\nprecision agriculture have offered novel tools in the discovery and development of\nnew agrochemicals. For example, new chemical classes such as the sulfonylurea\nand imidazolinone herbicides, the neonicotinoid insecticides, and the strobilurin\nfungicides were signiﬁcant discoveries during this 24-year period.\nPublic concerns pay more attention to risk assessment and risk management\nof pesticide use regarding human health and environmental safety. The discovery\nof “safer” pesticides with new modes of action becomes the challenge of the new\ngeneration of pesticide scientists. One of the highlights of this Kobe Congress was\nthe presentation of a novel class of insecticides (RynaxypyrTM and ﬂubendiamide)\nwhich target speciﬁcally the insect ryanodine receptor. For example, RynaxypyrTM\nshowed laboratory and ﬁeld activities on all major lepidoptera pests at a low rate\nof 0.01 ppm (equivalent to approximately 10 g/ha). Furthermore, a 350+-fold\ndifference in safety/selectivity was observed between mammalian and insect cell\ntargets. Both compounds show a great safety margin to non-target organisms.\nThese attributes set new standards for new safe and active insecticides.\nThe high cost of conducting pesticide research and discovery, along with the\never-increasing pesticide registration requirements and regulations, also have\na negative impact on the advances of pesticide science. With an estimated cost\nof approximately 200 million USD and 11 years to bring a new pesticide from\ndiscovery to the market place, this economic pressure resulted in a signiﬁcant\nconsolidation of the agrochemical business during the past 10+ years. Today,\nfewer than 10 major agrochemical companies remain. Important products\n\n1341vch00.indd VII\n\n23.03.2007 13:09\n\n\fcancellation of these products has a signiﬁcant impact on farmers, especially in\ndeveloping countries. It is a continuous loss of useful tools in crop protection\nand public health protection. The topics of global harmonization of pesticide\nresidues (MRL) and vector-borne communicable diseases were addressed in this\nCongress.\nOn a more positive note, presentations on new technologies and approaches\nfrom this Congress offer a bright future for discovery of a new generation of low\nuse rate, highly selective, and environmentally friendly products.\nThe Congress had 1142 participants from 52 countries. The Congress’ scientiﬁc\nprogram included the keynote address, 4 plenary lectures, and approximately\n110 session lectures. In addition to the platform lectures, more than 550 posters\nwere submitted. The poster award committee selected three excellent papers\nfrom each of three categorized presentations. The ﬁrst paper in each category\nwas also awarded by IUPAC. This publication includes the keynote address, 4\nplenary lectures, and one or two papers in each of 20 sessions, and the posters\nawarded by IUPAC.\nWe take this opportunity to acknowledge the efforts from members of the\norganizing and programming committees, along with the help from countless\nvolunteers. We also need to point out several novel programs implemented at\nthis Congress, including the Research Director Forum, the programming of the\nspecial luncheon and evening seminars, and the publication of the Congress\nNews Letter (Kobe Gazette).\nThe publication of the Congress Proceeding is a time-honored tradition for\nthe IUPAC International Congress of Pesticide Chemistry. This Proceeding not\nonly accurately documents the scientiﬁc contents of this Conference, but also\nhighlights signiﬁcant advances and important issues facing pesticide science\nand technology. The Editorial Team expresses our appreciation to Wiley-VCH\n(Germany) and Dr. Frank O. Weinreich for the publication of this Proceeding. In\nparticular, we gratefully acknowledge the dedicated editorial support provided by\nMs. Carol Ashman.\nFinally, this Proceeding is dedicated to all past and present pesticide scientists;\nit is their vision and creativity that continue to push back the frontier of pesticide\nsciences. We look forward to seeing you at the 12th IUPAC International Congress\nof Pesticide Chemistry, in Melbourne, Australia, 2010.\nEditorial Team\nHideo Ohkawa (Kobe, Japan)\nHisashi Miyagawa (Kyoto, Japan)\nPhilip W.
