market\nactivities, including special events,\nadvertising, and meetings.\nBudgeting: Work with the board to\ndevelop and manage a reasonable budget\nbased on short-term goals and activities.\nImplementing: Implement actions\ncollectively chosen or individually as\nappropriate.\n\nProblem-solving: Identify, analyze, and\npropose solutions for problems.\nCommunicating: Communicate\neffectively with various constituencies\nof the market—vendors, customers,\nregulators, public officials, the media, the\ncommunity at large, and the board of\ndirectors.\nManaging conflict: Effectively manage\nconflict that arises among vendors,\nconsumers, the staff, volunteers, and\nboard members.\nDelegating: Delegate tasks that can be\naccomplished by others.\nLong-range planning: Periodically\nengage the board to develop and revise\nlong-range strategic plans to position or\nreposition the market to access emerging\nopportunities or confront threats to its\nsustainability.\n\nMonitoring: Monitor progress on\nprojects and activities and provide\nappropriate feedback.\nEvaluating: Evaluate the level of\nachievement and performance of changes\nin the system.\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nissues keep you awake at night? The answers to these questions can tell you where\nto begin. Alternately, you may want to start with something that seems familiar,\nhone your skills, and continue to improve on your strengths.\nEach chapter begins with a brief introduction and outlines the skills and abilities the chapter is designed to develop. By looking at the learning objectives for\nthe chapter, you can assess your need for the contents. You may return again and\nagain to certain chapters and skip others completely. While the skills, abilities, and\nrelationships discussed in this book are complex and interwoven, you may already\nhave many of the necessary qualities and skills.\nTwo of the most important sources of learning for managers are on-the-job experience and discussions with colleagues. We encourage you to read this book\nwith your actual experiences in mind and to find colleagues with whom and from\nwhom you can learn.\n\n\u0018\n\n\f2\n\nThe Manager as Visionary and Leader\n\nY\n\nThis chapter will\nenable you to:\n\n Understand what is\n\ninvolved in developing\nthe vision and direction\nfor your farmers\nmarket.\n\n Know the traits\n\ncommon to effective\nleaders.\n\n Know how vision and\nleadership contribute\nto your success as a\nmanager.\n\nou may know more about your farmers market than\nanybody else. You know who your farmers are, who your\ncustomers are, who supports your market, how and when the\nbills are paid, and where the stationery is kept. You know the\nsmall details and the big issues. But you are not working alone.\nTo be an effective leader, you need to have a vision. It is the\nresponsibility of the board of directors to work with you to\ncreate the vision for your market, but you play a huge role in\ndeveloping, communicating, and implementing the vision.\n\nDeveloping the Vision\nand Direction for Your Market\n\nYou need to be clear about the goals and objectives of your\nmarket so that you can articulate them to the people with\nwhom you work. Even though you may have a hundred things\non your to-do list each week, it is critical always to keep the\n“big picture” in mind.\nTake some time to reflect on these questions:\n\n\nHow does this market fit into the community?\n\n\n\nWhat products and services does it provide?\n\n\n\nWhat role do I see it playing in the neighborhood now and into the future?\n\n\n\nHow do the decisions I make affect the bigger picture?\n\nKnow how to talk about your market. Be prepared for questions from a variety of\ndifferent sources and have answers to them that will generate interest and excitement in what you are doing. Whether you are recruiting new vendors for the\nmarket or planning a promotional event with local talent, sharing your market’s\nstory will get people involved.\nFor instance, if a local reporter is interested in writing an article on your market,\nwhat would you say at the interview? “Well, we are a bunch of farmers out here\nselling our produce so come support us” does not convey the same message as\n“We are providing a valuable service to the community by bringing high quality,\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nfresh produce straight from the farm to a friendly social environment in the center\nof your community.” The way you represent the market to the public will have a big\nimpact on how customers perceive it.\nThe vision that you carry will be the source of energy and enthusiasm from which\neveryone involved in your market will draw. People will be looking to you for\ndirection; if you are not motivated to make the\nmarket the best that it can be, they likely will not\nbe either.\n\nThe Manager as Leader\nWhen you think back on the best managers you\nhave worked with in the past, what personality\ncharacteristics stand out for you? Is it their strong\nbooming voice or the infectious energy they brought to the job? Is it the way they\nalways seemed to make the right decision at the right time? Great managers are\nleaders. They are confident, optimistic, decisive, creative, and ethical. They also\ntreat people with respect and often maintain a sense of humor when the going\ngets tough. There are different leadership styles, and some great managers get\nthings done in the background, enabling others to shine and developing their\ntalents, while others naturally gravitate toward the spotlight. Use the following list\nof leadership traits to build on your natural strengths.\n\nConfident\nGreat managers know they can get the job done. No problem is insurmountable.\nIt may take some creative thinking and hard work to figure out, but it can be fixed,\naccomplished, or improved. To inspire the confidence of the people with whom\nyou work, you must display this trait yourself. People do not like to work for someone who is unsure or can be easily pushed around. The confidence you project as\nmanager will help others feel more confident about the work they are doing for\nthe market as well. Being confident does not mean making unrealistic promises\nabout your abilities. Rather, it means knowing exactly what you are capable of and\nworking to your highest capacity.\n\nOptimistic\nPeople are attracted to optimism. If you see the gold at the end of the rainbow, you\nmake it easier for others to see it too. No one wants to work for a negative or pessimistic manager. Focusing on the difficulties of running the market or the lousy\n\n\u0018\n\n\fThe Manager as Visionary and Leader\n\n2\n\nweather on market day will demotivate people around you. On the other hand,\nyou have great potential to infect others with positive energy that will make them\nfeel good about the market and their contributions to it.\n\nDecisive\nA good manager knows how to make decisions. Effective decision-making, which\nis discussed in more detail later, requires not just blurting out orders and directives\nfrom the top but gathering input from diverse perspectives and critically assessing\nsituations.\n\nCreative\nManagement techniques are not set in stone. A good manager often relies on\na little creative thinking to develop new strategies, solve problems, or find new\nsources of funding. Do not be afraid to look beyond the “normal” way of doing\nthings for some creative options that have not been tried before.\n\nRespectful\nPeople fundamentally want to be treated well. If you treat others with respect, they\nwill return the favor. A great deal of your job centers on your ability to get other\npeople to work with you. Respecting your coworkers and business associates will\nmake your task much easier.\n\nBalanced\nDo not forget your sense of humor. If you are not enjoying your work, you may be\nmaking others miserable too. Humor can take the edge off the most difficult situations, so do not be afraid to look at the humorous side of things.\n\nEthical\nOver time, you will develop a detailed understanding of the issues and problems\nthat your market faces. But have you taken time to think about your personal ethics in regard to these issues? Say, for instance, that you have begun to suspect that\none of your more popular vendors is selling produce that she did not grow herself.\nShe draws many customers to the market, and the other vendors do not appear\nto be aware of what she is doing. Where do you draw the line? Do you run the risk\nof losing customers and kick her out of the market? Do you say nothing and hope\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\nthat no one else figures out what is going on? In this case, it is your personal and\nprofessional ethics that will decide the outcome. Perhaps no one will figure out\nthat you let this woman sell outside produce, but they may know instinctively that\nyou are applying different standards to different situations.\n\nTrustworthy\nBuilding trust takes time. If you have been active in your community for a long\ntime, you likely have already established a reputation with many of the people\naround you. You know the value of the long-term relationships you have developed with your neighbors, business associates, and friends. If you are new to the\narea, you have a lot of work to do to encourage trust from the people with whom\nyou work. If you have taken over a market from a previous manager, you may be\nseen as an outsider for awhile and you will have to put some extra effort into building trust with established vendors and customers.\nThe importance of being trustworthy cannot be overstated. The degree to which\nothers trust you will determine how likely they will be to work with you now and to\ncontinue to work with you in the future. Once you have secured the trust of those\naround you, it is important to maintain that trust. If a person trusts you and you\nbetray that trust, it will be quite difficult, perhaps impossible, to regain it. Furthermore, word of mouth from scorned business associates or former customers can\nbe devastating to a business.\n\nConclusion\nThis chapter has introduced you to some of the responsibilities of leaders and to\nsome of the characteristics shared by effective leaders. The next chapter discusses\nroles and responsibilities of the manager in carrying out the day-to-day work of\nthe market.\n\n\u0018\n\n\f3\n\nDay-to-Day Management\n\nC\n\nhapter One introduced you to the manager’s roles as visionary and leader\nand as day-to-day manager and administrator. This chapter provides further\nexplanation and detail about the manager’s functions in running the market. The\nfunctions of a manager, with some overlap, includes analyst, goal-setter, organizer,\ndecision-maker, communicator, and evaluator. Each of those roles occurs to some\nextent as you carry out others; for example, in your role as organizer you certainly need to communicate and when you set\nThis chapter will\ngoals you must analyze and evaluate. In this chapter we bring\nenable you to:\neach of those different roles to the foreground so that you can\n Understand the many\nsee how each contributes to your success as a manager.\nfunctions of the farmers\nmarket manager.\n\n Add to the strategies\n\nThe Manager as Analyst\n\nAs a farmers market manager, you must be able to think critically and, at times, ask difficult questions. Your vision of the\nideal farmers market must be grounded in practical and realistic\nterms. The simplest solution is not always the best so do not be\nafraid to ask questions and rethink your original plans. Some\nquestions to keep in mind whenever you are trying to accomplish something new\nor reflecting on recent events include:\n\nand techniques you use\nin each function.\n\n\n\nWhat can we do to make this situation better?\n\n\n\nWhat have we overlooked?\n\n\n\nDoes everyone have the resources they\nneed to accomplish the task?\n\n\n\nDo we have a backup plan in case something goes wrong?\n\nTaking time to critically analyze and revise your plans will support actions that better fit into your big picture rather than detract from it.\n\nThe Manager as Goal-Setter\nEvery organization has goals that it is trying to achieve and every manager is\nresponsible in some part for setting them. Well-defined goals are critical for provid-\n\n\u0018\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\ning direction and purpose to an organization. You cannot achieve something if you\ncannot see it, and goals are the lanterns lighting the way. They not only help you\nsee where you are going; they help you measure how far you have come. Goals\nhelp clarify individuals’ roles in an organization, identify tasks that need to be accomplished, and articulate the vision of the organization to others.\n\nSetting Goals\nWhether you are setting the goals for the entire organization or working with a diverse group of board and\ncommunity members, setting goals is an important process in determining the future direction of your market.\nFollowing are some things to consider when setting\ngoals.\n\nKeep Your Goals Simple\nSimple goals can be understood by everyone involved in the organization. They\nare clear and unambiguous. If the goal you have in mind takes more than one\nsentence to state, you probably are trying to describe multiple goals. If this is the\ncase, break your idea into single, one-sentence goals. Goals should be specific\nstatements about what you want to achieve. Following are examples of clear, welldefined goals:\n\n\nIncrease the number of customers at the market by 10 percent by next\nyear.\n\n\n\nFind two new sponsors for our market by Christmas.\n\nNotice that these goals have specific timelines and numbers that can be measured.\nIf your goals are too vague, you will have difficulty working toward them or knowing when they have been accomplished.\n\nLimit the Number of Goals\nHaving too many goals is as bad as not having any. An extensive list of goals will\nonly detract energy from the focus of making real progress in your organization.\nPick two or three goals that have the greatest relevance to your organization and\nwork on accomplishing those.\n\n10\n\n\fDay-to-Day Management\n\n3\n\nTake Small Steps to Reach Big Goals\nIt is important to break big goals into small steps. For example, you may want to\nreach the goal of increasing your number of customers by 10 percent in the next\nyear. That may seem like an overwhelming task without a plan for how to get there.\nBy breaking that goal down into smaller, more easily accomplished steps, however,\nyou can begin to understand your progress toward the goal and know when you\neventually will reach it.\n\nLink Goals to the Roles and Responsibilities of Individuals\nIt does little good to define goals that no one has the time or resources to achieve.\nMake sure that the goals you set for your organization fit with the responsibilities\nof the people working with you. Otherwise, they soon are forgotten or pushed\naside.\n\nRevisit and Revise\nPeriodically look back to your goals and assess your progress toward achieving\nthem. Goals should be flexible enough that you can modify them as situations and\nenvironments change. If the goals you first set do not address the reality of your\nmarket, do not be afraid to change them. They should help you, not hold you back.\n\nCommunicate Your Goals\nOnce you have identified your\ngoals, everyone involved in\nyour organization needs to be\nmade aware of them. Communicating your goals will help\nget others to commit to them.\nHave one-on-one meetings\nwith people to inform them\nof the significance of the goals. Get people excited about what you are trying to\naccomplish. If a goal requires a team effort, have a meeting with the entire team\nto plan and discuss the project. Well-defined, relevant goals help create a shared\nvision of the organization, so use them to your advantage.\nIf your goals require work to be done by others, assign specific projects with clearly\nunderstood timelines. Explaining the overall vision of the project will help get\npeople committed to it. By outlining specific tasks, you help others understand\n\n11\n\n\fFarmers Market Management Skills\ntheir roles in making the vision happen in a way that is manageable. Make sure\nthat people are clear about what their responsibilities are before sending them off\nto do the job. Finally, in subsequent days or weeks, monitor their progress. Provide\npositive feedback as well as constructive criticism when you can to encourage continued performance on the project.\n\nThe Manager as Organizer\nMuch of your time is spent organizing—organizing yourself and organizing the\nwork of others.\n\nGetting Yourself Organized\nBefore you can effectively organize other people and coordinate something as\ncomplex as a farmers market, you must first get yourself organized. Knowing exactly what your roles and responsibilities are—and, more importantly, how you are\ngoing to accomplish them—will make your job much easier and make your goals\nmore attainable.\nManage your time. One of the most difficult and essential elements of being\norganized is managing your time. Chances are you spend more time than\nyou realize doing things that are not top priorities for your job: getting\ninterrupted by phone calls, shuffling through endless emails, coordinating\nparts of an event that other people could do just as easily. If, as nineteenth-century economist Vilfredo Pareto theorized, most people spend 20\npercent of their effort accomplishing 80 percent of their job, where does\nthe rest of their effort go? Chapter Thirteen offers some helpful tips for\nlearning to effectively manage your time.\nPrioritize your responsibilities. Another tool to help you get organized is to\nprioritize the tasks associated with your job. Which activities are the most\nimportant? Which are most urgent? Which ones can wait for a few days\nuntil things slow down? Try to identify what must be done and which tasks\nare just not that important. Remember, someone else’s crisis is not necessarily your crisis. Poor planning or lack of foresight on their part may not\nbe your fault. Do not let someone else’s problems distract you from what is\nmost important for you to accomplish to effectively do your job.\nKeep your desk organized. As simple as it may seem, having an organized\nwork space will make it easier for you to get your work done. When the\nphone rings and someone wants to know who is performing at your\n\n12\n\n\fDay-to-Day Management\n\n3\n\nmarket next week, do you have the schedule nearby? Can you find things\neasily when you need them? Take time to create a system for filing your\npaperwork, stacking your bills, and keeping track of appointments. Get\na day planner or desk calendar on which you can keep track of meetings\nand make notes so you know what you have coming up in the near future.\nMake sure this system works for you—it does no good if you cannot remember how it works or spend more time organizing your desk than doing\nyour work.\nSet clear boundaries. Because you are responsible for making the market\nfunction, you most likely have people coming to you all the time asking\nyou to solve problems and make decisions. It is easy to get distracted from\nwhat you are doing to answer a question or do something else that is “more\nurgent.” To effectively manage time, however, try to keep interruptions to\na minimum when you work on a complex project. Make sure that other\npeople know your availability, both while you are in the office and once\nyou leave. It is okay to turn your pager off when you are not at work and\nnot take phone calls at home past a certain hour in the evening. Set clear\nboundaries on your time and make sure people respect them. This will save\nyour energy for the times you are actually on the clock.\n\nOrganizing Others\nOne of your primary roles as manager is to organize the activities and responsibilities of the people with whom you work. Doing this effectively requires strong\norganizational and planning skills and the ability to see how individual people\nand available resources fit together to accomplish the goals of the organization. It\nrequires both that “big picture” thinking and critical analysis of little details to make\nthe market a success.\nDetermine Tasks. Before you can lead other people in the\nright\ndirection, you must know what steps need to be\ntaken to accomplish the market’s goals and objectives. You need to have a clear idea of what tasks need\nto be done and the deadlines for each job. Some will\nbe\nshort-term, one-time projects. Others will be long term and complex. Make\nlists if this helps you identify everything that must be done.\n\n13\n\n\f","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}

us.................................................................................................................102\n\n\fCABI is an international not-for-profit organization that improves people’s lives worldwide by\nproviding information and applying scientific expertise to solve problems in agriculture and the\nenvironment.\nOur approach involves putting information, skills and tools into people’s hands. CABI’s 48 member\ncountries guide and influence our work which is delivered by scientific staff based in our global\nnetwork of centres.\nCABI is a leading global publisher producing key scientific publications, including the world\nrenowned CAB Abstracts (the leading abstracting and indexing database in the applied life\nsciences), Global Health, as well as Compendia, books, eBooks and full text electronic resources.\nBehind each of our products is a team of subject specialists committed to delivering the most\nrelevant and authoritative information to researchers worldwide.\nWe also offer CAB Direct, CABI’s online development platform, providing a single point of access to\nall your CABI database subscriptions.\n\n\fPreface\nCABI’s PestSmart Diagnostic Field Guide is intended for a wide range of users around the world. It\nshould be a companion to anyone whose work or hobby requires them to associate poor plant health\nwith likely causes. This applies to those who work in the field as well as those who are laboratory\nbased.\nPlants can suffer from such a variety of problems it is not possible for a person to be an expert on\nall problems of all plants. The PestSmart Diagnostic Field Guide provides information relating to\ncommon symptoms expressed by crop plants when under attack from a wide variety of pathogens\nand pests as well as symptoms due to sub-optimal growing conditions.\nThe Field Guide assists the reader in linking the symptoms seen on plants with the potential\ncauses. Where there is more than one potential cause it provides information on how to tease the\ntwo candidates apart so as to find the problem. While this guide commonly uses the term ‘crop’\nand does focus on agricultural crops, the principles outlined can be applied to all types of plants,\nregardless of how they are grown and whether they are grown for commercial, aesthetic or other\npurposes.\nMore information on diagnosing plant health problems and methods for maintaining healthy\ncrops can be obtained through various knowledge resources that may be available, such as pest\nmanagement decision guides, factsheets and other extension materials, text books, and internet\ntools such as CABI’s Plantwise knowledge bank and Factsheet app.\nComments on CABI’s PestSmart Diagnostic Field Guide are welcome.\nTo provide feedback, please send it in an email: info@cabi.org.\n\n\fAcknowledgements\nCABI is an international intergovernmental organisation and we gratefully acknowledge the core\nfinancial support from our member countries (and lead agencies) including: Department for\nInternational Development (DfID, United Kingdom), Australian Centre for International Agricultural\nResearch (ACIAR, Australia), Directorate General International Cooperation (DGIS, Netherlands), and\nSwiss Agency for Development Cooperation (SDC, Switzerland). We also gratefully acknowledge\nthe funding provided for this work by the following agencies: EuropeAid/Development Cooperation\n(DEVCO, European Commission), the International Fund for Agricultural Development (IFAD) and Irish\nAid (Ireland).\nThe PestSmart Diagnostic Field Guide contains content that is the result of years of concept\ndevelopment and field testing, involving a number of CABI staff and partners around the world. The\ncompilation of this field guide was led by Dr Phil Taylor, with contributions by many others, both inside\nand outside of CABI, who provided content and feedback.\nThe content on the diagnosis of mineral deficiencies has been enormously improved by the use of\ninformation and illustrations from Prakash Kumar and Manoj Kumar Sharma’s Nutrient Deficiencies\nof Field Crops: Guide to Diagnosis and Management published by CABI in 2013.\nMany thanks to all who have injected energy and ideas into this publication.\nUlrich Kuhlmann\nExecutive Director, Global Operations, CABI\n\n\fINTRODUCTION\nThe first step to identifying possible solutions to a plant health problem is understanding the\nproblem correctly. CABI’s PestSmart Diagnostic Field Guide supports diagnostic decisions by\nshowing relationships between common symptoms on plants and the various possible causes.\nThis document provides images and descriptions of many typical symptoms associated with biotic\nand abiotic factors that harm plant health. Among the biotic factors, the major pest groups are\nrepresented. Throughout the Field Guide, the term ‘pests’ will refer to all animals, microorganisms\nand weeds that damage plants. The most common abiotic factors of plant health decline are also\nhighlighted. This book does not attempt to show all possible plant/disorder combinations; therefore,\nit is important to understand the different types of symptoms caused by each factor and to use that\nknowledge to make a field diagnosis.\nThe Field Guide consists of ‘ready reckoners’ (simple information tables for quick and easy\nreference) that serve as reminders of symptom–cause associations and therefore can assist in\ndiagnosing plant health problems. Many biotic and abiotic factors create very similar symptoms\nin plants, therefore a table is provided (Potential sources of confusion) that identifies these\nareas of overlap and provides methods to differentiate between the possible causes.\nThe Field Guide concludes with a short section on important points to remember when\nselecting management options for crop problems following an integrated pest management\napproach.\n\nIntroduction\n\n7\n\n\fFIELD DIAGNOSIS: A PROCESS OF\nELIMINATION\nThe process of diagnosing a plant health problem without any specialised laboratory equipment\nis called ‘field diagnosis’. Field diagnosis involves careful observation of the symptoms on a\nplant and linking those symptoms with possible causes. While some plant health problems are\nrelatively easy to diagnose, others can be very difficult for a number of reasons. For instance,\nthere may be multiple factors that cause similar symptoms, or the plant being examined may not\nshow very clear symptoms. As a result, it will often not be possible to make a highly specific field\ndiagnosis, such as giving the name of a nutrient that is deficient or the pest species causing the\nproblem. However, in many cases a very specific diagnosis is not necessary to identify corrective\nmeasures. A diagnosis of a pest group, such as fungus or stem-boring insect, will provide a lot\nof useful information for developing an adequate solution. The precision and accuracy of the\ndiagnosis ultimately depends on one’s knowledge of the plants and the known problems in the\narea, with additional support from tools like the PestSmart Diagnostic Field Guide.\nPrecise versus accurate\nThese two terms are often used interchangeably but they are different.\nA precise diagnosis is very specific, perhaps naming a pest species\n(as opposed to a pest group like virus or mite). An accurate diagnosis\nis one that is correct.\nIf someone claimed she weighed 62.64932 kg, that would be very\nprecise but hopelessly inaccurate if she actually weighed 67.5 kg. It\nis sometimes better to settle for lower precision (specificity) to ensure\nhigher accuracy. In this example about body weight, it would have\nbeen far better for the person to have said she weighed about 65 kg.\nA field diagnosis of Fusarium oxysporum race 4 would be very precise\nbut very inaccurate if the cause was nematode attack. Do not try to be\nprecise unless you can be sure you are also accurate (correct).\nThe first step in narrowing down the cause of a plant health problem is to determine if the\nsymptoms are caused by a living organism (biotic) or by a non-living factor (abiotic).\nAbiotic factors usually affect the whole plant and often (but not always) there is not a defined line\nbetween healthy and affected tissue, although exceptions will be discussed later in the section on\npotential sources of confusion (Table 6). Many abiotic causes of poor plant health are associated\nwith the soil environment (compaction, pH, nutritional deficiencies, etc.). These tend to affect all\nof the upper parts of the plant since they are all connected to the same root system. Other abiotic\nfactors, such as heat, wind, cold, hail, etc. have direct effects on the above-ground parts of the\nplant and lead to symptoms that are often distributed symmetrically within the plant and within\nindividual leaves.\n\nField Diagnosis: A Process of Elimination\n\n8\n\n\fThe huge variety of pests that attack crops gives rise to a great variety of symptoms. Where the\npest is active there may be a clear line between the healthy and the affected tissue, with more\ngeneral symptoms of stress elsewhere on the plant. Much of the remainder of this field guide\nprovides information on how to link symptoms with cause.\nOnce the cause of the crop problem has been narrowed down to either a biotic or abiotic factor,\nit will usually be possible to take the diagnosis to the next level of detail. For biotic causes this\nwould mean identifying the pest group (virus, mite, etc.), and for abiotic causes this would mean\ndetermining whether the problem is associated with water, nutrients, temperature or some other\nenvironmental factor. It may be possible to take the diagnosis even further, such as naming a\nspecific nutrient that is deficient or naming the virus, or mite, or even giving a species name.\nThe more precise a diagnosis is, the better it is because it means that a more specific and\neffective recommendation can be given. However, making a very precise diagnosis is more\ndifficult (for instance, many pest species look very similar) and the risk of making a mistake is\nhigher compared to making a general diagnosis. Whenever you are unable to provide a full\ndiagnosis to the pest species level or of the specific abiotic factor, make as precise a diagnosis\nas you can with confidence. As a diagnosis, ‘insect’ is useful but not particularly informative.\n‘Maize stem borer’ is more helpful and ‘Lepidoptera maize stem borer’ is better still; however,\navoid going further unless you know which stem borer it is because, depending on where you\nwork, it could be one of several species that are difficult to distinguish.\nDon’t be in too much of a hurry. Slow down, cut open the plant and have a look inside. Use a hand\nlens to look for fungal fruiting bodies or small insects. Remember to ALWAYS eliminate all the\nalternative causes before pronouncing your final diagnosis.\nIn many cases, a single plant sample and information may not be enough to make a diagnosis,\nin which case a visit to the production site or garden may be required.\n\nField Diagnosis: A Process of Elimination\n\n9\n\n\fExample: Field diagnosis of spots on leaves\nThis cashew leaf has leaf spots, which are clearly defined and are all a similar size.\nThis clear delineation between healthy (green) and unhealthy (tan brown) tissue indicates that there is a biotic cause. If\nthe cause were an abiotic one you would expect to see a much more general yellowing or browning of the leaf without\nthe sharp divide between healthy and unhealthy plant material.\nWe can eliminate certain biotic causes through a mixture of experience and observation because the symptoms they\ngenerally produce do not fit in with the evidence presented.\n\nRobert Reeder, CABI\n\n• This symptom is not caused by insect or mite damage; there is no evidence of chewing, no webbing seen and no\npresence of insects or mites.\n\n• This symptom is not caused by a nematode, virus or phytoplasma, as these organisms do not cause leaf spots.\n• This symptom is not caused by a mammal or parasitic plants or weeds, as there is no evidence of it having been\neaten (mammal) or of decline caused by competition by weeds or parasitisation by another plant.\n\n• This symptom could be caused by a bacterial pathogen but you would expect the spots to have a water-soaked\nmargin, to be bound between the leaf veins and generally associated with the edge of the leaf.\n\n• This symptom could be caused by a water mould but the leaf spots are not spreading aggressively (they are all of a\nsimilar size), have a clearly defined border and show no evidence of fluffy sporulation. All of these characteristics\ntogether indicate that a water mould is not the causal agent.\n\nBy the process of elimination it can be concluded that this symptom has a fungal cause. They are known to cause\nleaf spots with these characteristics. More detailed examination of the material with a hand lens reveals fungal fruiting\nbodies (arrowed), making this diagnosis definitive.\n\nField Diagnosis: A Process of Elimination\n\n10\n\n\fField visits\nFor those who advise others on managing plant health, there is often a limited amount of\ninformation available to them, especially if they are only shown a plant sample. Furthermore,\nthe sample may not contain all the important symptoms required for a diagnosis and the\nsample may have deteriorated in transit. It may be necessary to visit the field to see fresh\nsymptoms and to gain other information on the problem. If you intend to send a sample to\nanother colleague or a formal diagnostic support service, it is usually a good idea to visit the\nfield yourself and to select a fresh sample of your own.\nThe following is a summary of what to do when visiting a field to observe the symptoms in the\ncontext of the entire crop. (All of the information gathered would be of interest to a diagnostic\nsupport service if a sample were to be sent.)\nSTEP 1: GET IN CLOSE\n\n• What parts are affected?\n• Describe symptoms using the correct terminology.\n• Observe changes in shape, colour and growth.\n• Look for visible signs of insects, fungi or other pests.\nSTEP 2: LOOK AT THE WHOLE PLANT (INCLUDING ROOTS)\n• Where are the symptoms within the plant?\n• Which growth stages are affected?\n• How do the symptoms progress from early to late stages?\n• How severe is the attack?\nSTEP 3: EXAMINE GROUPS OF PLANTS\n• Incidence: how many plants are affected?\n• Distribution: random, edge of the plot only, in patches, pattern caused by use\nof machinery?\n\n• Remember: consider plant variety, age and how it is grown.\nSTEP 4: SPEAK TO FARMERS AND OTHER LOCAL EXTENSION WORKERS\n• When did the problem appear? Is this the first time?\n• Record local name for the problem.\n• Consider soil type and climate (patterns).\n• Obtain information on the varieties used, recent history of chemical inputs used, etc.\n\nField Diagnosis: A Process of Elimination\n\n11\n\n\fREADY RECKONERS FOR\nPEST IDENTIFICATION\nThe following tables (ready reckoners) provide a rapid method of linking plant symptoms with\npossible causes. In order to use the ready reckoners effectively, some experience of diagnosing\nplant health problems is required by the observer. There is one table for insect and mite pests\nand one for pathogens, and the latter is then expanded to deal with each symptom group in\nmore detail.\n\nInsect and mite pests\nThe body shape and general appearance are often sufficient to identify many insects and mites\n(Tables 1 and 2) to a group level, so no additional text is supplied in this PestSmart Diagnostic\nField Guide.\n\nPlant pathogen pests\nAs microorganisms are generally not visible, diagnosis is primarily based on symptoms (Table 3)\nhowever additional information is provided in the remainder of this PestSmart Diagnostic Field Guide.\n\nMineral deficiencies\nThis ready reckoner (Table 4) summarises the major symptoms that many plants show when short\n(deficient) of a mineral. It provides only general information and shortage of a mineral is just one\nof many causes that can produce some of these symptoms. Not all crop plants display the same\nsymptoms even when deficient in the same mineral (especially true for zinc). Some plants are\nmore susceptible to shortage of minerals than others (e.g. molybdenum shortage in brassicas).\nPhotographs of mineral deficient plants are provided in Table 5.\nIt is unlikely that the mineral deficiency will be so acute that the seedlings die (the seed carries\nlimited amounts of mineral). In most cases the shortage is chronic and the plants will survive\nbut show symptoms that can be severe or mild. Chronic symptoms generally include slow\ngrowth (possibly stunting) and poor flowering and fruit production (where relevant). Yields\ncan be affected even when symptoms are not seen. The more commonly deficient minerals\nare listed on the left of the table and the less commonly deficient ones on the right. While field\nsymptoms may be sufficient to provide an accurate diagnosis by an experienced person who\nis familiar with local soil conditions, it is usually essential to have the field diagnosis confirmed\nwith a laboratory soil analysis. This is especially important if someone is intending to spend a\nsignificant amount of money or effort on correcting the problem.\n\nReady Reckoners for Pest Identification\n\n12\n\n\fTable 1. With the increasing demands of a growing\npopulation and the critical challenges posed by climate change, effective plant disease\nmanagement has gained significant urgency. Pathogens affecting plants are diverse\nand ever-evolving, impacting yield, quality, and the resilience of crops. To address\nthese issues comprehensively, Plant Pathology and Disease Management brings\ntogether recent advancements, methodologies, and strategies for combating plant\ndiseases and safeguarding agricultural productivity.\nThis book serves as a comprehensive guide, offering insights into plant diseases\ncaused by fungi, bacteria, viruses, and nematodes, as well as abiotic stresses. We\nexplore diagnostic tools and methods for identifying plant pathogens and symptoms\nand provide detailed approaches to integrated disease management (IDM). The book\nalso delves into the potential of biological control agents, resistant crop varieties, and\ngenetic engineering in reducing crop losses. Emphasis is given to sustainable and ecofriendly practices, considering the environmental impacts of conventional methods.\nPlant Pathology and Disease Management is a valuable resource for students,\nresearchers, educators, and professionals in agriculture, botany, and related fields. We\nhope that the knowledge and perspectives offered here contribute to the advancement\nof sustainable disease management practices and inspire future research and\ninnovation.\nWe extend our deepest gratitude to the contributors, whose expertise and\ndedication have made this book possible, and to all readers who seek to strengthen the\nresilience of our agricultural systems through science and stewardship.\nEditors\n\n\fTABLE OF CONTENT\nSr. No.\n\nBook Chapter and Author(s)\n\nPage No.\n\n1.\n\nADVANCES IN PLANT DISEASE MANAGEMENT FOR PRECISION\n\n1 – 11\n\nCROP PROTECTION\nLokesh Kumar Meena and Prashant P. A. Vhanalakar\n11.\n\nFUSARIUM WILT OF TOMATO AND CONTROLLING STRATEGIES\nUSING NANOPARTICLES\nC. P. Bhagat\n\n96 – 106\n\n\f\fPlant Pathology and Disease Management\n(ISBN: 978-93-95847-89-6)\n\nADVANCES IN PLANT DISEASE MANAGEMENT FOR PRECISION CROP\nPROTECTION\nLokesh Kumar Meena*1 and Prashant P. Jambhulkar2\n1Department of Pathology,\n\nRajasthan College of Agriculture, MPUAT, Udaipur, Rajasthan, 313 001, India\n2Department of Plant Pathology,\n\nRani Laxmi Bai Central Agriculture University, Jhansi, U.P., India\n*Corresponding author E-mail: meenalokesh170694@gmail.com\nAbstract:\nPlant diseases cause severe losses to humanity in various forms, affecting food\nsecurity and livelihoods. Families may face displacement and hunger due to crop failures\ndriven by plant diseases. Managing plant diseases aims to reduce both economic and\naesthetic impacts by preventing outbreaks and targeting the most susceptible stages of the\ndisease cycle. Accurate disease diagnosis is crucial to identify pathogens effectively, while\nunderstanding the disease cycle, environmental influences, and the cultural requirements\nof the host plant helps shape successful management strategies. Integrated Disease\nManagement (IDM) generally involves systematic scouting and the timely integration of\nvarious methods and strategies. Key elements of an IDM plan include disease forecasting,\nassessing economic thresholds, and implementing precise interventions. Innovations such\nas CRISPR/Cas systems, microbiome engineering, omics technologies, advanced\nphenotyping, and cutting-edge detection tools present new opportunities to enhance\nsustainable agriculture and precision crop protection. Coordinating these advances can\nsupport the goal of resilient, sustainable crop production systems.\nKeywords: CRISPR/CAS, Disease, Diagnosis, Forecasting, IDM, Microbiome engineering,\nOmics.\nIntroduction:\nA plant disease arises when an infectious agent, or pathogen, disrupts normal plant\nfunctions, leading to persistent stress in the plant. Such diseases can compromise the\nplant’s capacity to grow, reproduce, and produce yields in multiple ways. Diagnosing plant\ndiseases can be challenging, especially when distinguishing between biotic (living)\npathogens and abiotic (non-living) factors, which both can cause similar symptoms. It’s\nessential to clearly differentiate these factors for accurate diagnosis. Even after confirming\n1\n\n\fBhumi Publishing, India\n\na disease, its impact might only be cosmetic or cause a minor yield reduction, making costly\ncontrol measures impractical. In some cases, a disease may significantly affect young plants\nbut have minimal impact on mature, well-established plants.\nPlant Disease Triangle\nThe triangle shape is often used to illustrate the progression of plant diseases,\nsymbolizing the three essential conditions required for a disease to develop. These three\nconditions, represented by each side of the triangle, are:\n•\n\nA pathogen, or disease-causing organism, such as a bacterium or fungus\n\n•\n\nA susceptible host plant\n\n•\n\nEnvironmental conditions that favor disease development\nA disease can only occur when all three of these factors are present. Another key\n\nconcept in plant pathology is the disease cycle, which outlines the life stages of a pathogen\nand the series of events that lead to disease progression. In most cases, disease can be\nmanaged effectively by disrupting the spread of the pathogen's inoculum.\nDisease Cycle\nThe following are the steps in a typical illness cycle:\n•\n\nInfectious inoculum production\n\n•\n\nThe inoculum's spread.\n\n•\n\nInoculum penetration into the host plant.\n\n•\n\nInfection of the plant that is being infected.\n\n•\n\nSecondary cycles for inoculum production.\n\n•\n\nGetting by in between planting seasons\n\nFive steps to Diagnose Plant Pathogens\nFollow these five steps to determine the most likely cause:\n•\n\nIdentify the host plant correctly.\n\n•\n\nDetermine the plant's regular behaviour.\n\n•\n\nBecome familiar with the plant's most typical issues.\n\n•\n\nDifferentiate between biotic and abiotic causes.\n\n•\n\nLook for symptoms and indicators on the plant.\nVarious methods, strategies, and techniques for disease management fall under a\n\nfew key principles of action. The two primary principles are prevention and treatment.\nPrevention involves proactive measures taken to protect plants from infection, while\ntreatment, or curative action, includes any interventions made after a plant is already\n2\n\n\fPlant Pathology and Disease Management\n(ISBN: 978-93-95847-89-6)\n\naffected by disease. H.H. Whetzel introduced four general principles for disease control:\nexclusion, eradication, protection, and immunization.\nExclusion\nH. H. Whetzel initially proposed four general concepts for disease control: exclusion,\neradication, protection, and immunization. Karnal bunt of wheat, caused by Tilletia indica,\nis a disease of quarantine significance, and numerous measures have been implemented to\nprevent its spread—yet it eventually reached the United States. Similarly, soybean rust,\ncaused by Phakopsora pachyrhizi, emerged in the southeastern United States, where\npreventive actions have been taken to limit its spread.\nEradication\nEradication involves removing a pathogen after it has been introduced to a specific\narea. This approach can be applied to fields and seed lots but is rarely effective on a large\nscale. Two major eradication programs have been attempted to date: one in the United\nStates targeting the golden nematode (Globodera rostochiensis) and the other addressing\ncitrus canker (caused by Xanthomonas axonopodis pv. citri and pv. aurantifolii). However,\nneither program achieved complete success.\nEfforts to eradicate the golden nematode included removing infested soil,\nfumigating fields, and repurposing land previously used for potato crops. For citrus canker,\ndiseased trees were removed and burned en masse, with entire citrus farms and nurseries\nsometimes destroyed. Smaller-scale eradication techniques include pruning branches\ninfected with blister rust on white pines (caused by Cronartium ribicola) or fire blight on\napple and pear trees (caused by Erwinia amylovora). This approach may also involve\nremoving infected flower bulbs, corms, or rhizomes and sorting them carefully. Seeds are\noften treated with hot water to eliminate smut mycelium, and fruit is heated to kill viruses.\nWheat stem rust and white pine blister rust have been managed by completely eradicating\nbarberry and Ribes plants, respectively.\nProtection\nThis method requires isolating the pathogen from the host plant or its susceptible\nparts. The most commonly used approach involves applying chemicals, such as fungicides,\nbactericides, or nematicides. Fungicides have been in use for nearly a century, with\ncontinuous advancements bringing new products to the market. The Bordeaux mixture, a\ncopper sulfate-based fungicide, was the first widely used fungicide and remains in use in\nvarious forms today. Early fungicides were inorganic, composed of simple substances like\n3\n\n\fBhumi Publishing, India\n\nsulfur or metallic compounds of copper or mercury. In the early to mid-20th century,\norganic fungicides like thiram, captan, and bisdithiocarbamates were developed.\nThe first \"systemic\" fungicides emerged in the 1960s, followed by newer classes like\nstrobilurins, which also possess systemic properties. Biological control—using one living\norganism to manage another—has recently gained significant attention as an alternative\nmanagement strategy.\nTable 1: Components of Integrated Disease Management\n\nResistance\nUsing disease-resistant plants is an ideal strategy for managing plant diseases,\nprovided that resilient varieties with suitable quality, adaptability to changing\nenvironmental conditions, and lasting tolerance are available. Researchers are also\nexploring chemicals known as \"plant activators,\" which stimulate systemic acquired\nresistance (SAR) and induced resistance (IR) in plants. Genetic modification has recently\nenabled the development of crops resistant to specific pathogens, such as varieties\nresistant to papaya ring spot virus. Crops that endure environmental stress often possess\ngenetic traits for tolerance, which can be directly utilized or bred into new resistant plants\nwith desirable characteristics. For instance, genes from the bacterium Bacillus thuringiensis\nhave been introduced into plants to provide resistance against insect pests. These\ngenetically modified organisms (GMOs), containing introduced genes, have sparked\nconcerns that unintended traits—such as allergens—could potentially transfer to future\nplant generations.\n\n4\n\n\fPlant Pathology and Disease Management\n(ISBN: 978-93-95847-89-6)\n\nCrop Diseases Caused by Bacteria\nBacterial crop illnesses are one of the most common infections in agriculture. In this\nregard, preventing and controlling this sort of stress is tough. The causal agent must enter\nthe tissue to infect the culture. The main reasons are damaged regions, those were create\nby farm instruments, parasite (fleas), or easily unfavourable circumstances (Sand, storm,\nand torrential rain Bacteria also can enter plants via natural pores or glands (for example,\nwhich secrete nectar). Another characteristic of bacterial crop diseases is the causal\norganisms can lay latent in a plant or soil for an extended period of time until favourable\nconditions arise. For starters, extreme temperature swings and high humidity levels\npromote microbial action.\nControl Measures\nControlling bacterial infections in crop plants is challenging due to their rapid\nspread and the difficulty of reaching bacteria protected within plant tissues. Preventative\nmeasures include using pathogen-free seeds grown in dry regions, treating seeds with hot\nwater, soil solarization, and applying germicidal chemicals to seeds to manage plant\ndiseases. Spraying can also help prevent crop infections. Additionally, good sanitation\npractices are essential, including controlling weeds, sterilizing tools, properly disposing of\nwaste, and avoiding fieldwork when leaves are wet.\nCrop Diseases Caused by Fungi\nPathogenic fungi are among the most prevalent agricultural challenges, causing\nsignificant losses by damaging roughly a third of all food crops annually. This presents a\nserious concern both economically and in terms of food security. Similar to bacterial\ndiseases, fungal infections commonly enter plants through wounds, stomata, and water\npores. Fungal spores are often spread through wind, further facilitating their transmission.\nControl Measures\nCrop plant diseases caused by fungi have a wide range of prevention methods. Plant\nmatter that contains hazardous fungi should be eliminated, healthy seeds should be used,\ncrop rotation should be performed on a regular basis, and chemical and biological\nfungicides should be employed.\nCrop Diseases Caused by Virus\nViruses and viroids are among the smallest yet most significant threats to plants\n(subviral infectious agents). Once a plant is infected, it is nearly impossible to save it,\nmaking the impact of these plant diseases on global crop production a major concern. In\n5\n\n\fBhumi Publishing, India\n\nmost cases, viruses spread when healthy plants come into contact with infected ones. They\ncan also be transmitted through vegetative propagation, seeds, pollen, and insects.\nHowever, viruses are most commonly spread through the soil.\nControl of Crop Diseases Caused by Viruses\nMost virus-caused vegetable crop disease, unlike earlier illnesses, is extremely\ndifficult, if not impossible, to eliminate. As a result, control should be as effective as\npossible in this case. Some of the most common measures are the cultivation of resistant\ncrops, indexing, and identifying the absence or presence of virus that is not mechanically\ntransmitted. The plant under research is grafted onto an indicator plant that exhibits\nsimilar symptoms in the presence of the virus. Unfortunately, infected cultures must\noccasionally be destroyed, and quarantine may be essential in large agricultural areas to\nsave the remaining crop output.\nTable 2: Disease Management Strategies against Different Crops\n\nCrop Diseases Caused by Parasitic Plants\nCommon Parasitic Plants\nMistletoe (Viscaceae), Cuscuta spp., Orobanche spp., Striga spp.\nMethods of Controlling Crop Diseases Caused By Parasitic Plants\nPest management can be accomplished in a number of ways. One of these is\n\"suicidal germination,\" which entails nourishing the soil in order to make life easier for\nparasites. If the germinated seeds do not have a host, they will die. Herbicides are also used\non resistant crops to keep parasites from attaching to them. However, these preparations\nare rather costly. As a result, non-host crop rotation and human weeding are frequently\nrequired. The most effective strategy, however, is to use naturally sustainable plants.\n\n6\n\n\fPlant Pathology and Disease Management\n(ISBN: 978-93-95847-89-6)\n\nRecent Advances in Management of Plant Diseases\nA. CRISPR/Cas\nRecent advances in genome editing technologies have created new possibilities for\nenhancing crop resistance. The CRISPR/Cas system has been applied to address various\nagricultural challenges, including improving resistance to biotic stresses. CRISPR/Cas\ntechnologies have primarily focused on combating viral infections, with subsequent efforts\naimed at enhancing resistance to fungal and bacterial diseases. There are several\napproaches for using the CRISPR/Cas system to study and improve plant disease\nresistance, such as: (i) knocking out genes that encode susceptibility factors (e.g., MLO, a\nmildew resistance locus O), (ii) modifying or deleting cis-elements in promoters, (iii) using\nhomology-directed repair (HDR) to introduce specific mutations in coding regions, (iv)\naltering amino acids in plant surface receptor proteins to block pathogen effectors (e.g.,\nAtBAK1), (v) knocking out negative regulators of plant defense (e.g., TcNPR3), and (vi)\nmodifying central regulators of defense responses (e.g., BnWRKY70).\nFungal resistance\nBread wheat plants with CRISPR/Cas9-induced mutations in one of the three MLO\nhomeoalleles (TaMLO-A1) exhibited enhanced resistance to Blumeria graminis f. sp. tritici\ninfection, reinforcing the crucial role of TaMLO genes in powdery mildew resistance. In\ntomatoes, the SlMlo1 gene, identified as the most significant of 16 SlMlo genes, was\ntargeted at two specific sites, resulting in a 48-bp deletion. This new non-transgenic tomato\nvariety, \"Tomelo,\" demonstrated complete resistance to Oidium neolycopersici.\nBacterial Resistance\nRice has been genetically modified using CRISPR/Cas9 to develop resistance to\nbacterial blight caused by the Xanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae. The OsSWEET13 gene, which\nencodes a sucrose transporter, is a susceptibility gene involved in the plant-pathogen\ninteraction. The effector protein PthXo2, produced by X. oryzae, triggers the expression of\nOsSWEET13 in the host, making it susceptible to infection. In previous studies using the\nTALEN method to mutate the OsSWEET14 promoter, the loss of this gene prevented the X.\noryzae effector from binding to OsSWEET14, resulting in enhanced resistance to the\ndisease.\nViral resistance\nThe first two investigations examined 43 putative sgRNA/Cas9 target sites in coding\nand non-coding sections of the BSCTV genome, focusing on resistance to gemini viruses.\n7\n\n\fBhumi Publishing, India\n\nB. Microbiome engineering\n“Microbiome” is a combination of word microbe and biome which means different\nmicroorganisams living in particular habitat in symbiosis, parasitism etc. with their theater\nof activity. Based on synthetic biology is increasingly recognized as a way to give host\nplants PGP advantages. This approach allows laboratory selection of microbes according to\ntheir ability to colonize plants, specifically on the basis of how well they can deliver PGP\nadvantages. Researchers could potentially deliver these microbes to specific plant species\nand locations (e.g., roots, leaves) at different growth and developmental stages under\nvarious environmental conditions.\nAdvantages\nComplex synthetic communities create more stable metabolic networks and can\nreplace functional species as environmental conditions change. For instance, consortia\ncomposed of Revibacillus fluminis, Brevibacillus agri, and Bacillus paralicheniformis have\nbeen found to be highly salt-tolerant in the rhizosphere of several solanaceous crops. In\nbiocontrol, the deletion of a virulence factor from Burkholderia ambifaria enhanced its\neffectiveness as a biocontrol agent.\nAs biofertilizers, synthetic genetic sensors have been developed to replace the\nregulatory control of nif transcription, allowing plants to respond to root exudates, soil\nbacteria, and agricultural biocontrol agents. As biostimulants, integrating a quorumsensing (QS) circuit with hormone synthesis pathways has led to the production of indole3-acetic acid (IAA) in Cupriavidus pinatubonensis V (Zuniga). Additionally, the enzyme 1Aminocyclopropane-1-carboxylate (ACC) deaminase, expressed in banana endophytes,\npromotes banana plant growth by catalyzing the precursor of ethylene.\nC.
