{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:5","agent_id":"adbfae2f6","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:4","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"PAT 301 Diseases of Field and Horticultural crops and their management-I (1+1)\nTheory\nEtiology, symptoms, mode of spread, survival, epidemiology and integrated\nmanagement of important diseases\nUnit-I Diseases of cereals and Millets\nCereals: rice and maize; Millets: sorghum, bajra, finger millet and small millets\nUnit- II Diseases of Pulses and Oilseeds\nPulses: pigeon pea, urd bean, mung bean, soyabean, cowpea; Oilseeds: ground nut,\ncastor and Sesame\nUnit- III Diseases of Cash crops: tobacco, jute and mulberry\nUnit- IV Diseases of Fruits and vegetables crops\nFruits: banana, guava, papaya, pomegranate; Vegetables: tomato, brinjal, okra ,\ncruciferous vegetables, beans, colacasia and sweet potato\nUnit- V Diseases of Plantation crops\nPlantation: coconut, arecanut, tea, coffee, rubber and cocoa\nPractical\nStudy of symptoms and host parasite relationship of rice, maize, sorghum, bajra,\nfinger millet ,small millets, pigeon pea, urd bean, mung bean, soyabean, cowpea, ground\nnut, castor Sesame, tobacco, jute , mulberry, banana, guava, papaya, pomegranate, tomato,\nbrinjal, okra , cruciferous vegetables, beans, colacasia , sweet potato, coconut, arecanut,\ntea, coffee, rubber and cocoa\nTheory\nEtiology, symptoms, mode of spread, survival, epidemiology and integrated\nmanagement of\n1. Diseases of rice\n2. Diseases of maize and sorghum\n3. Diseases of pearl millet, finger millet and small millets,\n4. Diseases of pigeonpea, urdbean, mungbean, soybean and cowpea\n5. Diseases of groundnu , sesame and castor\n6. Diseases of tobacco, jute and mulberry\n7. Diseases of banana\n8. Diseases of guava, papaya and pomegranate\n9. Mid semester examination\n10. Diseases of tomato\n11. Diseases of brinjal and okra\n12. Diseases of crucifers\n13. Diseases of sweet potato and beans\n14. Diseases of coconut and arecanut\n15. Diseases of tea\n16. Diseases of coffee\n17. Diseases of rubber and cocoa\nPractical\nStudy of symptoms and host-parasite relationship of:\n1. Diseases of rice\n2. Diseases of maize and sorghum\n3. Diseases of pearl millet, finger millet and small millets,\n4. Diseases of pigeonpea, urdbean, mungbean, soybean and cowpea\n\n\f5. Diseases of groundnut , sesame and castor\n6. Diseases of tobacco, jute and mulberry\n7. Diseases of banana\n8. Diseases of guava, papaya and pomegranate\n9. Diseases of crucifers\n10. Field visit/ exposure visit to hilly fruits , vegetables and plantation crops\n11. Diseases of tomato, brinjal and okra\n12. Diseases of sweet potato and beans\n13. Diseases of coconut and arecanut\n14. Diseases of tea\n15. Diseases of coffee\n16. Diseases of rubber and cocoa\n17. Final practical examination\nReference Books\n1. Arjunan.G. Karthikeyan, G, Dinakaran ,D. Raguchander,T. 1999 Diseases of Horticultural\nCrops, AE Publications, Coimbatore.\n2. Rangasawmi ,G and Mahadevan, A. 1998. Diseases of crop Plants in India, Prentice Hall\nof India Pvt. Ltd., New Delhi\n3. Prakasam, V., Valluvaparidasan, V., Raguchander, T. and K.Prabakar. 1997. Field crop\ndiseases, AE Publication, Coimbatore.\nE- Books\n1. Agrios, G.N. 2008. Plant Pathology, Academic Press, New York.\n2. Rangaswami, G. 2005. Diseases of Crop plants in India. Prentice Hall of India Pvt. Ltd.,\nNew Delhi.\nThakur, B.R. 2006. Diseases of field crops and their management\n\n\fDISEASES OF RICE\nRICE (Oryza sativa)\n1. Blast\nPyricularia oryzae (Syn : P. grisea)\n(Sexual stage : Magnaporthe grisea)\nSymptoms\nThe fungus attacks the crop at all stages from seedlings in nursery to heading in\nmain field. The typical symptoms appear on leaves, leaf sheath, rachis, nodes and even the\nglumes are also attacked. On the leaves, the lesions start as small water soaked blusih\ngreen flecks, soon enlarge and form characteristic spindle shaped spots with grey centre\nand dark brown margin (Leaf blast). The spots join together as the disease progresses\nand large areas of the leaves dry up and wither. Similar spots are also formed on the sheath.\nSeverely infected nursery and field show a burnt appearance. In infected nodes, irregular\nblack areas encircle the nodes can be noticed. The affected nodes may break up and all the\nplant parts above the infected nodes may die (Node blast). At the flower emergence, the\nfungus attacks the peduncle which is engirdled, and the lesion turns to brownish-black. This\nstage of infection is commonly referred to as rotten neck/neck rot/neck blast/panicle blast. In\nearly neck infection, grain filling does not occur and the panicle remains erect like a dead\nheart caused by a stem borer. In the late infection, partial grain filling occurs. Small brown to\nblack spots also may be observed on glumes of the heavily infected panicles. The pathogen\ncaused by yield losses ranged from 30-61 per cent depending upon the stages of infection.\nPathogen\nMycelium of the fungus, is hyaline to olivaceous, septate and highly branched.\nConidia are produced in clusters on long septate, olivaceous slender conidiophores.\nConidia are pyriform to ellipsoid, attached at the broader base by a hilum. Conidia are\nhyaline to pale olive green, usually 3 celled. The perfect state of the fungus is M. grisea. It\nproduces perithecia. The ascospores are hyaline, fusiform, 4 celled and slightly curved.\nFavourable Conditions\nApplication of excessive doses of nitrogenous fertilizers, intermittent drizzles,\ncloudy weather, high relative humidity (93-99 per cent), low night temperature\n(between 15-20°C or less than 26°C), more number of rainy days, longer duration of dew,\ncloudy weather, slow wind movement and availability of collateral hosts.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nThe disease spreads primarily through airborne conidia since spores of the fungus\npresent throughout the year. Mycelium and conidia in the infected straw and seeds are\nimportant sources of inoculum. Irrigation water may carry the conidia to different fields. The\n\n\ffungus also survives on collateral hosts viz., Panicum repens, Digitaria magrginata,\nBrachiaria mutica, Leersia hexandra, Dinebra retroflea, Echinochloa crusgalli and\nStenotaphrum secondatum.\nFore casting\nForecasting blast of rice can be made on the basis of minimum night temperature\nrange of 20-26OC in association with a high relative humidity range of 90 per cent and\nabove lasting for a period of a week or more during any of the three susceptible phases of\ncrop growth, viz., seedling stage, post transplanting tillering stage and neck emergence\nstage. In Japan, the first leaf blast model was developed and named as BLAST. Later based\non different field experiments various models were developed namely, PYRICULARIA,\nPYRIVIEW, BLASTAM, EPIBLA and P BLAST.\nManagement\nGrow resistant varieties like Ponmani, ADT40, Co25 in Samba and Co37, Co43,\nIR20, Co44, ADT36 and ADT39 in Thaladi seasons. The varieties viz. Co45, ADT37,\nVikas, Jaya, IR62, IR64 and ASD 18 also showed moderate resistance to blast. Avoid\ncultivation of highly susceptible varieties viz., IR50 and TKM6 and susceptible varieties viz.,\nPonni, White Ponni and IR36 in disease favourable season. Remove and destory the weed\nhosts in the field bunds and channels. Treat the seeds with Captan or Thiram or\nCarbendazim or Carboxin or Tricyclazole at 2 g/kg. Seed treatment with biocontrol agent\nTrichoderma viride@ 4g/kg or Pseudomonas fluorescens @ 10g/kg of seed. Avoid close\nspacing of seedlings in the main field. Spray the nursery with Carbendazim 25 g or\nEdifenphos 25 ml for 8 cent nursery. Spray the main field with Edifenphos 250 ml or\nIprobenphos 500 ml or Carbendazim 250 g or Tricyclazole 400 g or Thiophanate Methyl 500\ng or Pyroquilon 500 g/ha.\n2. Brown Spot or Sesame leaf spot\n\nHelminthosporium oryzae (Syn : Drechslera oryzae)\n(Sexual stage : Cochliobolus miyabeanus)\nSymptoms\nThe fungus attacks the crop from seedling in nursery to milk stage in main field.\nSymptoms appear as lesions (spots) on the coleoptile, leaf blade, leaf sheath, and glume,\nbeing most prominent on the leaf blade and glumes. The disease appears first as minute\nbrown dots, later becoming cylindrical or oval to circular. The several spots coalesce\nand the leaf dries up. The seedlings die and affected nurseries can be often recognised from\na distance by their brownish scorched appearance. Dark brown or black spots also appear\n\n\fon glumes which contain large number of conidiophores and conidia of the fungus. It causes\nfailure of seed germination, seedling mortality and reduces the grain quality and weight.\nPathogen\nH. oryzae produces greyish-brown to dark brown septate mycelium. Conidiophores\nmay arise singly or in small groups. They are straight, sometime geniculate, pale to brown in\ncolour. Conidia are usually curved with a bulge in the centre and tapering towards the\nends occasionally almost straight, pale olive green to golden brown colour and are 614 septate. The perfect stage of the fungus is C. miyabeanus. It produces perithecia with\nasci containing 6-15 septate, filamentous or long cylinderical, hyaline to pale olive green\nascospores. It produces C25 terpenoid phytotoxins called ophiobolin A, (or Cochliobolin\nA), ophiobolin B (or cochliobolin B) and ophiobolin I. Ophiobolin A is most toxic.\nThese breakdown the protein fragment of cell wall resulting in partial disruption of integrity of\ncell.\nFavourable Conditions\nTemperature of 25-30°C with relative humidity above 80 per cent are highly\nfavourable. Excess of nitrogen aggravates the disease incidence.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nThe infected seeds are the most common source of primary infection. The conidia\npresent on infected grain and mycelium in the infected tissue may viable for 2 to 3 years.\nThe fungus may survive in the soil for 28 months at 30°C and 5 months at 35°C. Airborne\nconidia infect the plants both in nursery and in main field. Maximum flight of conidia takes\nplace at a wind velocity of 4.0 - 8.8 hr. Minimum temperature of 27 -28°C, Relative humidity\nof 90-99% and rainfall of 0.4 -14.4 mm favoured the dispersal of the conidia to maximum\nextent. The fungus also survives on collateral hosts like Leersia hexandra, Arundo donux,\nand Echinochlora colonum.\nManagement\nField sanitation-removal of collateral hosts and infected debris in the field. Crop\nrotation, adjustment of planting time and proper fertilization are suggested. Use of slow\nrelease nitrogenous fertilizers is advisable. Grow disease tolerant varieties viz., Co44,\nCauvery, Bala Bhavani. Use disease free seeds. Treat the seeds with Thiram or Captan at\n4 g/kg. Spray the nursery with Edifenphos 40 ml or Mancozeb 80 g or Captafol 40 g for 8\ncents nursery. Spray the crop in the main field with Edifenphos 500 ml or Mancozeb 1 kg or\nCaptafol 625 g/ha.\n3. Narrow brown leaf spot\nCercospora janseana\n(Sexual stage : Sphaerulina oryzina)\n\n\fSymptoms\nThe fungus produces short, linear brown spots mostly on leaves and also on\nsheaths, pedicels and glumes. The spots appear in large numbers during later stages of\ncrop growth.\nPathogen\nConidiophores are produced in small groups and brown in colour. Conidia are hyaline\nor sub hyaline, cylindrical and 3-5 septate.\nManagement\nSpray Carbendazim 250 g or Mancozeb 1 kg/ha.\n4. Sheath rot\nSarocladium oryzae\n(Syn : Acrocylindrium oryzae)\nSymptoms\nInitial symptoms are noticed only on the upper most leaf sheath enclosing young\npanicles. The flag leaf sheath show oblong or irregular greyish brown spots. They enlarge\nand develop grey centre and brown margins covering major portions of the leaf\nsheath. The young panicles may remain within the sheath or emerge partially. The panicles\nrot and abundant whitish powdery fungal growth is formed inside the leaf sheath.\nPathogen\nThe fungus produces whitish, sparsely branched and septate mycelium. Condiophore\nis slightly thicker than the vegetative hyphae. Conidia are hyaline, smooth, single celled and\ncylindrical in shape.\nFavourable Conditions\nCloser planting, high doses of nitrogen, high humidity and temperature around 2530°C.Injuries made by leaf folder, brown plant hopper and mites increase infection.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nMainly through air-borne conidia and also seed-borne.\nManagement\nApply\n\nrecommended doses of fertilizers. Adopt\n\noptimum\n\nspacing.\n\nSpray\n\nCarbendazim 250g or Edifenphos 1 lit or mancozeb 1 kg or Chlorothalonil 1 kg/ha at boot\nleaf stage and 15 days later. Soil application of gypsum in 2 equal splits (500 kg/ha) reduce\nthe sheath rot incidence.\n\n\f5. Sheath blight\nRhizoctonia solani\n(Sexual stage : Thanetophorus cucumeris)\nSymptoms\nThe fungus affects the crop from tillering to heading stage. Initial symptoms are\nnoticed on leaf sheaths near water level. On the leaf sheath oval or elliptical or irregular\ngreenish grey spots are formed. As the spots enlarge, the centre becomes greyish white with\nan irregular blackish brown or purple brown border. Lesions on the upper parts of plants\nextend rapidly coalesing with each other to cover entire tillers from the water line to\nthe flag leaf. The presence of several large lesions on a leaf sheath usually causes death of\nthe whole leaf, and in severe cases all the leaves of a plant may be blighted in this way. The\ninfection extends to the inner sheaths resulting in death of the entire plant. Older plants are\nhighly susceptible. Five to six week old leaf sheaths are highly susceptible. Plants heavily\ninfected in the early heading and grain filling growth stages produce poorly filled grain,\nespecially in the lower part of the panicle.\nPathogen\nThe fungus produces usually long cells of septate mycelium which are hyaline when young,\nyellowish brown when old. It produces large number of globoses sclerotia, which are initially\nwhite, later turn to brown or purplish brown.\nFavourable Conditions\nHigh relative humidity (96-97 per cent), high temperature (30-32°C), closer planting\nand heavy doses of nitrogenous fertilizers.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nThe pathogen can survive as sclerotia or mycelium in dry soil for about 20 months\nbut for 5-8 months in moist soil. It infects more than 188 crop species in 32 families.\nSclerotia spread through irrigation water.\nManagement\nAvoid excess doses of fertilizers. Adopt optimum spacing. Eliminate weed hosts.\nApply organic amendments. Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected fields to healthy\nfields. Deep ploughing in summer and burning of stubbles. Spray Carbendazim 250 g or\nChlorothalonil 1 kg or Edifenphos 1 lit/ha. Seed treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens @\nof 10g/kg of seed followed by seedling dip @ of 2.5 kg or products/ha dissolved in 100 litres\nand dipping for 30 minutes. Soil application of P.fluorescens @ of 2.5 kg/ha after 30 days of\ntransplanting (This product should be mixed with 50 kg of FYM/Sand and then applied. Foliar\nspray at 0.2% concentration commencing from 45 days after transplanting at 10 days\ninterval for 3 times depending upon the intensity of disease.\n6. Grain discolouration\nDrechslera oryzae, D. rostratum, D.tetramera, Curvularia lunata, Trichoconis\npadwickii, Sarocladium oryzae, Alternaria tenuis, Fusarium moniliforme, Cladosporium\nherbarum, Epicoccum purpurascens, Cephalosporium sp., Phoma sp., Nigrospora sp.\n\n\fSymptoms\nThe grains may be infected by various organisms before or after harvesting causing\ndiscolouration, the extend of which varies according to season and locality. The infection\nmay be external or internal causing discoloration of the glumes or kernels or both. Dark\nbrown or black spots appear on the grains. The discolouration may be red, yellow, orange,\npink or black, depending upon the organism involved and the degree of infection. This\ndisease is responsible for quantitative and qualitative losses of grains.\nFavourable Conditions\nHigh humidity and cloudy weather during heading stage.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nThe disease spreads mainly through air-borne conidia and the fungus survives as\nparasite and saprophyte in the infected grains, plant debris and also on other crop debris.\nManagement\nPre and post-harvest measures should be taken into account for prevention of grain\ndiscolouration. Spray the crop at bootleaf stage with Mancozeb 1 kg or Iprobenphos 500 ml\nor Carbendazim 250 g/ha. Store the grains with 13.5-14% moisture content.\n\n7. False smut\nUstilaginoidea virens\n(Syn : Claviceps oryzae - sativa)\nSymptoms\nThe fungus transforms individual grains into greenish spore balls of velvetty\nappearance. Due to the development of the fructification of the pathogen, the ovaries are\ntransformed into large velvetty green masses. Usually only a few spikelets in a panicle are\naffected.\nPathogen\nChlamydospores are formed on the sporeballs, they are spherical to elliptical, waxy\nand olivaceous.\nFavourable conditions\nRainfall and cloudy weather during the flowering and maturity periods are favourable.\n8. Udbatta disease\nEphelis oryzae\n(Sexual stage : Balansia oryzae-sativa)\nSymptoms\nThe symptoms are evident at the time of panicle emergence. The infected panicle\ninside the sheath is matted together by the mycelium. The entire ear head is converted\ninto a straight compact cylindrical dark spike like structure. The spikelets are cemented\n\n\fto the central rachis and the size is remarkeably reduced. No grain is formed. The entire\nspike is covered by greyish stroma and convex pycnidia are immersed in the stroma.\nPathogen\nPycnidiospores are hyaline, needle shaped and 4-5 celled.\nManagement\nHot water seed treatment at 45OC for 10 min. effectively controls the disease.\nRemoval of collateral hosts like Isachne elegans, Eragrostis tenuifolia and Cynadon\ndactylon.\n9. Stack burn disease\nTrichoconis padwickii\n(Syn : Alternaria padwickii)\nSymptoms\nLeaves and ripening grains are affected. In the leaves, circular to oval spots with dark\nbrown margins are formed. The central portion of the spot turns to light brown or almost\nwhite and has numerous minute dots. On the glumes reddish brown or pale brown spots\nappear. The kernels may shrivel and become brittle when severe spotting occurs.\nPathogen\nConidia are elongately fusoid, long beak at the tip, 3 to 5 septate, thick walled and\nconstricted at the septa.\nManagement\nTreat the seeds with Thiram or Captan or Mancozeb at g/kg. Hot water treatment at\n54°C for 15 minutes is also effective. Burn the stubbles and straw in the field.\n\n10.\n\nBunt or Kernel Smut or black smut\nTilletia barclayana\nMinute black pustules or streaks are produced in the grains which burst open at the\n\ntime of ripening. The grains may be partially or entirely replaced by the fungal spores. The\nsorus pushes the glumes apart exposing the black mass of spores. Only a few flowers are\ninfected in an inflorescence. The fungus survives as chlamydospores for one or more years\nunder normal condition and 3 years in stored grains.\n11.\n\nStem rot\nSclerotium oryzae\n(Sexual stage : Magnaporthe salvinii)\n\nSymptoms\nSmall black lesions are formed on the outer leaf sheath and they enlarge and reach\nthe inner leaf sheath also. The affected tissues rot and abundant sclerotia are seen in the\nrotting tissues. The culm collapses and plants lodge. If the diseased tiller is opened, profuse\n\n\fmycelial growth and large number of sclerotia can be seen. The sclerotia may be seen in the\nstubbles after harvest.\nPathogen\nWhite to greyish hyphae produce smooth, spherical black and shiny sclerotia, visible\nto naked eyes as black masses.\nFavourable Conditions\nInfestation of leaf hoppers and stem borer and high doses of nitrogenous fertilizers.\nMode of Spead of Survival\nThe sclerotia survive in stubbles and straw and are carried through irrigation water.\nManagement\nUse of recommended doses of fertilizer. Deep ploughing in summer and burning of\nstubbles. Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected fields to healthy fields.\n12.\n\nFoot rot or Bakanae disease\nFusarium moniliforme\n(Sexual stage : Gibberella fujikuroi)\n\nSymptoms\nThe infected seedings in nursery are lean and lanky, much taller than healthy\nseedlings and die after some time. In the main field, the affected plants have tall lanky tillers\nand have longer internodes and aerial adventitious roots from the nodes above ground level.\nThe root system is fibrous and bushy. The plants are killed before earhead formation or they\nproduce only sterile spikelets. When the culm is split open white mycelial growth can be\nseen.\nPathogen\nFungus produces both macro and micro conidia. Micro conidia are hyaline, single\ncelled and oval in shape. Macro conidia are slightly sickle shaped, narrow at both ends and\ntwo to five celled. The fungus produces phytotoxin called fusaric acid, which is non-host\nspecific.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nThe fungus is externally seed-borne.\nManagement\nTreat the seeds with Thiram or Captan or Carbendazim at 2 g/kg.\n13 Bacterial leaf blight\n\nXanthomonas oryzae p.v. oryzae\n\nSymptoms\nThe disease is usually noticed at the time of heading but in severe cases occur\nearlier also. Seedlings in the nursery show circular, yellow spots in the margin, later enlarge,\n\n\fcoalesce and cause drying of foliage. “Kresek” symptom is seen in seedlings, 1-2 weeks\nafter transplanting. The bacterium enters through the cut wounds in the leaf tips, becomes\nsystemic and cause death of entire seedling.\nIn grown up plants water soaked, translucent lesions appear usually near the leaf margin.\nThe lesions enlarge both in length and width with a wavy margin and turn straw yellow\nwithin a few days, covering the entire leaf. As the disease progresses, the lesions cover\nthe entire leaf blade which may turn white or straw coloured. Lesions may also be seen on\nleaf sheaths in susceptible varieties. Milky or opaque dew drops containing bacterial masses\nare formed on young lesions in the early morning. They dry up on the surface leaving a white\nencrustation. The affected grains have discoloured spots surrounded by water soaked areas.\nIf the cut end of leaf is dipped in water, bacterial ooze makes the water turbid.\nPathogen\nThe bacterium is strict aerobe, gram negative, non spore forming, rod shaped\nwith size ranging from 1-2 x 0.8-1.0um with monotrichous polar flagellum of 6-8 um.\nThe bacterial cells are capsulated and are joined to form an aggregate mass. Colonies are\ncircular, convex with entire margins, whitish yellow to straw yellow later and opaque.\nFavourable Conditions\nClipping of tip of the seedling at the time of transplanting, heavy rain, heavy dew,\nflooding, deep irrigation water, severe wind, temperature of 25-30OC and application of\nexcessive nitrogen, especially late top dressing.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nThe infected seeds as a source of inoculum may not be important since the bacteria\ndecrease rapidly and die in the course of seed soaking. The pathogen survives in soil and in\nthe infected stubbles and on collateral hosts like Leersia spp. Plantago najor, Paspalum\ndictum, and Cyanodon dactylon. The pathogen spreads through irrigation water in dry\nseason and also through typhoons and rain storms.\nManagement\nBurn the stubbles. Use optimum dose of fertilizers. Avoid clipping of tip of seedling at\nthe time of transplanting. Avoid flooded conditions. Remove weed hosts. Grow resistant\ncultivars like IR 20 and TKM 6. Spray Streptomycin sulphate and tetracycline combination\n300 g + Copper oxychloride 1.25 g/ha.\n14. Bacterial leaf streak Xanthomonas campestris p.v. oryzicola\nSymptoms\nFine translucent streaks are formed on the veins and the lesions enlarge lengthwise\nand infect larger veins and turn brown. On the surface of the lesions, bacteria ooze out and\n\n\fform small yellow band-like exudates under humid conditions. In severe cases the leaves\nmay dry up.\nManagement\nSimilar to bacterial leaf blight.\n15. Tungro disease Virus\nSymptoms\nInfection occurs both in the nursery and in the main field. Plants are markedly\nstunted. Leaves show yellow to orange discoloration and interveinal chlorosis. Yellow\ndiscoloration is commonly seen in “Japonica” varieties, while “Indica” varieties show orange\ndiscoloration. Young leaves are often mottled with pale green to whitish interveinal stripes.\nThe plants may be killed if infected early. Tillering is reduced with poor root system. The\ninfected plants have few spikelets and panicles are small with discoloured grains. Tungro\ninfected plants can be chemically identified by lodine Test. Ten cm long leaf tip is cut in the\nearly morning before 6 A.M. and dipped in a solution containing 2 g lodine and 6 g\nPotassium lodide in 100 ml of water for 30 minutes. Tungro infected leaves show dark blue\nstreaks.\nPathogen\nIt is a composite disease caused by two morphologically unrelated viruses : rice\ntungro bacilliform virus (RTBV) and rice tungro spherical virus (RTSV). RTBV has a\nbacilliform capsid 130 x 30 nm made up of a single species of coat protein of MW 36 K and a\nsingle molecule of circular double - standed DNA of 8.3 KbP. RTSV has a isomatric capsid,\n30 nm in diameter comprising two to three polypeptide species and a single speices of\npolyadenylated single - standed RNA of about 10 KbP.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nTwo types of virus particles are associated with the disease. Bacilliform particles\ncause majority of the symptoms of the disease. Spherical particles help in the transmission\nof bacilliform virus by the green leaf-hoppers. If the bacilliform virus particles are alone\npresent in the rice plant they will not be transmitted by the leafhopper vector. The\nleafhoppers viz, Nephotettix virescens, N. nigropictus, N. parvus, N.malayanus and Recilia\ndorsalis transmit the virus in a non-persistent manner. There is no latent period in the vector\nand infectivity is retained for a maximum period of 6 days after acquisition of the virus.\nManagement\nSummer deep ploughing and burning of stubbles. Destory weed hosts of the virus\nand vectors. Grow disease tolerant cultivars like IR50 and Co45. Control the vectors in the\nnursery by application of carbofuran 170 g/cent, 10 days after sowing. Spray Phosphamidan\n500 ml or Fenthion 500 ml or Monocrotophos 1 lit/ha or Neem oil 3 per cent in the main field\n15 and 30 days after transplanting to control leaf hoppers.\n\n\fGrassy stunt Virus\nThe infected plants are markedly stunted and have excessive tillering and has an\nerect growth habit. The leaves become short, narrow, pale green or pale yellow and have\nnumerous small dark brown spots. On older leaves these spots spreading give a bronze\ncolour to the plants. The plants may produce a few small panicles which bear dark brown\nand unfilled grains.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nThe virus is transmitted in a persistent manner by the brown planthopper,\nNilaparvata lugens. It has a latent period of 5 to 28 days in the vector.\n16. Rice dwarf Virus\nSymptoms\nThe virus infected plants show marked stunted growth with chlorotic or whitish\nspecks on the leaves. The size of specks varies often and form interrupted streaks along the\nveins and distal part of infected leaves show diffuse yellowing. The number of tillers may be\nreduced with retarded growth. The diseased plants may survive until harvest time, remaining\nmore or less green. Plants which are infected at early stage produce no eartheads, if\nproduced, may have small unfilled grains.\nPathogen\nThe partially purified virus particle have a hexagonal outline and are 70 nm in\ndiameter with a surrounding envelope. The virus particle contains double stranded RNA or of\ndouble helical in nature.\nFavourable Conditions\nHigh population of Nephotettix cincticeps, Recllia dorsalis and N. nigropictus. The\npresence of gramineous weeds like Echinochloa crusgalli, Glyceria acutifolia and Panicum\nmiliaceaaum.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nThe virus is found to survive in the gramineous weeds. The virus is transmitted\nthrough the egg masses of leafhoppers from one generation to another. (Trans ovarial\ntransmission).\nManagement\nDestory the weed host which harbour the virus and the\n\nvectors.\n\nSpray\n\nPhosphamidon or Fenthinon 500 ml or Monocrotophos 1 lit/ha.\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}

{"jsonrpc":"2.0","method":"event","params":{"type":"SubagentEvent","payload":{"parent_tool_call_id":"functions.Agent:5","agent_id":"adbfae2f6","subagent_type":"coder","event":{"type":"ToolResult","payload":{"tool_call_id":"functions.Shell:5","return_value":{"is_error":false,"output":"COURSE MATERIAL FOR PATH 371\nDISEASES OF FIELD CROPS AND THEIR MANAGEMENT\n\nPrepared By\nDR. P. KISHORE VARMA,\nASSISTANT PROFESSOR,\nDEPARTMENT OF PLANT PATHOLOGY\nAGRICULTURAL COLLEGE,\nASWARAOPET 507 301\n\n1\n\n\fLECTURE 1\nDISEASESW OF RICE (ORYZA SATIVA)\nBlast\nPyricularia oryzae (Syn: P. grisea)\n(Sexual stage: Magnaporthe grisea)\nThe disease was first recorded in China in 1637. In Japan, it is believed to have occurred\nas early as in 1704. In Italy the disease called “brusone” was reported in 1828 and in\nUSA in 1876. The disease was first recorded from Tanjore district of Tamil Nadu in 1918.\nEconomic importance: The pathogen cause yield loss ranging from 30-61 per cent\ndepending upon the stage of infection. In severe cases, losses amounting to 70-80 per cent\nof grain yield are reported.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus attacks the crop at all stages from seedlings in nursery to heading in main\nfield. The typical symptoms appear on leaves, leaf sheath, rachis, nodes and even the\nglumes are also attacked.\n¾ Leaf blast: On the leaves, the lesions start as small water soaked bluish green specks,\nsoon enlarge and form characteristic spindle shaped spots with grey centre and dark\nbrown margin. The spots join together as the disease progresses and large areas of the\nleaves dry up and wither. Similar spots are also formed on the sheath. Severely\ninfected nursery and field show a burnt appearance.\n¾ Node blast: In infected nodes, irregular black areas that encircle the nodes can be\nnoticed. The affected nodes may break up and all the plant parts above the infected\nnodes may die (Node blast).\n¾ Neck blast: At the flower emergence, the fungus attacks the peduncle which is\nengirdled, and the lesion turns to brownish-black. This stage of infection is commonly\nreferred to as rotten neck/neck rot/neck blast/panicle blast. In early neck infection,\ngrain filling does not occur and the panicle remains erect like a dead heart caused by a\nstem borer. In the late infection, partial grain filling occurs. Small brown to black spots\nalso may be observed on glumes of the heavily infected panicles.\n\nLeaf blast\nNode blast\n3 celled conidia\nEtiology\nThe causal organism was first detected by Cavara in 1891 from Italy. Mycelium of the\nfungus, is hyaline to olivaceous, septate and highly branched. Conidia are produced in\nclusters on long septate, olivaceous slender conidiophores. Conidia are pyriform to\nobclavate or somewhat top shaped, attached at the broader base by a hilum. Conidia are\n2\n\n\fhyaline to pale olive green, usually 3 celled. The perfect state of the fungus is M. grisea.\nIt produces perithecia. The ascospores are hyaline, fusiform, 4 celled and slightly curved.\nThe pathogen produces few toxins namely, α-picolinic acid, Pyricularin and pyriculol.\nDisease cycle\nMycelium and conidia in the infected straw and seeds are important sources of primary\ninoculum. The seed borne inoculum fails to initiate the disease in the plains due to high\nsoil temperature in June. In both tropical and temperate regions, the fungus overwinters in\nstraw piles or grain. In tropics, one method of survival is through infection of collateral\nhosts such as Panicum repens, Digitaria marginata, Brachiaria mutica, Leersia\nhexandra, Dinebra retroflexa, Echinochloa crusgalli, Setaria intermedia and\nStenotaphrum secondatum. The most probable source of perennation and initiation of the\ndisease appear to be the grass hosts and early sown paddy crop. The disease cycle is short\nand most damage is caused by secondary infections. Air can carry the conidia for long\ndistances. The conidia from these sources are carried by air currents to cause secondary\nspread. Most conidia are released at night in the presence of dew or rain.\nFavourable Conditions\nApplication of excessive doses of nitrogenous fertilizers, intermittent drizzles, cloudy\nweather, high relative humidity (93-99 per cent), low night temperature (between 15-20\n0\nC or less than 26 0C), more number of rainy days, longer duration of dew, cloudy\nweather, slow wind movement and availability of collateral hosts.\nForecasting\nForecasting blast of rice can be made on the basis of minimum night temperature range of\n20-26 0C in association with a high relative humidity range of 90 per cent and above\nlasting for a period of a week or more during any of the three susceptible phases of crop\ngrowth, viz., seedling stage, post transplanting tillering stage and neck emergence stage.\nIn Japan, the first leaf blast model was developed and named as BLAST. Later based on\ndifferent field experiments various models were developed namely, PYRICULARIA,\nPYRIVIEW, BLASTAM, and P BLAST. A model to forecast the disease called “EpiBla” has been evolved in India.\nManagement\n¾ Use of seeds from a disease free crop\n¾ Grow resistant varieties like Simhapuri, Tikkana, Sriranga, Phalguna, Swarnadhan,\nSwarnamukhi, MTU 7414, MTU 9992, MTU 1005, Swathi, IR 64, IR 36, Sravani,\nJaya, Vijaya, Ratna, RP 4-14, IET 1444, IR20, TKM 6, MTU-3 & 5 and NLR 9672 &\n9674 in different tracts of Andhra Pradesh.\n¾ Remove and destroy the weed hosts in the field bunds and channels.\n¾ Split application of nitrogen and judicious application of nitrogenous fertilizers\n¾ Treat the seeds with Captan or Thiram or Carbendazim or Carboxin or Tricyclazole at\n2 g/kg.\n¾ Seed treatment with biocontrol agent Trichoderma viride@ 4g/kg or Pseudomonas\nfluorescens @ 10g/kg of seed. Avoid close spacing of seedlings in the main field.\n¾ Spray the nursery with Carbendazim 25 g or Edifenphos 25 ml for 8 cent nursery.\n¾ Spray the main field with Edifenphos@0.1% or Carbendazim@0.1% or Tricyclazole\n@0.06% or Thiophanate Methyl@0.1%.\n\n3\n\n\fBrown Spot or Sesame leaf spot or Helminthosporiose\nHelminthosporium oryzae (Syn: Drechslera oryzae)\n(Sexual stage: Cochliobolus miyabeanus)\nIn India, this disease is the principal cause of Bengal famine of 1942-43. The first report\nof the disease in India was made by Sundararaman from Madras in 1919, and now is\nreported from all of the rice growing states. Under highly favourable conditions, the\ndisease causes a reduction in yield ranging upto 90 per cent.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus attacks the crop from seedling in nursery to milk stage in main field.\nSymptoms appear as lesions (spots) on the coleoptile, leaf blade, leaf sheath, and glumes,\nbeing most prominent on the leaf blade and glumes. The disease appears first as minute\nbrown dots, later becoming cylindrical or oval to circular. The several spots coalesce and\nthe leaf dries up. The seedlings die and affected nurseries can often be recognized from a\ndistance by their brownish scorched appearance. Dark brown or black spots also appear\non glumes which contain large number of conidiophores and conidia of the fungus. It\ncauses failure of seed germination, seedling mortality and reduces the grain quality and\nweight. The disease is associated with a physiological disorder known as akiochi in\nJapan. Abnormal soil conditions (Deficiency of potassium) predispose the plants to heavy\ninfection.\n\nOval to circular spots\n\nOlive green to golden brown conidia\n\nEtiology\nH. oryzae produces greyish-brown to dark brown septate mycelium. Conidiophores may\narise singly or in small groups. They are straight, sometime geniculate, pale to brown in\ncolour. Conidia are usually curved with a bulge in the centre and tapering towards the\nends occasionally almost straight, pale olive green to golden brown colour and are 6-14\nseptate. The perfect stage of the fungus is C. miyabeanus. It produces perithecia with asci\ncontaining 6-15 septate, filamentous or long cylinderical, hyaline to pale olive green\nascospores. It produces C25 terpenoid phytotoxins called ophiobolin A, (or Cochliobolin\nA), ophiobolin B (or cochliobolin B) and ophiobolin I. Ophiobolin A is most toxic.\nThese result in the breakdown of the protein fragment of cell wall resulting in partial\ndisruption of integrity of cell.\nDisease cycle\nThe fungus overwinters mainly in the infected plant parts. It is not soil borne. The\ninfected seeds are the most common source of primary infection. Diseased seeds\n4\n\n\f(externally seed borne) may give rise to the seedling blight, the first phase of the disease.\nThe young seedlings show infection symptoms soon after germination. Pale yellowishbrown spots appear on the coleoptiles, spreading to cover the other tissues of the seedling.\nThe fungus reproduces on the spots and is disseminated by air currents. The fungus also\nsurvives on collateral hosts like Digitaria sanguinalis, Leersia hexandra, Echinochloa\ncolonum, Pennisetum typhoides, Setaria italica and Cynodon dactylon.\nThe symptoms of potassium deficiency are somewhat similar to that of brown spot\nmaking it often difficult to ascribe the symptoms to fungus attack or nutrient deficiency.\n\nFavourable Conditions\nTemperature of 25-300C with relative humidity above 80 per cent are highly favourable.\nExcess of nitrogen aggravates the disease incidence.\nManagement\n¾ Use disease free seeds.\n¾ Field sanitation-removal of collateral hosts and infected debris in the field.\n¾ Crop rotation,\n¾ Adjustment of planting time\n¾ Proper fertilization\n¾ Use of slow release nitrogenous fertilizers is advisable.\n¾ Good water management\n¾ Use of soil amendments\n¾ Grow disease tolerant varieties viz., Bala, BAM 10, IR-20, Jaya, Ratna, Tellahamsa\nand Kakatiya.\n¾ Treat the seeds with Thiram or Captan at 4 g/kg and with Mancozeb @0.3%\n5\n\n\f¾ Spray the crop in the main field twice with Mancozeb@0.2%, once after flowering and\nsecond spray at milky stage.\nSheath rot\nSarocladium oryzae\n(Syn: Acrocylindrium oryzae)\nEconomic importance\nSheath rot was first described in Taiwan in 1922. It is reported in all countries in South\nAsia. In A.P, sheath rot was found to be severe in Godavari delta, Nellore and Chittor. 10\nto 25% tillers may occasionally be infected.\nSymptoms\nSheath rot occurs usually at the booting stage of the crop. Initial symptoms are noticed\nonly on the upper most leaf sheath enclosing young panicles. The flag leaf sheath show\noblong or irregular greyish brown spots. They enlarge and develop grey centre and brown\nmargins covering major portions of the leaf sheath. The young panicles may remain\nwithin the sheath or emerge partially. The affected sheath and panicles rot and abundant\nwhitish powdery fungal growth is formed inside the leaf sheath. The grain discolours and\nshrivels.\n\nInfection on flag leaf sheath\nEtiology\nThe fungus produces whitish, sparsely branched and septate mycelium. Conidiophore is\nslightly thicker than the vegetative hyphae. Conidia are hyaline, smooth, single celled\nand cylindrical in shape.\nFavourable Conditions\nCloser planting, high doses of nitrogen, high humidity and temperature around 25-30OC.\nInjuries made by leaf folder, brown plant hopper and mites increase infection.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nMainly through air-borne conidia and also seed-borne.\nManagement\n¾ Apply recommended doses of fertilizers.\n¾ Adopt optimum spacing.\n¾ Spray twice with Carbendazim @0.1% or Benomyl@0.05% or Mancozeb@0.2% or\nChlorothalonil@0.2% at boot leaf stage and 15 days later.\n¾ Soil application of gypsum in 2 equal splits (500 kg/ha) reduce the sheath rot\nincidence.\n6\n\n\fStem rot\nSclerotium oryzae\n(Sexual stage: Leptosphaeria salvinii)\nEconomic importance\nStem rot was reported in Japan in 1910 and in India in 1913. Early reports indicated heavy\nlosses from stem rot. In India 18 to 56% loss was reported. IRRI studies show that the\nstem rot fungus is a wound parasite. Due to the damage caused by injuries the disease\nincidence initiates.\nSymptoms\nSmall black lesions are formed on the outer leaf sheath near the water line and they\nenlarge and reach the inner leaf sheath also. The affected tissues rot and abundant\nsclerotia are seen in the rotting tissues. The culm collapses and plants lodge. If the\ndiseased tiller is opened, profuse mycelial growth and large number of sclerotia can be\nseen. The sclerotia may be seen in the stubbles after harvest.\n\nBlack lesions on leaf sheath\n\nSclerotia in rotting tissue\n\nEtiology\nWhite to greyish hyphae produces smooth, spherical black and shiny sclerotia, visible to\nnaked eyes as black masses.\nDisease cycle\nIn the field, sclerotia are mostly distributed in the upper 5 to 10 cm of the soil. These\nsclerotia float on the water during ploughing, puddling, weeding and other operations.\nPropagules in contact with the leaf sheath produce appressoria and may start infection.\nInfection takes place readily in the presence of a wound. After harvest the fungus\ncontinues to grow on stubbles producing large quantities of sclerotia. Irrigation water\ncarries the sclerotia to other fields.\nFavourable Conditions\nInfestation of leaf hoppers and stem borer and high doses of nitrogenous fertilizers\naggravates the disease.\nManagement\n¾ Use recommended doses of fertilizer.\n¾ Deep ploughing in summer and burning of stubbles and infected straw\n¾ Use of resistant or non-lodging varieties (Basumati 3, Basumati 370, Mushkan 7,\nMushkan 41 and Bara 62 were found resistant to stem rot in Punjab)\n¾ Draining off the irrigation water and allow the soil to dry\n¾ Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected fields to healthy fields.\n7\n\n\fNarrow brown leaf spot\nCercospora oryzae\n(Sexual stage: Sphaerulina oryzae)\nEconomic importance\nIt is a minor disease, present in almost all rice growing countries of the world. In the year\n1953-54, 40% loss from the disease was reported in Surinam. In Asia, narrow brown leaf\nspot is important on very susceptible varieties.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus produces short, linear brown spots mostly on leaves and also on sheaths,\npedicels and glumes. The spots appear in large numbers during later stages of crop\ngrowth. It may also occur as long and about 1mm narrow, short and dark on resistant\nvarieties, but wide and light brown on susceptible ones.\n\nNarrow elongated spots\nCylindrical 3-10 septate conidia\nEtiology\nConidiophores are produced in small groups and are brown/dark at the base and pale at\nthe apex with three or more septa. Conidia are hyaline or sub hyaline, cylindrical and 3-10\nseptate.\nDisease cycle\nPrimary source of inoculum is by means of infected plant debris. Secondary spread of the\ndisease is by means of air borne conidia produced on leaves.\nManagement\n¾ Destruction of infected plant debris.\n¾ Spray Mancozeb@0.2% or Carbendazim@0.1% twice at 15 days interval starting with\ndisease appearance.\nSheath blight\nRhizoctonia solani\n(Sexual stage : Thanetophorus cucumeris)\nSymptoms\nThe fungus affects the crop from tillering to heading stage. Initial symptoms are noticed\non leaf sheaths near water level. On the leaf sheath oval or elliptical or irregular greenish\ngrey spots are formed. As the spots enlarge, the centre becomes greyish white with an\nirregular blackish brown or purple brown border. Lesions on the upper parts of plants\nextend rapidly coalesing with each other to cover entire tillers from the water line to the\nflag leaf. The presence of several large lesions on a leaf sheath usually causes death of the\nwhole leaf, and in severe cases all the leaves of a plant may be blighted in this way. The\ninfection extends to the inner sheaths resulting in death of the entire plant. Older plants\nare highly susceptible. Five to six week old leaf sheaths are highly susceptible. Plants\n8\n\n\fheavily infected in the early heading and grain filling growth stages produce poorly filled\ngrain, especially in the lower part of the panicle.\nPathogen\nThe fungus produces usually long cells of septate mycelium which are hyaline when\nyoung, yellowish brown when old. It produces large number of globose sclerotia, which\nare initially white, later turn to brown or purplish brown.\nDisease cycle\nThe pathogen can survive as sclerotia or mycelium in dry soil for about 20 months but for\n5-8 months in moist soil. It infects more than 188 crop species in 32 families. Sclerotia\nspread through irrigation water.\nFavourable Conditions\nHigh relative humidity (96-97 per cent), high temperature (30-32 0C), closer planting and\nheavy doses of nitrogenous fertilizers.\n\nManagement\n¾ Avoid excess doses of fertilizers.\n¾ Adopt optimum spacing.\n¾ Eliminate weed hosts.\n¾ Apply organic amendments.\n¾ Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected fields to healthy fields.\n¾ Deep ploughing in summer and burning of stubbles.\n¾ Grow disease tolerant varieties like Shiva (WGL 3943)\n¾ Spray Propiconazole@0.1% or Hexaconazole@0.2% or Validamycin@0.2%\n\n9\n\n\f¾ Seed treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens @ of 10g/kg of seed followed by\nseedling dip @ of 2.5 kg of product/ha dissolved in 100 litres and dipping for 30\nminutes.\n¾ Soil application of P.fluorescens @ of 2.5 kg/ha after 30 days of transplanting (This\nproduct should be mixed with 50 kg of FYM/Sand and then applied.\n¾ Foliar spray at 0.2% concentration commencing from 45 days after transplanting at 10\ndays interval for 3 times depending upon the intensity of disease.\nFalse smut\nUstilaginoidea virens\n(P.S: Claviceps oryzae - sativa)\nEconomic importance\nMost countries in Asia, Latin America and Africa have reported the presence of the\ndisease. There was severe epidemic in Burma in 1935. Its presence was believed to\nindicate a good crop year. This belief is still common in South-east Asia.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus transforms individual grains into yellow or greenish spore balls of velvety\nappearance which are small at first and 1 cm or longer at later stages. At early stages the\nspore balls are covered by a membrane which bursts with further growth. Due to the\ndevelopment of the fructification of the pathogen, the ovaries are transformed into large\nvelvety green masses. Usually only a few spikelets in a panicle are affected.\n\nYellow or greenish spore balls\nEtiology\nChlamydospores are formed on the spore balls, they are spherical to elliptical, waxy and\nolivaceous.\nDisease cycle\nIn temperate regions, the fungus survives the winter through sclerotia as well as through\nchlamydospores. Ascospores produced on the over wintered sclerotia apparently start\nprimary infection. Chlamydospores are important in secondary infection which is a major\npart of the disease cycle. Infection usually occurs at the booting stage of rice plants.\nChlamydospores are borne, but do not free them from spore ball easily because of the\npresence of sticky material.\nFavourable conditions\nRainfall and cloudy weather during the flowering and maturity periods are favourable.\nManagement\n¾ Spray copper oxychloride@0.3% or carbendazim@0.1% at panicle emergence stage\n10\n\n\fBacterial leaf blight\nXanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae\nEconomic importance\nThe disease was first observed in Japan (1884). In Indonesia, Kresek disease was\nreported to kill young seedlings completely in 1950. In India, BLB was first reported in\n1959. A severe outbreak of the disease occurred in Bihar and Uttar Pradesh in 1963. In the\ntropics the disease is usually referred as bacterial blight as it often kills entire young\nseedlings\nYield losses in severely diseased fields range from 20-30% and occasionally 50%. In\nIndia, millions of hectares are infected every year. Yield losses have been as high as 60%\nin some states and Godavari district of Andhra Pradesh which are endemic to this disease.\nTaichung Native 1 is highly susceptible.\nSymptoms\nThe bacterium induces either wilting of plants or leaf blight. Wilt syndrome known as\nKresek is seen in seedlings within 3-4 weeks after transplanting of the crop. Kresek\nresults either in the death of whole plant or wilting of only a few leaves. The bacterium\nenters through the hydathodes and cut wounds in the leaf tips, becomes systemic and\ncause death of entire seedling.\nThe disease is usually noticed at the time of heading but in severe cases occur earlier also.\nIn grown up plants water soaked, translucent lesions appear usually near the leaf margin.\nThe lesions enlarge both in length and width with a wavy margin and turn straw yellow\nwithin a few days, covering the entire leaf. As the disease progresses, the lesions cover the\nentire leaf blade which may turn white or straw coloured. Lesions may also be seen on\nleaf sheaths in susceptible varieties. Milky or opaque dew drops containing bacterial\nmasses are formed on young lesions in the early morning. They dry up on the surface\nleaving a white encrustation. The affected grains have discoloured spots surrounded by\nwater soaked areas. If the cut end of leaf is dipped in water, bacterial ooze makes the\nwater turbid.\n\nEtiology\nThe bacterium is strict aerobe, gram negative, non spore forming, rod shaped with\nmonotrichous polar flagellum of at one end. The bacterial cells are capsulated and are\njoined to form an aggregate mass. Colonies are circular, convex with entire margins,\nwhitish yellow to straw yellow and opaque. The bacterium has many strains that differ in\nability to infect rice plants. Strains in tropical countries are usually more virulent than\nthose in temperate areas like Japan.\n\n11\n\n\fDisease cycle\nThe bacterium enters the plant through water pores (hydathodes) along the edges of the\nleaf and through injuries in roots or leaves. It does not enter through stomata. BLB is\nprimarily a vascular or systemic disease. Bacterial cells move along the vascular tissues\ncausing wilting. Rain storms and typhoons help in the spread of the disease. Irrigation\nwater also carries the organism from field to field. The primary source of infection is\nthrough bacterium overwintering in seed (husk and endosperm). Bacteria may survive in\nsoil, plant stubbles and debris. The pathogen also survives on collateral hosts like Leersia\nhexandra, Leersia oryzoides, Zizania latifolia, Cyprus rotundus, Cyprus deformis,\nPhalaris arundinacea, Cyanodon dactylon, etc. The bacterial ooze serves as secondary\ninoculum and cause secondary infection.\nFavourable Conditions\nClipping of tip of the seedling at the time of transplanting, heavy rain, heavy dew,\nflooding, deep irrigation water, severe wind, temperature of 25-300C and application of\nexcessive nitrogen, especially late top dressing.\nManagement\n¾ Grow resistant cultivars like MTU 9992, Swarna, Ajaya, IR 20, IR 42, IR 50, IR 54,\nTKM 6, Mashuri, IET 4141, IET 1444, IET 2508, Chinsura Boro, etc.\n¾ Resistant donors: Tetep, Tadukan, Zenith, etc.\n¾ Affected stubbles are to be destroyed by burning or through ploughing\n¾ Judicious use of nitrogenous fertilizers\n¾ Avoid clipping of tip of seedling at the time of transplanting.\n¾ Avoid flooded conditions or drying of the field (not at the time of flowering)\n¾ Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected to healthy field\n\n12\n\n\f¾ Remove and destroy weed hosts.\n¾ Soaking seeds for 8 hrs in Agrimycin (0.025%) followed by hot water treatment for 10\nminutes at 52-54 0C eradicates the bacterium in the seed\n¾ Spray Streptocycline (250 ppm) along with copper oxychloride (0.3%)\nBacterial leaf streak\nXanthomonas campestris p.v. oryzicola\nEconomic importance\nBacterial leaf streak was first found in Phillippines in 1918. The disease is common in\ntropical Asia, but is not present in Japan or other parts of the world. In India, it is reported\nby Srivastava from U.P, MP, AP, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Orissa, Haryana and West\nBengal. IR 8, Jaya and Padma are highly susceptible to BLS.\nSymptoms\nFine translucent streaks appear between the veins of the leaf are the first symptoms. The\nlesions enlarge lengthwise and advance over larger veins laterally and turn brown. On\nvery susceptible varieties a yellow halo appears around the lesions. On the surface of the\nlesions, bacteria ooze out and form small yellow band-like exudates under humid\nconditions. In severe cases the leaves may dry up.\n\n13\n\n\fEtiology\nThe organism is short rod, about 1.2µ x 0.3 to 0.5µ, and gram negative. The strains of the\nbacteria differ in pathogenicity, the virulent strains causing longer streaks.\nDisease cycle\nThe pathogen can survive in infected seed but not in crop debris. The bacteria enter the\nleaves through stomata and wounds. It mainly infects the parenchymatic cells but does not\nenter the vascular systems. BLS is not a systemic disease. When the leaves are wet,\nexudate from infected leaf spread to other portions of the leaf and to other plants. Rain\nstorms and typhoons favour the spread of the disease.\n\nFavourable conditions\nHigh relative humidity (83-93%) or dew during morning hours for 2 to 3 hours\n\n14\n\n\fManagement\n¾ Grow resistant varieties\n¾ IR 20, Krishna and Jagannath are tolerant to BLS\n¾ Affected stubbles are to be destroyed by burning or through ploughing\n¾ Judicious use of nitrogenous fertilizers\n¾ Avoid clipping of tip of seedling at the time of transplanting.\n¾ Avoid flooded conditions or drying of the field (not at the time of flowering)\n¾ Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected to healthy field\n¾ Soak the seed in Streptocycline (250 ppm) followed by hot water treatment at 52 0C for\n30 minutes eradicates seedling infection.\n¾ Spray Streptocycline (250 ppm) along with copper oxychloride (0.3%)\n\nTungro disease\nEconomic importance\nPenyakitmerah which has been known in Malaysia since 1938 was identified as Tungro\nin 1965. The mentak disease of Indonesia is also identified as Tungro. Tungro is\ncommonly found in Bangladesh and India. In India, it is seen in states of West Bengal,\nKerala and other parts of India. Tungro is one of the most widely distributed and most\ndestructive diseases in tropical Asia. The loss was estimated during 1940 as 30% or 1.4\nmillion hectares annually. In Thailand a severe epidemic occurred in 1966 affecting more\nthan 3 lakh hectares. An outbreak of Tungro in 1971 affected hudred and thousands of\nhectares in Phillippines.\nSymptoms\nInfection occurs both in the nursery and in the main field. Plants are markedly stunted.\nStunting is more severe on susceptible varieties and slight on more resistant varieties.\nLeaves show yellow to orange discoloration and interveinal chlorosis. Yellow\ndiscoloration is commonly seen in “Japonica” varieties, while “Indica” varieties show\norange discoloration. Yellowing starts from the tip of the leaf and may extend to the lower\npart of the leaf blade. Young leaves are often mottled with pale green to whitish\ninterveinal stripes and the old leaves may have rusty streaks of various sizes. The plants\nmay be killed if infected early. Tillering is reduced with poor root system. The infected\nplants have few spikelets and panicles are small with discoloured grains.\nTungro infected plants can be chemically identified by lodine Test. Ten cm long leaf tip\nis cut in the early morning before 6 A.M. and dipped in a solution containing 2g Iodine\nand 6 g Potassium Iodide in 100 ml of water for 30 minutes. Tungro infected leaves show\ndark blue streaks.\nEtiology\nIt is a composite disease caused by two morphologically unrelated viruses: rice tungro\nbacilliform virus (RTBV) and rice tungro spherical virus (RTSV). RTBV has a\nbacilliform capsid or bullet-shaped particles (130 x 30 nm) made up of a single piece of\ncoat protein of MW 36 K and a single molecule of circular ds DNA of 8.3 KbP. RTSV\nhas a isometric capsid, 30 nm in diameter comprising two to three polypeptide pieces and\na single piece of polyadenylated ss RNA of about 10 KbP.\n\n15\n\n\fYellow or orange\ndiscolouration\n\nRTBV\n\nRTSV\n","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}

Notice how the\nedges of the canker are bulging.\n\nThe ooze of material from the wound is typical of some cankers.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nScot Nelson, University of Hawaii\n\nAn old and mostly dead canker on beech; the swollen edges of the\ncanker remain although the canker does not appear active.\n\nCankers can develop resinous lumps as the plant sap becomes\nsolid when leaves the plant.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nAnnexes\n\n94\n\n\fBORE HOLES (STEM/FRUIT)\n\nThe entry or exit hole of an insect pest, sometimes\nsurrounded by frass. It is often the laval stages that produce\nthe bore hole, but it can be the adults too.\n\nInsect bore hole in banana stem.\n\nInsect bore hole in avocado.\n\nJulien Lamontagne-Godwin, CABI\n\nEric Boa, CABI\n\nInsect bore hole in tomato.\n\nMaize shoot borer and its bore hole damage, with associated\nsecondary rot and scattered frass.\n\nJulien Lamontagne-Godwin, CABI\n\nMattthew Cock, CABI\n\nAnnexes\n\n95\n\n\fGALLS/SWELLINGS\n\nThe plant material grows in an abnormal way, often as a\nswelling, for the benefit of the pest.\n\nGalls on roots, the roots are swollen and misshapen.\n\nGalls on woody twigs. These galls are growing in a disordered\nfashion and are not determinate.\n\nEric Boa, CABI\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nGalls on the underside of tree leaves.\n\nInsect gall on thistle, note that the gall is smooth.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nInsect galls on oak. Note that they are all precisely the same size.\n\nSpectacular galls on crack willow.\n\nRobert Reeder, CABI\n\nRobert Reeder, CABI\n\nAnnexes\n\n96\n\n\fROT\n\nThe tissue has to become soft and slimy (in addition to\nbrown) to be considered a true rot.\n\nThis lettuce is rotting rapidly, the leaves have lost all structure and\nare falling apart.\n\nFruit is especially prone to rotting as it is often sweet and soft. This\nmango is rotting from one end and will soon consume the whole\nfruit.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nRot on the tuber of Jerusalem artichoke.\n\nClassic rot on cacao; the rot is spreading up from the base of the\npod (surface growth is also visible on the outside of the fruit in the\nblackened area).\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nAnnexes\n\n97\n\n\fSTAINING\n\nThis usually refers to streaks that are only visible once the stem\nhas been split; make sure you compare it with a healthy stem.\nThe insides of a tuber can be discoloured even though the\nmaterial is not rotting. The discolouration on the outside of a fruit\ndue to rotting or surface growth is not considered staining.\n\nInternal staining within a banana stem split lengthways.\n\nInternal staining within a banana stem cut crossways.\n\nJulien Lamontagne-Godwin, CABI\n\nEric Boa, CABI\n\nThe bark has been removed on this cinchona tree to reveal the\ninternal staining beneath.\n\nInternal staining within a radish. Depending on whether the material\nhad softened this could also be considered as rot.\n\nJulien Lamontagne-Godwin, CABI\n\nEric Boa, CABI\n\nBrown flecks on the cut surface of a capsicum stem.\n\nInternal staining of the stem is commonly associated with\npathogens that cause plants to wilt.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nCABI\n\nAnnexes\n\n98\n\n\fDRYING\n\nSevere localised stress, often associated with dieback.\n\nOn one small branch of this pear, the leaves have dried and\nshrivelled. The remaining leaves appear unaffected.\n\nSome branches on this coffee have dried. The whole plant is\nwilting but there are some areas where the material has dried.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nRobert Reeder, CABI\n\nThis eggplant has dried, the leaves have all shrivelled and turned\nbrown, and the plant is dead or dying.\n\nThese cashews have not developed and have dried out but have\nremained hard and have not rotted.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nRob Reeder, CABI\n\nComplete drying of a coffee plant.\n\nDrying of isolated branches is a common symptom on mango.\n\nRobert Reeder, CABI\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nAnnexes\n\n99\n\n\fFRASS\n\nMany insect pests (larvae and adults) leave excreta (faeces)\non the surface of the plant; it may indicate the type of insect\nattacking the plant even if the insect is absent.\n\nBall shaped soft pellets of frass are typical of many types of\ncaterpillar.\n\nTiny black dots (some of which are ringed) of sticky material are\ntypical of the frass of thrips, often seen alongside the white areas of\nthrips feeding.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nRobert Reeder, CABI\n\nSmall white pellets deposited on the surface of these seeds are the\nfrass of the boring beetle that hollowed them out.\n\nSlimy brown oval shaped frass is typical of many grasshoppers.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nRobert Reeder, CABI\n\nAnnexes\n\n100\n\n\fWEBBING\n\nInsect larvae and mites have the ability to spin thin silken\nwebs; they often use these threads to create a habitat for\nthemselves.\n\nThese larvae live together and combine their webbing to make a communal\nhabitat.\n\nLive mites, castoff skins and frass are visible on the webbing when\nheld against a white background.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nMites have produced a sleeve of webbing over this badly infested\naubergine (eggplant, brinjal) leaf.\n\nSometimes the webbing is seen as fine silk threads, larvae pull leaves\ntogether with these threads for protection.\n\nPhil Taylor, CABI\n\nTim Haye, CABI\n\nAnnexes\n\n101\n\n\fContact us\nAfrica\nGhana\n\nKenya\n\nZambia\n\nCABI, CSIR Campus\nNo. 6 Agostino Neto Road\nAirport Residential Area\nP. and make\nrecommendations for their management\n\nCompiled by Phil Taylor\nThe huge variety of plants that are grown for various reasons and the numerous biotic\nand abiotic factors that can reduce plant vigour make it very difficult to diagnose all\nplant health problems. That is a problem since a good diagnosis is the first step in\nidentifying solutions.\nThe PestSmart Diagnostic Field Guide provides the essentials of diagnosing plant\nhealth problems, covering all the main problems that crops encounter (nine groups\nof pests and two abiotic conditions) to group level. It includes summary sheets that\ncross-reference symptoms with causes, line drawings of the major insect pest groups,\nand photos of the symptoms of the major microbial pest groups and symptoms\nassociated with mineral deficiencies.\nThis Field Guide has a full colour glossary to allow for accurate symptom description.\nIt also has a section that explains how to tell the difference between similar symptoms\nwith different causes.\nCABI publishes high-quality books on all aspects of agriculture and applied\nlife sciences for practitioners, researchers and students throughout the world.\nwww.cabi.org/bookshop\n\n\f","message":"Command executed successfully.","display":[],"extras":null}}}}}}
