P. KISHORE VARMA,\nASSISTANT PROFESSOR,\nDEPARTMENT OF PLANT PATHOLOGY\nAGRICULTURAL COLLEGE,\nASWARAOPET 507 301\n\n1\n\n\fLECTURE 1\nDISEASESW OF RICE (ORYZA SATIVA)\nBlast\nPyricularia oryzae (Syn: P. grisea)\n(Sexual stage: Magnaporthe grisea)\nThe disease was first recorded in China in 1637. In Japan, it is believed to have occurred\nas early as in 1704. In Italy the disease called “brusone” was reported in 1828 and in\nUSA in 1876. The disease was first recorded from Tanjore district of Tamil Nadu in 1918.\nEconomic importance: The pathogen cause yield loss ranging from 30-61 per cent\ndepending upon the stage of infection. In severe cases, losses amounting to 70-80 per cent\nof grain yield are reported.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus attacks the crop at all stages from seedlings in nursery to heading in main\nfield. The typical symptoms appear on leaves, leaf sheath, rachis, nodes and even the\nglumes are also attacked.\n¾ Leaf blast: On the leaves, the lesions start as small water soaked bluish green specks,\nsoon enlarge and form characteristic spindle shaped spots with grey centre and dark\nbrown margin. The spots join together as the disease progresses and large areas of the\nleaves dry up and wither. Similar spots are also formed on the sheath. Severely\ninfected nursery and field show a burnt appearance.\n¾ Node blast: In infected nodes, irregular black areas that encircle the nodes can be\nnoticed. The affected nodes may break up and all the plant parts above the infected\nnodes may die (Node blast).\n¾ Neck blast: At the flower emergence, the fungus attacks the peduncle which is\nengirdled, and the lesion turns to brownish-black. This stage of infection is commonly\nreferred to as rotten neck/neck rot/neck blast/panicle blast. In early neck infection,\ngrain filling does not occur and the panicle remains erect like a dead heart caused by a\nstem borer. In the late infection, partial grain filling occurs. Small brown to black spots\nalso may be observed on glumes of the heavily infected panicles.\n\nLeaf blast\nNode blast\n3 celled conidia\nEtiology\nThe causal organism was first detected by Cavara in 1891 from Italy. Mycelium of the\nfungus, is hyaline to olivaceous, septate and highly branched. Conidia are produced in\nclusters on long septate, olivaceous slender conidiophores. Conidia are pyriform to\nobclavate or somewhat top shaped, attached at the broader base by a hilum. Conidia are\n2\n\n\fhyaline to pale olive green, usually 3 celled. The perfect state of the fungus is M. grisea.\nIt produces perithecia. The ascospores are hyaline, fusiform, 4 celled and slightly curved.\nThe pathogen produces few toxins namely, α-picolinic acid, Pyricularin and pyriculol.\nDisease cycle\nMycelium and conidia in the infected straw and seeds are important sources of primary\ninoculum. The seed borne inoculum fails to initiate the disease in the plains due to high\nsoil temperature in June. In both tropical and temperate regions, the fungus overwinters in\nstraw piles or grain. In tropics, one method of survival is through infection of collateral\nhosts such as Panicum repens, Digitaria marginata, Brachiaria mutica, Leersia\nhexandra, Dinebra retroflexa, Echinochloa crusgalli, Setaria intermedia and\nStenotaphrum secondatum. The most probable source of perennation and initiation of the\ndisease appear to be the grass hosts and early sown paddy crop. The disease cycle is short\nand most damage is caused by secondary infections. Air can carry the conidia for long\ndistances. The conidia from these sources are carried by air currents to cause secondary\nspread. Most conidia are released at night in the presence of dew or rain.\nFavourable Conditions\nApplication of excessive doses of nitrogenous fertilizers, intermittent drizzles, cloudy\nweather, high relative humidity (93-99 per cent), low night temperature (between 15-20\n0\nC or less than 26 0C), more number of rainy days, longer duration of dew, cloudy\nweather, slow wind movement and availability of collateral hosts.\nForecasting\nForecasting blast of rice can be made on the basis of minimum night temperature range of\n20-26 0C in association with a high relative humidity range of 90 per cent and above\nlasting for a period of a week or more during any of the three susceptible phases of crop\ngrowth, viz., seedling stage, post transplanting tillering stage and neck emergence stage.\nIn Japan, the first leaf blast model was developed and named as BLAST. Later based on\ndifferent field experiments various models were developed namely, PYRICULARIA,\nPYRIVIEW, BLASTAM, and P BLAST. A model to forecast the disease called “EpiBla” has been evolved in India.\nManagement\n¾ Use of seeds from a disease free crop\n¾ Grow resistant varieties like Simhapuri, Tikkana, Sriranga, Phalguna, Swarnadhan,\nSwarnamukhi, MTU 7414, MTU 9992, MTU 1005, Swathi, IR 64, IR 36, Sravani,\nJaya, Vijaya, Ratna, RP 4-14, IET 1444, IR20, TKM 6, MTU-3 & 5 and NLR 9672 &\n9674 in different tracts of Andhra Pradesh.\n¾ Remove and destroy the weed hosts in the field bunds and channels.\n¾ Split application of nitrogen and judicious application of nitrogenous fertilizers\n¾ Treat the seeds with Captan or Thiram or Carbendazim or Carboxin or Tricyclazole at\n2 g/kg.\n¾ Seed treatment with biocontrol agent Trichoderma viride@ 4g/kg or Pseudomonas\nfluorescens @ 10g/kg of seed. Avoid close spacing of seedlings in the main field.\n¾ Spray the nursery with Carbendazim 25 g or Edifenphos 25 ml for 8 cent nursery.\n¾ Spray the main field with Edifenphos@0.1% or Carbendazim@0.1% or Tricyclazole\n@0.06% or Thiophanate Methyl@0.1%.\n\n3\n\n\fBrown Spot or Sesame leaf spot or Helminthosporiose\nHelminthosporium oryzae (Syn: Drechslera oryzae)\n(Sexual stage: Cochliobolus miyabeanus)\nIn India, this disease is the principal cause of Bengal famine of 1942-43. The first report\nof the disease in India was made by Sundararaman from Madras in 1919, and now is\nreported from all of the rice growing states. Under highly favourable conditions, the\ndisease causes a reduction in yield ranging upto 90 per cent.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus attacks the crop from seedling in nursery to milk stage in main field.\nSymptoms appear as lesions (spots) on the coleoptile, leaf blade, leaf sheath, and glumes,\nbeing most prominent on the leaf blade and glumes. The disease appears first as minute\nbrown dots, later becoming cylindrical or oval to circular. The several spots coalesce and\nthe leaf dries up. The seedlings die and affected nurseries can often be recognized from a\ndistance by their brownish scorched appearance. Dark brown or black spots also appear\non glumes which contain large number of conidiophores and conidia of the fungus. It\ncauses failure of seed germination, seedling mortality and reduces the grain quality and\nweight. The disease is associated with a physiological disorder known as akiochi in\nJapan. Abnormal soil conditions (Deficiency of potassium) predispose the plants to heavy\ninfection.\n\nOval to circular spots\n\nOlive green to golden brown conidia\n\nEtiology\nH. oryzae produces greyish-brown to dark brown septate mycelium. Conidiophores may\narise singly or in small groups. They are straight, sometime geniculate, pale to brown in\ncolour. Conidia are usually curved with a bulge in the centre and tapering towards the\nends occasionally almost straight, pale olive green to golden brown colour and are 6-14\nseptate. The perfect stage of the fungus is C. miyabeanus. It produces perithecia with asci\ncontaining 6-15 septate, filamentous or long cylinderical, hyaline to pale olive green\nascospores. It produces C25 terpenoid phytotoxins called ophiobolin A, (or Cochliobolin\nA), ophiobolin B (or cochliobolin B) and ophiobolin I. Ophiobolin A is most toxic.\nThese result in the breakdown of the protein fragment of cell wall resulting in partial\ndisruption of integrity of cell.\nDisease cycle\nThe fungus overwinters mainly in the infected plant parts. It is not soil borne. The\ninfected seeds are the most common source of primary infection. Diseased seeds\n4\n\n\f(externally seed borne) may give rise to the seedling blight, the first phase of the disease.\nThe young seedlings show infection symptoms soon after germination. Pale yellowishbrown spots appear on the coleoptiles, spreading to cover the other tissues of the seedling.\nThe fungus reproduces on the spots and is disseminated by air currents. The fungus also\nsurvives on collateral hosts like Digitaria sanguinalis, Leersia hexandra, Echinochloa\ncolonum, Pennisetum typhoides, Setaria italica and Cynodon dactylon.\nThe symptoms of potassium deficiency are somewhat similar to that of brown spot\nmaking it often difficult to ascribe the symptoms to fungus attack or nutrient deficiency.\n\nFavourable Conditions\nTemperature of 25-300C with relative humidity above 80 per cent are highly favourable.\nExcess of nitrogen aggravates the disease incidence.\nManagement\n¾ Use disease free seeds.\n¾ Field sanitation-removal of collateral hosts and infected debris in the field.\n¾ Crop rotation,\n¾ Adjustment of planting time\n¾ Proper fertilization\n¾ Use of slow release nitrogenous fertilizers is advisable.\n¾ Good water management\n¾ Use of soil amendments\n¾ Grow disease tolerant varieties viz., Bala, BAM 10, IR-20, Jaya, Ratna, Tellahamsa\nand Kakatiya.\n¾ Treat the seeds with Thiram or Captan at 4 g/kg and with Mancozeb @0.3%\n5\n\n\f¾ Spray the crop in the main field twice with Mancozeb@0.2%, once after flowering and\nsecond spray at milky stage.\nSheath rot\nSarocladium oryzae\n(Syn: Acrocylindrium oryzae)\nEconomic importance\nSheath rot was first described in Taiwan in 1922. It is reported in all countries in South\nAsia. In A.P, sheath rot was found to be severe in Godavari delta, Nellore and Chittor. 10\nto 25% tillers may occasionally be infected.\nSymptoms\nSheath rot occurs usually at the booting stage of the crop. Initial symptoms are noticed\nonly on the upper most leaf sheath enclosing young panicles. The flag leaf sheath show\noblong or irregular greyish brown spots. They enlarge and develop grey centre and brown\nmargins covering major portions of the leaf sheath. The young panicles may remain\nwithin the sheath or emerge partially. The affected sheath and panicles rot and abundant\nwhitish powdery fungal growth is formed inside the leaf sheath. The grain discolours and\nshrivels.\n\nInfection on flag leaf sheath\nEtiology\nThe fungus produces whitish, sparsely branched and septate mycelium. Conidiophore is\nslightly thicker than the vegetative hyphae. Conidia are hyaline, smooth, single celled\nand cylindrical in shape.\nFavourable Conditions\nCloser planting, high doses of nitrogen, high humidity and temperature around 25-30OC.\nInjuries made by leaf folder, brown plant hopper and mites increase infection.\nMode of Spread and Survival\nMainly through air-borne conidia and also seed-borne.\nManagement\n¾ Apply recommended doses of fertilizers.\n¾ Adopt optimum spacing.\n¾ Spray twice with Carbendazim @0.1% or Benomyl@0.05% or Mancozeb@0.2% or\nChlorothalonil@0.2% at boot leaf stage and 15 days later.\n¾ Soil application of gypsum in 2 equal splits (500 kg/ha) reduce the sheath rot\nincidence.\n6\n\n\fStem rot\nSclerotium oryzae\n(Sexual stage: Leptosphaeria salvinii)\nEconomic importance\nStem rot was reported in Japan in 1910 and in India in 1913. Early reports indicated heavy\nlosses from stem rot. In India 18 to 56% loss was reported. IRRI studies show that the\nstem rot fungus is a wound parasite. Due to the damage caused by injuries the disease\nincidence initiates.\nSymptoms\nSmall black lesions are formed on the outer leaf sheath near the water line and they\nenlarge and reach the inner leaf sheath also. The affected tissues rot and abundant\nsclerotia are seen in the rotting tissues. The culm collapses and plants lodge. If the\ndiseased tiller is opened, profuse mycelial growth and large number of sclerotia can be\nseen. The sclerotia may be seen in the stubbles after harvest.\n\nBlack lesions on leaf sheath\n\nSclerotia in rotting tissue\n\nEtiology\nWhite to greyish hyphae produces smooth, spherical black and shiny sclerotia, visible to\nnaked eyes as black masses.\nDisease cycle\nIn the field, sclerotia are mostly distributed in the upper 5 to 10 cm of the soil. These\nsclerotia float on the water during ploughing, puddling, weeding and other operations.\nPropagules in contact with the leaf sheath produce appressoria and may start infection.\nInfection takes place readily in the presence of a wound. After harvest the fungus\ncontinues to grow on stubbles producing large quantities of sclerotia. Irrigation water\ncarries the sclerotia to other fields.\nFavourable Conditions\nInfestation of leaf hoppers and stem borer and high doses of nitrogenous fertilizers\naggravates the disease.\nManagement\n¾ Use recommended doses of fertilizer.\n¾ Deep ploughing in summer and burning of stubbles and infected straw\n¾ Use of resistant or non-lodging varieties (Basumati 3, Basumati 370, Mushkan 7,\nMushkan 41 and Bara 62 were found resistant to stem rot in Punjab)\n¾ Draining off the irrigation water and allow the soil to dry\n¾ Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected fields to healthy fields.\n7\n\n\fNarrow brown leaf spot\nCercospora oryzae\n(Sexual stage: Sphaerulina oryzae)\nEconomic importance\nIt is a minor disease, present in almost all rice growing countries of the world. In the year\n1953-54, 40% loss from the disease was reported in Surinam. In Asia, narrow brown leaf\nspot is important on very susceptible varieties.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus produces short, linear brown spots mostly on leaves and also on sheaths,\npedicels and glumes. The spots appear in large numbers during later stages of crop\ngrowth. It may also occur as long and about 1mm narrow, short and dark on resistant\nvarieties, but wide and light brown on susceptible ones.\n\nNarrow elongated spots\nCylindrical 3-10 septate conidia\nEtiology\nConidiophores are produced in small groups and are brown/dark at the base and pale at\nthe apex with three or more septa. Conidia are hyaline or sub hyaline, cylindrical and 3-10\nseptate.\nDisease cycle\nPrimary source of inoculum is by means of infected plant debris. Secondary spread of the\ndisease is by means of air borne conidia produced on leaves.\nManagement\n¾ Destruction of infected plant debris.\n¾ Spray Mancozeb@0.2% or Carbendazim@0.1% twice at 15 days interval starting with\ndisease appearance.\nSheath blight\nRhizoctonia solani\n(Sexual stage : Thanetophorus cucumeris)\nSymptoms\nThe fungus affects the crop from tillering to heading stage. Initial symptoms are noticed\non leaf sheaths near water level. On the leaf sheath oval or elliptical or irregular greenish\ngrey spots are formed. As the spots enlarge, the centre becomes greyish white with an\nirregular blackish brown or purple brown border. Lesions on the upper parts of plants\nextend rapidly coalesing with each other to cover entire tillers from the water line to the\nflag leaf. The presence of several large lesions on a leaf sheath usually causes death of the\nwhole leaf, and in severe cases all the leaves of a plant may be blighted in this way. The\ninfection extends to the inner sheaths resulting in death of the entire plant. Older plants\nare highly susceptible. Five to six week old leaf sheaths are highly susceptible. Plants\n8\n\n\fheavily infected in the early heading and grain filling growth stages produce poorly filled\ngrain, especially in the lower part of the panicle.\nPathogen\nThe fungus produces usually long cells of septate mycelium which are hyaline when\nyoung, yellowish brown when old. It produces large number of globose sclerotia, which\nare initially white, later turn to brown or purplish brown.\nDisease cycle\nThe pathogen can survive as sclerotia or mycelium in dry soil for about 20 months but for\n5-8 months in moist soil. It infects more than 188 crop species in 32 families. Sclerotia\nspread through irrigation water.\nFavourable Conditions\nHigh relative humidity (96-97 per cent), high temperature (30-32 0C), closer planting and\nheavy doses of nitrogenous fertilizers.\n\nManagement\n¾ Avoid excess doses of fertilizers.\n¾ Adopt optimum spacing.\n¾ Eliminate weed hosts.\n¾ Apply organic amendments.\n¾ Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected fields to healthy fields.\n¾ Deep ploughing in summer and burning of stubbles.\n¾ Grow disease tolerant varieties like Shiva (WGL 3943)\n¾ Spray Propiconazole@0.1% or Hexaconazole@0.2% or Validamycin@0.2%\n\n9\n\n\f¾ Seed treatment with Pseudomonas fluorescens @ of 10g/kg of seed followed by\nseedling dip @ of 2.5 kg of product/ha dissolved in 100 litres and dipping for 30\nminutes.\n¾ Soil application of P.fluorescens @ of 2.5 kg/ha after 30 days of transplanting (This\nproduct should be mixed with 50 kg of FYM/Sand and then applied.\n¾ Foliar spray at 0.2% concentration commencing from 45 days after transplanting at 10\ndays interval for 3 times depending upon the intensity of disease.\nFalse smut\nUstilaginoidea virens\n(P.S: Claviceps oryzae - sativa)\nEconomic importance\nMost countries in Asia, Latin America and Africa have reported the presence of the\ndisease. There was severe epidemic in Burma in 1935. Its presence was believed to\nindicate a good crop year. This belief is still common in South-east Asia.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus transforms individual grains into yellow or greenish spore balls of velvety\nappearance which are small at first and 1 cm or longer at later stages. At early stages the\nspore balls are covered by a membrane which bursts with further growth. Due to the\ndevelopment of the fructification of the pathogen, the ovaries are transformed into large\nvelvety green masses. Usually only a few spikelets in a panicle are affected.\n\nYellow or greenish spore balls\nEtiology\nChlamydospores are formed on the spore balls, they are spherical to elliptical, waxy and\nolivaceous.\nDisease cycle\nIn temperate regions, the fungus survives the winter through sclerotia as well as through\nchlamydospores. Ascospores produced on the over wintered sclerotia apparently start\nprimary infection. Chlamydospores are important in secondary infection which is a major\npart of the disease cycle. Infection usually occurs at the booting stage of rice plants.\nChlamydospores are borne, but do not free them from spore ball easily because of the\npresence of sticky material.\nFavourable conditions\nRainfall and cloudy weather during the flowering and maturity periods are favourable.\nManagement\n¾ Spray copper oxychloride@0.3% or carbendazim@0.1% at panicle emergence stage\n10\n\n\fBacterial leaf blight\nXanthomonas oryzae pv. oryzae\nEconomic importance\nThe disease was first observed in Japan (1884). In Indonesia, Kresek disease was\nreported to kill young seedlings completely in 1950. In India, BLB was first reported in\n1959. A severe outbreak of the disease occurred in Bihar and Uttar Pradesh in 1963. In the\ntropics the disease is usually referred as bacterial blight as it often kills entire young\nseedlings\nYield losses in severely diseased fields range from 20-30% and occasionally 50%. In\nIndia, millions of hectares are infected every year. Yield losses have been as high as 60%\nin some states and Godavari district of Andhra Pradesh which are endemic to this disease.\nTaichung Native 1 is highly susceptible.\nSymptoms\nThe bacterium induces either wilting of plants or leaf blight. Wilt syndrome known as\nKresek is seen in seedlings within 3-4 weeks after transplanting of the crop. Kresek\nresults either in the death of whole plant or wilting of only a few leaves. The bacterium\nenters through the hydathodes and cut wounds in the leaf tips, becomes systemic and\ncause death of entire seedling.\nThe disease is usually noticed at the time of heading but in severe cases occur earlier also.\nIn grown up plants water soaked, translucent lesions appear usually near the leaf margin.\nThe lesions enlarge both in length and width with a wavy margin and turn straw yellow\nwithin a few days, covering the entire leaf. As the disease progresses, the lesions cover the\nentire leaf blade which may turn white or straw coloured. Lesions may also be seen on\nleaf sheaths in susceptible varieties. Milky or opaque dew drops containing bacterial\nmasses are formed on young lesions in the early morning. They dry up on the surface\nleaving a white encrustation. The affected grains have discoloured spots surrounded by\nwater soaked areas. If the cut end of leaf is dipped in water, bacterial ooze makes the\nwater turbid.\n\nEtiology\nThe bacterium is strict aerobe, gram negative, non spore forming, rod shaped with\nmonotrichous polar flagellum of at one end. The bacterial cells are capsulated and are\njoined to form an aggregate mass. Colonies are circular, convex with entire margins,\nwhitish yellow to straw yellow and opaque. The bacterium has many strains that differ in\nability to infect rice plants. Strains in tropical countries are usually more virulent than\nthose in temperate areas like Japan.\n\n11\n\n\fDisease cycle\nThe bacterium enters the plant through water pores (hydathodes) along the edges of the\nleaf and through injuries in roots or leaves. It does not enter through stomata. BLB is\nprimarily a vascular or systemic disease. Bacterial cells move along the vascular tissues\ncausing wilting. Rain storms and typhoons help in the spread of the disease. Irrigation\nwater also carries the organism from field to field. The primary source of infection is\nthrough bacterium overwintering in seed (husk and endosperm). Bacteria may survive in\nsoil, plant stubbles and debris. The pathogen also survives on collateral hosts like Leersia\nhexandra, Leersia oryzoides, Zizania latifolia, Cyprus rotundus, Cyprus deformis,\nPhalaris arundinacea, Cyanodon dactylon, etc. The bacterial ooze serves as secondary\ninoculum and cause secondary infection.\nFavourable Conditions\nClipping of tip of the seedling at the time of transplanting, heavy rain, heavy dew,\nflooding, deep irrigation water, severe wind, temperature of 25-300C and application of\nexcessive nitrogen, especially late top dressing.\nManagement\n¾ Grow resistant cultivars like MTU 9992, Swarna, Ajaya, IR 20, IR 42, IR 50, IR 54,\nTKM 6, Mashuri, IET 4141, IET 1444, IET 2508, Chinsura Boro, etc.\n¾ Resistant donors: Tetep, Tadukan, Zenith, etc.\n¾ Affected stubbles are to be destroyed by burning or through ploughing\n¾ Judicious use of nitrogenous fertilizers\n¾ Avoid clipping of tip of seedling at the time of transplanting.\n¾ Avoid flooded conditions or drying of the field (not at the time of flowering)\n¾ Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected to healthy field\n\n12\n\n\f¾ Remove and destroy weed hosts.\n¾ Soaking seeds for 8 hrs in Agrimycin (0.025%) followed by hot water treatment for 10\nminutes at 52-54 0C eradicates the bacterium in the seed\n¾ Spray Streptocycline (250 ppm) along with copper oxychloride (0.3%)\nBacterial leaf streak\nXanthomonas campestris p.v. oryzicola\nEconomic importance\nBacterial leaf streak was first found in Phillippines in 1918. The disease is common in\ntropical Asia, but is not present in Japan or other parts of the world. In India, it is reported\nby Srivastava from U.P, MP, AP, Maharashtra, Karnataka, Orissa, Haryana and West\nBengal. IR 8, Jaya and Padma are highly susceptible to BLS.\nSymptoms\nFine translucent streaks appear between the veins of the leaf are the first symptoms. The\nlesions enlarge lengthwise and advance over larger veins laterally and turn brown. On\nvery susceptible varieties a yellow halo appears around the lesions. On the surface of the\nlesions, bacteria ooze out and form small yellow band-like exudates under humid\nconditions. In severe cases the leaves may dry up.\n\n13\n\n\fEtiology\nThe organism is short rod, about 1.2µ x 0.3 to 0.5µ, and gram negative. The strains of the\nbacteria differ in pathogenicity, the virulent strains causing longer streaks.\nDisease cycle\nThe pathogen can survive in infected seed but not in crop debris. The bacteria enter the\nleaves through stomata and wounds. It mainly infects the parenchymatic cells but does not\nenter the vascular systems. BLS is not a systemic disease. When the leaves are wet,\nexudate from infected leaf spread to other portions of the leaf and to other plants. Rain\nstorms and typhoons favour the spread of the disease.\n\nFavourable conditions\nHigh relative humidity (83-93%) or dew during morning hours for 2 to 3 hours\n\n14\n\n\fManagement\n¾ Grow resistant varieties\n¾ IR 20, Krishna and Jagannath are tolerant to BLS\n¾ Affected stubbles are to be destroyed by burning or through ploughing\n¾ Judicious use of nitrogenous fertilizers\n¾ Avoid clipping of tip of seedling at the time of transplanting.\n¾ Avoid flooded conditions or drying of the field (not at the time of flowering)\n¾ Avoid flow of irrigation water from infected to healthy field\n¾ Soak the seed in Streptocycline (250 ppm) followed by hot water treatment at 52 0C for\n30 minutes eradicates seedling infection.\n¾ Spray Streptocycline (250 ppm) along with copper oxychloride (0.3%)\n\nTungro disease\nEconomic importance\nPenyakitmerah which has been known in Malaysia since 1938 was identified as Tungro\nin 1965. The mentak disease of Indonesia is also identified as Tungro. Tungro is\ncommonly found in Bangladesh and India. In India, it is seen in states of West Bengal,\nKerala and other parts of India. Tungro is one of the most widely distributed and most\ndestructive diseases in tropical Asia. The loss was estimated during 1940 as 30% or 1.4\nmillion hectares annually. In Thailand a severe epidemic occurred in 1966 affecting more\nthan 3 lakh hectares. An outbreak of Tungro in 1971 affected hudred and thousands of\nhectares in Phillippines.\nSymptoms\nInfection occurs both in the nursery and in the main field. Plants are markedly stunted.\nStunting is more severe on susceptible varieties and slight on more resistant varieties.\nLeaves show yellow to orange discoloration and interveinal chlorosis. Yellow\ndiscoloration is commonly seen in “Japonica” varieties, while “Indica” varieties show\norange discoloration. Yellowing starts from the tip of the leaf and may extend to the lower\npart of the leaf blade. Young leaves are often mottled with pale green to whitish\ninterveinal stripes and the old leaves may have rusty streaks of various sizes. The plants\nmay be killed if infected early. Tillering is reduced with poor root system. The infected\nplants have few spikelets and panicles are small with discoloured grains.\nTungro infected plants can be chemically identified by lodine Test. Ten cm long leaf tip\nis cut in the early morning before 6 A.M. and dipped in a solution containing 2g Iodine\nand 6 g Potassium Iodide in 100 ml of water for 30 minutes. Tungro infected leaves show\ndark blue streaks.\nEtiology\nIt is a composite disease caused by two morphologically unrelated viruses: rice tungro\nbacilliform virus (RTBV) and rice tungro spherical virus (RTSV). RTBV has a\nbacilliform capsid or bullet-shaped particles (130 x 30 nm) made up of a single piece of\ncoat protein of MW 36 K and a single molecule of circular ds DNA of 8.3 KbP. RTSV\nhas a isometric capsid, 30 nm in diameter comprising two to three polypeptide pieces and\na single piece of polyadenylated ss RNA of about 10 KbP.\n\n15\n\n\fYellow or orange\ndiscolouration\n\nRTBV\n\nRTSV\n\nTwo types of virus particles are associated with the disease. Bacilliform particles cause\nmajority of the symptoms of the disease. Spherical particles help in the transmission of\nbacilliform virus by the green leaf-hoppers. If the bacilliform virus particles are alone\npresent in the rice plant they will not be transmitted by the leafhopper vector.\nDisease cycle\nThe virus causes severe damage only in area where the host plants and the insect vector\nmultiply the year round. In the areas where the rice is not grown continuously, collateral\nhosts, especially wild rice are probable sources of inoculum. Stubbles of infected plants\nfrom the previous season also serve as a source of inoculum. Grassy weeds such as\nEleusine indica, Echinochloa colonum, Echinochloa crusgalli may be infected\noccasionally. The leafhoppers viz, Nephotettix virescens, N. nigropictus, N. parvus,\nN.malayanus and Recilia dorsalis transmit the virus in a non-persistent manner.\nManagement\n¾ Summer deep ploughing and burning of stubbles.\n¾ Destroy weed hosts of the virus and vectors.\n¾ Grow disease tolerant cultivars like MTU 9992, 1002, 1003, 1005, Suraksha,\nVikramarya, Bharani, IR 36, IET 2508, RP 4-14, IET 1444, IR50 and Co45.\n¾ Control the vectors in the nursery by application of carbofuran granules @170 g/cent,\n10 days after sowing and @10kg/ac in main filed\n¾ Spray\nMonochrotophos@2.2\nml/lt\nor\nPhosphamidan\n@1ml/lt\nor\nEthophenphos@1.5ml/lt or Neem oil @3 per cent in the main field 15 and 30 days\nafter transplanting to control leaf hoppers.\n\n16\n\n\fLECTURE 6 & 7\nDISEASES OF SORGHUM (SORGHUM BICOLOR)\nAnthracnose or red leaf spot\nColletotrichum graminicola\nEconomic importance\nThis is wide spread and prevalent in all sorghum growing areas. In India anthracnose is\nsevere in Andhra Pradesh, Madhya Pradesh, Rajasthan, Tamil Nadu and Delhi.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus causes both leaf spot (anthracnose) and stalk rot (red rot) in sorghum. The\ndisease appears as small red coloured spots on both surfaces of the leaf. The centre of the\nspot is white in colour encircled by red, purple or brown margin. Numerous small black\ndots are seen on the white surface of the lesions which are the fruiting bodies (acervuli) of\nthe fungus. Many lesions coalesce and kill large leaf portions. In midrib region, elongate\nelliptical, red or purple regions with black acervuli are formed. Stalk and inflorescence\ninfection can be characterized externally by the development of circular cankers. Infected\nstem when split open shows discoloration, which may be continuous over a large area or\nmore generally discontinuous giving the stem a marbled appearance. The stem lesion also\nshows acervuli.\n\nAnthracnose\n\nStalk rot\n\nBlack acervuli\n\nFalcate conidia\n\nEtiology\nThe mycelium of the fungus is localized in the spot. Acervuli with long dark setae arise\nthrough epidermis. The conidiophores are short, single celled and colourless. Conidia are\nshort, hyaline, single celled, vacuolate and falcate in shape.\nDisease cycle\nFungus has wide host range and survives on Johnson grass, Sudan grass, maize, barley\nand wheat. Also survives in seed and infected plant debris. Primary infection is from the\nconidia produced on the infected plant debris and infected seed. Disease spread within the\nseason is through air borne conidia, which are produced on first infected plants.\nFavourable Conditions\nContinuous rain, temperature of 28-300C and high humidity aggravates the disease.\nManagement\n¾ Destruction of infected plant debris and collateral hosts\n¾ Crop rotation with non-host crops\n¾ Grow resistant varieties like SPV 162, CSV 17, Texas Milo and Tift sudan etc.\n17\n\n\f¾ Treat the seeds with Captan or Thiram @3 g/kg.\n¾ Spray the crop with Mancozeb @0.25% or carbendazim@0.1%\nRust\nPuccinia purpurea\nEconomic importance\nOccurs in warmer regions. In India it is recorded in all states. Damage caused by rust\ndepends on the time of infection and varieties affected. If infection occurs early the\npremature drying of leaves results in reduction of yields. In India rust is prevalent in all\nseasons. Both irrigated and rainfed crops are damaged.\nSymptoms\nThe fungus affects the crop at all stages of growth. The intensity of rust infection is\ngenerally severe after flag leaf stage of the crop growth. The first symptoms are small\nreddish brown flecks on the lower surface of the leaf (purple, tan or red depending upon\nthe cultivar). Pustules (uredosori) appear on both surfaces of leaf as purplish spots which\nrupture to release reddish powdery masses of uredospores. The pustules are elliptical and\nlie between and parallel with the leaf veins. Pustules are surrounded by a reddish or\nyellow halo. In highly susceptible cultivars, the pustules occur so densely that almost the\nentire leaf is destroyed. Teliopores develop later sometimes in the old uredosori or in\nteliosori, which are darker and longer than the uredosori. The pustules may also occur on\nthe leaf sheaths and on the stalks of inflorescence.\n\nRust on leaf\nPustules on stalk Telio & Uredospore\nEtiology\nThe uredospores are pedicellate, elliptical or oval, thin walled, echinulate and brown in\ncolour with 4 to 5 germ pores. Club shaped paraphyses are also found in uredosorus. The\nteliospores are reddish or brown in colour and two celled, rounded at the apex with one\ngerm pore in each cell. The teliospores germinate and produce promycelium and\nbasidiospores. Basidiospores infect Oxalis corniculata (alternate host) where pycnial and\naecial stages arise after infection.\nDisease cycle\nThe fungus is long cycled rust with Oxalis corniculata as the alternate host with aecial\nand pycnial stages. Presence of alternate host helps in perpetuation of the fungus. The\nuredospores survive for a short time in soil and infected debris. Air borne uredospores\nhelp in the secondary spread of the disease.\n\n18\n\n\fFavourable Conditions\nLow temperature of 10 to 120C favours teliospore germination and a spell of rainy\nweather favours the onset of the disease.\nManagement\n¾ Grow resistant varieties like CSH 5, SPV 13, 81, 126, PSH 1, CSV 17, etc.\n¾ Remove and destroy the alternate host Oxalis corniculata.\n¾ Spray the crop with Mancozeb @0.25%\n¾ Dusting of sulphur@25 kg/ha\nErgot or Sugary disease\nClaviceps sorghi or Sphacelia sorghi\nEconomic importance\nMc Rae first described this disease from Tamil Nadu. In South India, disease is prevalent\nduring October to January when cold weather prevails at crop maturity. CK 60 A, male\nsterile line is highly susceptible\nSymptoms\nThe disease is confined to individual spikelets. The first symptom is the secretion of\nhoney dew (creamy sticky liquid) from infected florets. The honey dew secretion attracts\nlarge number of insects and ants which help in spreading the disease. Often the honey dew\nis colonized by Cerebella sorghivulgaris which gives the head a blackened appearance.\nUnder favourable conditions, grain is replaced by long (1-2cm), straight or curved, cream\nto light brown, hard sclerotia. At the base of the affected plants white spots can be seen on\nthe soil surface, denoting the drops of honey dew which had fallen on the soil.\n\nHoney dew\nErgots\nEtiology\nThe fungus produces septate mycelium. The honey dew is a concentrated suspension of\nconidia, which are single celled, hyaline, elliptic or oblong in shape and slightly\nconstricted in the middle. The sclerotial bodies produced by the fungus are 10-12mm long\nand 2mm thick, hard and tough\nDisease cycle\nPrimary source of infection is through the germination of sclerotia which produce\nascospores, which infect the ovaries. The secondary spread takes place through air and\ninsect borne conidia, which settle in the spikelets. Rain splashes also help in spreading the\ndisease.\nFavourable Conditions\nA period of high rainfall and high humidity during flowering season. Cool night\ntemperature (20-25 0C) and cloudy weather during anthesis encourages disease spread\n19\n\n\frapidly causing severe losses in hybrid seed production. Male sterile lines are highly\nsusceptible.\nManagement\n¾ Adjust the date of sowing so that the crop does not flower during the periods of high\nrainfall and high humidity.\n¾ Grow resistant varieties like SPV 191, CSH 5, SPH 1 and CS3541.\n¾ Deep summer ploughing\n¾ Soaking seeds with 2% saline solution will aid to remove ergot infested seeds, as ergot\ninfested seeds will float in the salt solution.\n¾ Seed treatment with fungicides such as Captan or Thiram@4g/kg seed\n¾ Spray Ziram (or) Zineb (or) Captan (or) Mancozeb @0.2% at emergence of earhead\n(5-10 per cent flowering stage) followed by a spray at 50 per cent flowering and repeat\nthe spray after a week, if necessary.\n¾ Control of ergot with fungicides such as Propiconazole or Tebuconazole has proved to\nbe cost effective in seed production plots.\nHead mould/Grain mould/Head blight\nMore than thirty two genera of fungi were found to occur on the grains of sorghum. Some\nof them are pathogens, while many others are only saprophytes.\nSymptoms\nIf rains occur during the flowering and grain filling stages, severe grain moulding can\noccur. Infected grains are covered with pink or black mold and such grains disintegrate\nduring threshing process. Fusarium semitectum and F.moniliforme develop a fluffy white\nor pinkish colouration. C. lunata colours the grain black.\n\nFungi from many genera have been isolated from the infected sorghum grains and the\nmost frequently occurring genera are Fusarium, Curvularia, Alternaria, Aspergillus,\nCheatomium, Rhizopus, Helminthosporium and Phoma. Moldy grains contain toxic\nmycotoxins and are unfit for human consumption and cattle feed.\nDisease cycle\nThe fungi mainly spread through air-borne conidia. The fungi survive as parasites as well\nas saprophytes in the infected plant debris.\nFavourable Conditions\nWet weather following the flowering favours grain mould development and the longer the\nwet period the greater the mould development. Compact ear heads are highly susceptible.\nManagement\n¾ Adjust the sowing time.\n20\n\n\f¾ Grow resistant varieties like GMRP 4, GMRP 9, GMRP 13 and tolerant varieties like\nCSV 15.\n¾ Seed disinfestation with Thiram@0.3% will prevent seedling infection.\n¾ Spray Mancozeb (0.25%) or captan (0.2%) or captan 2g + Aureofungin 200ppm per\nliter, in case of intermittent rainfall during earhead emergence, a week later and during\nmilky stage.\nLeaf blight or leaf stripe\nExserohilum turcicum or Trichometasphaeria turcica\n(Syn : Helminthosporium turcicum or Drechslera turcicum)\nEconomic importance\nIn India, the disease is more prevalent in MP, AP and Karnataka causing heavy losses. If\nleaf blight is established on susceptible cultivars before earhead emergence, grain yield\nlosses may be upto 50%.\nSymptoms\nThe leaf blight pathogen also causes seed rot and seedling blight of sorghum. The disease\nappears in the form of small narrow elongated spindle shaped spots in the initial stage.\nBut in due course, they extend along the length of the leaf becoming bigger.\n\nElliptical lesion\nConidia\nOn older plants, the typical symptoms are long elliptical necrotic lesions, straw coloured\nin the centre with dark margins. The straw coloured centre becomes darker with the\nsporulation of the fungus. The lesions can be several centimeters long and wide. Many\nlesions may develop and coalesce on the leaves, destroying large areas of leaf tissue,\ngiving the crop a distinctly burnt appearance leading to premature drying of leaves.\nPathogen\nThe mycelium is brown and intercellular and is localized in the infected lesion.\nConidiophores emerge through stomata and are long, olivaceous, septate and geniculate.\nConidia are olivaceous brown, 3-8 septate and thick walled.\nDisease cycle\nThe fungus is found to persist in seed, soil and infected plant debris. Seed borne conidia\nare responsible for seedling infection. The secondary spread of the disease is through\nwind-borne conidia and seed.\nFavourable Conditions\nCool moist weather, high humidity (90 per cent) and high rainfall.\n\n21\n\n\fManagement\n¾ Use disease free seeds.\n¾ Rotation with non susceptible crops\n¾ Collect and destroy infected plant debris\n¾ Treat the seeds with Captan or Thiram at 4 g/kg.\n¾ Spray the crop with Mancozeb@0.25% at the age of 40 days and the spraying have to\nbe repeated twice at 15 days interval\n¾ Tift-Sudan is resistant to this disease\nGrain smut/Kernel smut / Covered smut / Short smut\nSphacelotheca sorghi or Sporisorium sorghi\nEconomic importance\nIt is considered as the most destructive disease among all the smuts of sorghum. The\nextent of damage is even upto 25% of the grain yield. It is widely prevalent in\nMaharashtra, UP, AP, Tamil Nadu, Gujarat and Karnataka when the seed is not\ndisinfected with fungicides. However, recently the losses due to this disease are reduced\nsince the seeds are being treated by fungicides before sowing. Most varieties of the\ncultivated species of Sorghum vulgare are susceptible, along with S. halapense and S.\nsudanens.\nSymptoms\nThe disease becomes apparent only at the time of grain formation in the ear. The\nindividual grains are replaced by smut sori which can be localized at a particular part of\nthe head or occur over the entire inflorescence. The sori are dirty white to gray in colour,\noval or cylindrical and are covered with a tough white cream to light brown skin\n(peridium) which often persists unbroken upto threshing. The glumes are unaltered and\nmay be found adhering to the sides of the sorus. Sometimes the stamens may develop\nnormally protruding out of the sorus. The size, colour and degree of breakage of the sori\nvary considerably with race of the fungus and the sorghum cultivar. Ratoon crops exhibit\nhigher incidence of disease.\n\nSmut sori\nSmut spores\nEtiology\nThe fungus is systemic. The mycelium occupies the growing point of the seedling and\ncontinues to grow along the plant without producing any external symptoms until the\nearhead is put forth. The mycelium aggregates in the immature ovary and the\nchlamydospores are formed by the rounding off of the mycelium. The sorus wall is\nformed mean while mainly by the outer layer of the mycelium, and partly by the host\ntissue. The fungus is present in the form of sorus, which has a tough wall and a long, hard,\ncentral tissue called columellum. The columella is bulbous at the base and narrowed\ntowards the tip. A dense mass of black to dark brown, smooth, thick walled spores, which\nare mostly single and measure 5-9µ in diameter, fill the space between the columellum\nand sorus wall. They germinate immediately if moisture is available, usually by producing\na four celled promycelium which buds off sporidia.\n22\n\n\fDisease cycle\nThe disease is externally seed borne and systemic. The spores germinate with the seed\nand infect the seed by penetrating through the radicle or mesocotyl to establish systemic\ninfection that develops along the meristematic tissues. At the time of flowering, the fungal\nhyphae get converted into spores, replacing the ovary with the sori. If the diseased ears\nare harvested with the healthy ones and threshed together, the healthy grains become\ncontaminated with the smut spores released from the bursting of the sori. The spores\nremain dormant on the seed until next season.\nManagement\n¾ Use disease free seeds.\n¾ Grow resistant varieties like T 29/1, PJ 7K, PJ 23K, Nandyal and Bilichigan.\n¾ Treat the seed with fine sulphur powder @0.5% or Captan or Thiram @0.3%.\n¾ Follow crop rotation.\n¾ Collect the smutted ear heads in cloth bags and dip in boiling water.\nCharcoal rot or hollow stem or stalk rot blight\nMacrophomina phaseolina\nEconomic importance\nIt is a major problem in the warmer regions. It causes seedling blight and stalk rot of\nplants. It is severe in Kurnool and Khammam districts of A.P.\nSymptoms\nThis disease is characterized by sudden wilting and death of the diseased plant resulting in\nlodging. If the infected stalk is split open, the pith is found to be disintegrated with\nlongitudinal shredding of the tissue into fibers. Small black sclerotial bodies are seen in\nthe infected tissues. The stalk is weak, hollow inside and break easily. The stem, breaks\nnear the ground level. Premature ripening takes place and the heads are poorly developed.\nDisease cycle\nPathogen survives in soil, plant debris and many cultivated and wild plants. Secondary\nspread is through sclerotial bodies.\n\nBlackening of roots Shredding of tissue\nFavourable conditions\nDisease is favoured by soil temperature of 350C and moisture stress conditions preceding\ncrop maturity and application of more nitrogenous fertilizers.\n\n23\n\n\fManagement\n¾ Thin plant population should be maintained in problematic areas (60,000 plants/ha)\n¾ The infected plants along with trash should be collected and burnt immediately\n¾ Avoid moisture stress at flowering\n¾ Grow resistant varieties like E-36-1, CSV 5, CSH 7-R, SPV 126 and SPV 193.\nDowny Mildew or Leaf shredding\nPeronosclerospora sorghi\nDisease is severe in delta regions of Andhra Pradesh where cool humid conditions prevail.\nSymptoms\nThe first few leaves that show symptoms are only partially infected with green or yellow\ncolouration of the infected portion. Abundant downy whitish growth is produced on the\nlower surface of the leaves. The downy growth spreads over a major portion of the upper\nsurface. As the plant grows, white streaks appear on both the surfaces of the leaves. The\ntissues then tear along the streaks causing shredding of the leaves which is the most\ncharacteristic symptom and hence the name leaf shredding. The tissue then turns brown\nin colour. Numerous oospores are found in the shredded leaves. The affected parts are\nstunted and sterile. In the standing crop healthy plants are infected due to secondary\ninfection by sporangia.\n\nWhite streaks on leaves Downy white growth Leaf shredding\nPathogen biology\nP. sorghi is systemic in young host plant in the form of intercellular, non-septate\nmycelium. It is an obligate parasite. Sporangiophores emerge through the stomata in\nsingle or in clusters which are stout and dichotomously branched with pointed sterigmata.\nOn each sterigmata a single hyaline, globose thin walled and non-papillate sporangium is\nformed which germinates directly by a germ tube without any zoospores. Oospores are\ntypically produced abundantly in parallel bands between fibro vascular strands of the\nshredded leaf tissue which are three walled, more or less round, thick walled and golden\nyellow in colour.\nDisease cycle\nThe primary infection is by means of oospores present in the soil which germinate and\ninitiate the systemic infection. The oospores persist in the soil for several years. Presence\nof mycelium of the fungus in the seeds of systemically infected plants is also a source of\ninfection. Secondary spread is by means of air-borne sporangia.\nFavourable Conditions\nMaximum sporulation takes place at 100 per cent relative humidity. Optimum temperature\nfor sporulation is 21-230C. Light drizzling accompanied by cool weather is highly\nfavourable.\n24\n\n\fManagement\n¾ Destroyal of affected plants by burning before oospore formation, reduce the inoculum\npotential\n¾ Crop rotation with other crops like pulses and oilseeds.\n¾ Grow tolerant varieties like CSH 2, CSV 5, SPV 101, 165 and 190.\n¾ Seed treatment with Metalaxyl (Apron 35 SD) @4g/kg seed\n¾ Spray Metalaxyl (Ridomyl MZ) @0.2% or Mancozeb @0.25%\nPhanerogamic parasite\n(Striga or Witch Weed)\nStriga asiatica and Striga densiflora\nSymptoms\nThe root exudates of sorghum stimulate the seeds of the parasite to germinate. The\nparasite then slowly attach to the root of the host by haustoria and grow below the soil\nsurface and produce underground stems and roots for about 1-2 months. The parasite\ngrows faster and appears at the base of the plant. Severe infestation causes yellowing and\nwilting of the host leaves. The infected plants are stunted in growth and may die prior to\nseed setting, if the infection occurs early\nEtiology\nIt is a partial root parasite and occurs mainly in the rainfed sorghum. It is a small plant\nwith bright green leaves, grows upto a height of 15-30 cm. It always occurs in clusters of\n10-20/host plant. S. asiatica produces red to pink flowers while. S. densiflora produces\nwhite flowers. Each fruit contains minute seeds in abundance which survives in the soil\nfor several years.\n\nDisease cycle\nIn the absence of host, the seeds remain viable in soil for many years. The seeds can\ngerminate only in contact with roots of host. Seeds can germinate even one foot below\nsoil. Soil temperature of 350C and soil moisture of 30 per cent is favourable for Striga\ninfection.\nManagement\n¾ Hand weeding of the parasites before flowering\n¾ Crop rotation with cowpea, groundnut and sunflower\n¾ Mixing of ethrel with soil triggers germination of Striga in the absence of host. After\ngermination, Striga can be removed and destroyed.\n¾ Spray Fernoxone (sodium salt of 2, 4-D) or Agroxone (MCPA) at 450g/500 liters of\nwater or Praquat@1kg/ha.\n¾ 1% Tetrachloro dimethyl phenoxy acetate can be used for instant killing of Striga, if\nwater is in scarce.\n25\n\n\fLECTURE 7& 8\nDISEASES OF MAIZE (ZEA MAYS)\nTurcicum Leaf blight\nHelminthosporium turcicum\n(Syn : H. maydis)\nEconomic importance\nIn Andhra Pradesh, it is severe in the districts of Warangal, Karimnagar, Medak,\nNizamabad, Adilabad and Ranga Reddy.\nSymptoms\nDisease is characterized by long elliptical grayish green or tan lesions on the leaves\nmeasuring 2.5 to 25 cm in length and upto 4 cm in width. The fungus affects the maize\nplant at young stage. Small yellowish round to oval spots are seen on the leaves. The spots\ngradually increase in area into bigger elliptical spots and are straw to grayish brown\ncolour in the centre with dark brown margins. The spots coalesce to form bigger spots and\ngives blighted appearance. The surface is covered with olive green velvety masses of\nconidia and conidiophores. Under high humidity the whole leaf area becomes necrotic and\nplant appears as dead. Lesions may be extended to husk.\n\nPathogen\nConidiophores are in group, geniculate, mid dark brown, pale near the apex and smooth.\nConidia are distinctly curved, fusiform, and pale to golden brown with 5-11 pseudosepta.\nDisease cycle\nFungus survives in plant debris, seed and collateral hosts. The fungus is externally seed\nborne. It also infects Sudan grass, Johnson grass, sorghum, wheat, barley, oats, sugarcane\nand spores of the fungus are also found to associate with seeds of green gram, black gram,\ncowpea, and Teosinte. Secondary spread is through wind borne conidia.\nFavourable Conditions\nOptimum temperature for the germination of conidia is 18 to 27OC provided with free\nwater on the leaf. Infection takes place early in the wet season.\nManagement\n¾ Crop rotation\n¾ Grow resistant hybrids like DHM-1\n¾ Treat the seeds with Captan or Thiram at 4 g/kg\n¾ Spray Mancozeb@0.25%\n\n26\n\n\f[...truncated]\n","message":"Command executed successfully. Lessenger has combined experience in\nprivate practice, preventive medicine, and public service in California’s San\nJoaquin Valley, one of the most productive agricultural regions in the world.\nHis experience and selection of chapter authors is,in every sense,a contribu-\ntion to illuminating the art and science of agromedicine.As one examines the\ntable of contents,one is impressed by the range of topics and the importance\nof each concern. Covering both injury prevention and environmental haz-\nards, this innovative work is a practical guide for the family physician work-\ning in a rural area. The contents demonstrate the vitality of agromedicine\nand the vision and insight of the authors.\nThe chapters on farm chemicals provide thorough information about the\nmany types of chemicals commonly used in the farm environment, how they\nare applied, and the principles of diagnosis and management for family\nphysicians treating patients for toxic chemical exposure. These chapters\nunderscore the fact that the use of farm chemicals is one of the things respon-\nsible for the increase in worldwide agricultural production and that risks can\nbe managed through preventive measures.\nThe Agricultural Medicine represents a benchmark in the evolution of a\nconcept begun in South Carolina over two decades ago called agromedicine.\nSeveral faculty members at two state-supported universities in South Car-\nolina needed a shortened name for our closer partnership between the land\ngrant campus of Clemson University and the Medical University of South\nCarolina at Charleston. In 1983 the agromedicine program was only an idea:\nhow to provide an innovative public service program to benefit farmers and\nfarm families with the most useful information on health, safety, nutrition,\nand preventive medicine. The new term agromedicine connotes an update of\nthe traditional terms agricultural health and safety and agricultural medicine.\nThe need for the agromedicine partnerships is just as real now as it was in\nits inception.The target population of farmers,farm families,and consumers\nof food and fiber are underserved by direct and effective forms of preventive\nmedicine.These forms include health education,patient motivation,and food\nsafety. Dr. Lessenger’s book addresses these issues as well as preventable dis-\norders such as noise-induced hearing loss, ultraviolet light–induced skin can-\ncers, heat and humidity syndromes, allergic anaphylaxis, zoonoses, injuries,\nand pulmonary disorders.\nComplex agromedicine questions keep arising: How should we focus on\nthe most practical health measures for the average hard-working rural farm\nv\nvi Foreword\nfamily and consumer? How should we react to illnesses resulting from haz-\nards such as infectious rodents and ticks, noxious hog-farm odors, botanical\ntoxins and dermatitis,pesticide residues measured in food at parts per billion\nor trillion, self-medication with herbal preparations, and excessive stress pre-\ndisposing farmers to depression and suicide? How should we define the prob-\nlem, select countermeasures, and communicate to farmers and farm families\nat risk?\nThe average farm family today differs from that of the 1980s. Rural\npatients and extension clients in the past were less oriented to the media and\nuntouched by cyberspace. Today’s farm family can be deluged with health\ninformation and misinformation. One constant issue is that health insurance\nis still an unmet need for many farm families. Chapters 1 and 2 of this book\naddress the context of agricultural medicine and traditions that affect treat-\nment. Health issues of migrant farm workers are also covered.\nOur experience in South Carolina helped other southeastern states initiate\nsimilar interuniversity partnerships for their farmers and farm families. Nat-\nurally, priorities and methods of outreach vary with the types of agriculture.\nGrain farmers endure different hazards than orchard sprayers; the ergonom-\nics of dairy farmers differ from those of vineyard workers; heat stress and\ncold injury vary with climate. Client-based research will lead to a broadened\nrange of preventable health problems, whether they involve food bioterror-\nism, the Norwalk virus in oyster beds, immunodeficiency in poultry 249702\necitcarp\nfo\nedoC\nerutlucirga\nni\nhtlaeh\ndna\nytefaS\nOLI\nSafety and health in agriculture\nAgriculture is one of the most hazardous of all economic sectors\nand many agricultural workers suffer occupational accidents and\nill health each year. It is also the largest sector for female employ-\nment in many countries, especially in Africa and Asia. Agriculture\nemploys some one billion workers worldwide, or more than a third\nof the world’s labour force, and accounts for approximately 70 per\ncent of child labour worldwide.\nThis code of practice is intended to raise awareness of the hazards\nand risks associated with agriculture and promote their effective\nmanagement and control; to help prevent occupational accidents\nand diseases and improve the working environment in practice;\nto encourage governments, employers, workers and other stake-\nholders to cooperate to prevent accidents and diseases; and to\npromote more positive attitudes and behaviour towards occupa-\ntional safety and health in agriculture throughout the sector.\nSafety and health\nThe code was adopted by an international group of experts\nmeeting in Geneva from 25 to 29 October 2010. It was endorsed\nin agriculture\nand approved for publication by the Governing Body of the ILO at\nits 310th Session (March 2011).\nSafety and health\nin agriculture\nThis code is dedicated\nto the farmers and agricultural workers\nwho feed the world, in the expectation that it will improve\nsafety and health in agriculture.\nILO code of practice\nSafety and health\nin agriculture\nInternational Labour Office Geneva\nCopyright © International Labour Organization 2011\nFirst published 2011\nPublications of the International Labour Office enjoy copyright under Protocol 2 of the\nUniversal Copyright Convention. We recognize sexual harassment as unlawful discrimination,\nas is conduct that belittles or demeans any individual on the basis of\nrace, religion, national origin, sexual preference, age, disability, or\nother similar characteristics or circumstances.\nNo manager or supervisor shall threaten or imply that an em-\nployee’s refusal to submit to sexual advances will adversely affect that\nperson’s employment, compensation, advancement, assigned duties,\n325\nSafety and health in agriculture\nor any other term or condition of employment or career development.\nSexual joking, lewd pictures and any conduct that tends to make\nemployees of one gender “sex objects” are prohibited.\n4. Employees who have complaints of sexual harassment should (and\nare encouraged to) report such complaints to their supervisor. If\nthis person is the cause of the offending conduct, the employee\nmay report this matter directly to [specify various officials (e.g.\nDirector of Human Resources, designated contact manager, etc)].\nYour complaint will be promptly and thoroughly investigated.\nConfidentiality of reports and investigations of sexual harassment\nwill be maintained to the greatest extent possible.\n5. Any manager, supervisor or employee who, after appropriate\ninvestigation, is found to have engaged in sexual harassment of\nanother employee will be subject to disciplinary action, up to and\nincluding dismissal.\n6. If any party directly involved in a sexual harassment investigation\nis dissatisfied with the outcome or resolution, that individual has\nthe right to appeal the decision. The dissatisfied party should sub-\nmit his or her written comments to [specify official (e.g. Gender\nCommittee, contact manager)].\n7. The Company will not in any way retaliate against any indi-\nvidual who makes a report of sexual harassment nor permit any\nemployee to do so. Retaliation is a serious violation of this sexual\nharassment policy and should be reported immediately. Any per-\nson found to have retaliated against another individual for report-\ning sexual harassment will be subject to appropriate disciplinary\naction, up to and including dismissal.\nSource: This guidance was developed by the Ethical Trading Ini-\ntiative in consultation with its tripartite membership. It is extracted\n326\nAppendix VII\nfrom a training manual for supervisors and managers which aims to\nassist employers and unions in tackling sexual harassment in agri-\nculture. http://www.ethicaltrade.org/in-action/projects/eti-supervisor-\ntraining-project. These training materials can be accessed freely at\nwww.ethicaltrade.org/resources/key-eti-resources/supervisor-train-\ning-programme-materials#downloads.\n327\n\nISBN 978-92-2-124970-2\n9 789221 249702\necitcarp\nfo\nedoC\nerutlucirga\nni\nhtlaeh\ndna\nytefaS\nOLI\nSafety and health in agriculture\nAgriculture is one of the most hazardous of all economic sectors\nand many agricultural workers suffer occupational accidents and\nill health each year. It is also the largest sector for female employ-\nment in many countries, especially in Africa and Asia. Agriculture\nemploys some one billion workers worldwide, or more than a third\nof the world’s labour force, and accounts for approximately 70 per\ncent of child labour worldwide.\nThis code of practice is intended to raise awareness of the hazards\nand risks associated with agriculture and promote their effective\nmanagement and control; to help prevent occupational accidents\nand diseases and improve the working environment in practice;\nto encourage governments, employers, workers and other stake-\nholders to cooperate to prevent accidents and diseases; and to\npromote more positive attitudes and behaviour towards occupa-\ntional safety and health in agriculture throughout the sector.\nSafety and health\nThe code was adopted by an international group of experts\nmeeting in Geneva from 25 to 29 October 2010. It was endorsed\nin agriculture\nand approved for publication by the Governing Body of the ILO at\nits 310th Session (March 2011).\n\n\n\n\n=== Processing storage_reserve_flag = 1: -6\n\nprice_volatility_score = round(average(all price_raw_score))\nif price_volatility_score > 80: price_volatility_score = price_volatility_score + 5\n```\n\n### Pesticide price_volatility_score * 0.22\n  + pesticide_risk_score * 0.20\n  + crop_disease_score * 0.22\n  + worker_health_score * 0.18\n)\n\ncross_domain_penalty = 0\nif price_volatility_score > 80 and market_system_score < 70: +10\nif pesticide_risk_score > 82 and worker_health_score > 78: +12\nif crop_disease_score > 80 and price_volatility_score > 75: +8\nif market_system_score > 80 and price_volatility_score < 70: -5\nif pesticide_risk_score > 75 and crop_disease_score > 75 and worker_health_score > 75: +10\n\nfinal_synthesis_value = base_resilience_pressure + cross_domain_penalty\n```\n\n## Source Files to List\nAll 14 PDF files from /environment/input_artifacts/:\n1. 09_01_2017.pdf\n2. 217.pdf\n3. 4.2020.pdf\n4.
